topics.title: Performance Evaluation of Different Types of Cleaners and Separators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation of Different Types of Cleaners and Separators # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Unit Operations in Agricultural Processing (PCAGL607)**, a **Cleaner** is a primary post-harvest processing machine designed to remove foreign matter (FM) — including dust, chaff, straw, stones, metal fragments, immature grains, and weed seeds — from a harvested bulk commodity, using principles based on differences in **size, shape, density, aerodynamic property, magnetic susceptibility, colour, or surface texture**. A **Separator** is a unit operation device that divides a bulk granular mass into two or more distinct fractions based on a specific physical property contrast between the desired product and the impurity, with the goal of producing a graded, purified, or classified output stream. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** "Cleaners and separators are pre-processing equipment that exploit the differences in physical properties of grains and impurities to improve the quality, storage life, and marketability of agricultural produce." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sieve" Mental Model Imagine you have a bowl of mixed dry fruit, rice grains, and tiny pebbles. To obtain clean rice, you instinctively: 1. **Blow air** across the bowl — the light husk pieces fly away (**Air Screen Cleaner / Aspirator**). 2. **Tilt the bowl and shake** it — small grains settle through the gaps but stones stay (**Screening**). 3. **Run a magnet** through it — iron filings cling to it (**Magnetic Separator**). 4. **Pour the mixture in water** — stones sink, grains float, chaff stays on top (**Gravity Separator / Specific Gravity Table**). Industrial cleaners and separators are simply **engineered, scaled-up, mechanized versions** of this exact intuitive logic. Each device exploits **one dominant physical contrast** between the grain (good product) and the contaminant (impurity). ## 1.3 Classification of Cleaners & Separators > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to classify equipment based on the **principle of separation**, not merely on the name of the machine. | S. No. | Principle of Separation | Equipment Example | Typical Impurity Removed | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Size (Diameter)** | Air-Screen Cleaner, Rotary Drum Sieve | Oversized straw, undersized sand | | 2 | **Aerodynamic Property (Terminal Velocity)** | Aspirator, Vertical Air Column | Light chaff, dust, empty glumes | | 3 | **Density (Specific Gravity)** | Gravity Table, Stoner | Stones, heavy metal, glass | | 4 | **Shape (Length / Width)** | Indented Cylinder, Disc Separator | Broken kernels, weed seeds | | 5 | **Magnetic Susceptibility** | Magnetic Drum, Magnetic Roll | Iron filings, rusted metal | | 6 | **Surface Texture / Friction** | Spiral Separator, Rough Roller | Smooth weed seeds from rough grain | | 7 | **Colour / Optical Reflectance** | Electronic Colour Sorter | Discoloured, mouldy, broken grains | | 8 | **Electrostatic Charge** | Electrostatic Separator | Decorticated husk, insect-damaged seed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** No single machine achieves 100 % purity. A typical grain cleaning line uses **3–5 machines in series**, each removing a *different class* of impurity. The output of one machine becomes the feed for the next — this is called a **Cleaning Train** or **Conditioning Line**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Governing Principles of Separation Every cleaner or separator, irrespective of make or model, operates on **one or more of the following physical contrasts**: ### Principle A — Size (Sieve-Based Separation) - Grains and impurities differ in **2D projected diameter**. - A **Screen** is a perforated surface with apertures of fixed dimension. - The fraction **passing through** the aperture is the **throughs (undersize)**; the fraction **retained** is the **overs (oversize)**. - Governing relation for a spherical particle of diameter $d$ passing a square aperture of side $a$: $$d \leq a$$ - Industrial screens are characterized by **Mesh Number** (number of openings per linear inch). ### Principle B — Aerodynamic Property (Air-Flow Separation) - Particles in an upward air stream experience **Drag Force** opposing gravity. - **Terminal Velocity** $v_t$ is the air velocity at which drag balances weight: $$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, m \, g}{\rho_a \, C_d \, A}}$$ where $m$ is particle mass, $A$ is cross-sectional area, $C_d$ is drag coefficient, $\rho_a$ is air density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration. - Particles with $v_t < v_{air}$ → carried upward (chaff). - Particles with $v_t > v_{air}$ → fall downward (good grain). ### Principle C — Density (Specific Gravity Separation) - Exploits difference in **mass per unit volume**. - On a vibrating deck with upward air-flow, light particles ride on top of the heavy bed layer and are pushed sideways by vibration. - **Differential Specific Gravity** is the operating metric: $$\Delta \rho = \rho_{grain} - \rho_{impurity}$$ ### Principle D — Shape (Length / Thickness) - **Indented Cylinder Separator**: Inner surface has small half-spherical pockets ("indents") of a calibrated diameter. - Short, round particles lodge in indents and are lifted out of the bulk stream. - Long, flat particles slide off and exit at the bottom. - **Disc Separator**: Discs with pockets lift short seeds from long grain streams (used in seed processing plants). ### Principle E — Magnetic Susceptibility - Materials with high **magnetic permeability** (iron, steel) are attracted to a magnetic field. - Two configurations: **Magnetic Drum** (rotating non-magnetic cylinder with internal stationary magnet) and **Magnetic Plate** (rows of magnets in a chute). ### Principle F — Optical / Colour - A high-speed **CCD / CMOS line-scan camera** captures reflected light from each kernel. - A reference colour window is pre-programmed; deviations trigger a precisely timed **pneumatic ejector** that fires a jet of air to divert the defective kernel. - Achieves sorting accuracies of **> 99.5 %** for premium rice and pulse processing. ## 2.2 Why Performance Evaluation is Mandatory > [!IMPORTANT] > A machine is only as good as its **measured cleaning efficiency**. The KTU 2024 Scheme (per the PCAGL607 syllabus) requires students to **quantitatively evaluate** cleaner and separator performance using standardized metrics. Evaluation answers four engineering questions: 1. **How much impurity was removed?** → Cleaning Efficiency $\eta_c$ 2. **How much good product was lost with the reject?** → Loss Percentage $L\%$ 3. **What is the purity of the output?** → Output Purity $P_{out}$ 4. **What is the throughput capacity?** → Throughput $Q$ in kg/h or t/h ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Favourite (Dec 2023 / June 2024 pattern):** Students often lose marks by confusing the **denominator** in efficiency formulas. Memorize which stream belongs in the numerator vs. denominator. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Symbol Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cleaning Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{M_{in,imp} - M_{out,imp}}{M_{in,imp}} \times 100$ | % | Mass of impurity before and after | | 2 | **Separation Efficiency (Overall)** | $\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{M_{good,out}}{M_{good,in}} \times 100$ | % | Recovery of good product in accept stream | | 3 | **Loss Percentage** | $L\% = \dfrac{M_{good,reject}}{M_{good,in}} \times 100$ | % | Good product going to reject | | 4 | **Output Purity** | $P_{out} = \dfrac{M_{good,out}}{M_{total,out}} \times 100$ | % | Accept stream quality | | 5 | **Throughput Capacity** | $Q = \dfrac{M_{feed}}{t_{process}}$ | kg/h or t/h | Mass processed per unit time | | 6 | **Terminal Velocity** | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, d}{C_d \, \rho_a}}$ | m/s | Stokes' regime modification | | 7 | **Screen Effectiveness** | $E_{screen} = \dfrac{(W_p - W_t)(1 - W_t/W_p)}{W_p(1 - W_t/W_p)}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of feed that *should* pass that *did* pass | | 8 | **Grade Efficiency** | $T(x) = \dfrac{\text{Mass of particles of size } x \text{ in accepts}}{\text{Mass of particles of size } x \text{ in feed}}$ | — | Size-specific separation curve | | 9 | **Mass Balance (Impurity)** | $F \cdot x_{F,i} = A \cdot x_{A,i} + R \cdot x_{R,i}$ | kg/h | $F$=feed, $A$=accepts, $R$=rejects | | 10 | **Reduced (Normalized) Efficiency** | $\eta_{norm} = \dfrac{\eta_c - L\%}{100 - L\%} \times 100$ | % | Corrects efficiency for unavoidable losses | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Loss:** The denominator for $\eta_c$ is **$M_{in,imp}$ (impurity in feed)** — NOT the total feed. The denominator for $P_{out}$ is **$M_{total,out}$ (total accept stream)** — NOT impurity only. Mixing these up loses full marks. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Pre-storage cleaning** in FCI godowns reduces **insect infestation by 60–70 %** because broken kernels and dust (which harbour insects) are removed. - **Pre-milling cleaning** in rice mills reduces **abrasive wear** on de-stoners and polishers, increasing machine life by ~30 %. - **Seed processing plants** use indented cylinders and gravity tables to achieve **> 99 % physical purity** certified by the Indian Minimum Seed Certification Standards. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cleaning Efficiency from First Principles **Problem Setup (Standard KTU Format):** A lot of paddy is fed to an Air-Screen Cleaner. The data collected during steady-state operation over a 10-minute interval is: | Stream | Total Mass (kg) | Impurity Content (%) | Good Grain (%) | |---|---|---|---| | Feed (F) | 500.0 | 4.0 | 96.0 | | Accept (A) | 460.0 | 0.5 | 99.5 | | Reject (R) | 40.0 | 47.5 | 52.5 | **Step 1 — Compute the absolute impurity mass in each stream.** For the **Feed**: $$M_{in,imp} = F \times \frac{x_{F,i}}{100} = 500.0 \times \frac{4.0}{100} = 20.0 \text{ kg}$$ For the **Accept (cleaned output)**: $$M_{out,imp} = A \times \frac{x_{A,i}}{100} = 460.0 \times \frac{0.5}{100} = 2.30 \text{ kg}$$ For the **Reject**: $$M_{R,imp} = R \times \frac{x_{R,i}}{100} = 40.0 \times \frac{47.5}{100} = 19.0 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 — Mass Balance Check (essential for full marks).** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Impurity In (Feed)} &= M_{in,imp} = 20.0 \text{ kg} \\ \text{Impurity Out (Accept + Reject)} &= M_{out,imp} + M_{R,imp} = 2.30 + 19.0 = 21.3 \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$ A 6.5 % deviation indicates experimental loss (dust entrainment, sampling error). For the KTU answer, we proceed using the **feed-side** impurity as the canonical reference. **Step 3 — Apply the Cleaning Efficiency Formula.** $$\eta_c = \frac{M_{in,imp} - M_{out,imp}}{M_{in,imp}} \times 100$$ Substituting the values: $$\begin{aligned} \eta_c &= \frac{20.0 - 2.30}{20.0} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{17.70}{20.0} \times 100 \\ &= 88.5 \ \% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Loss Percentage.** Good grain in feed: $$M_{in,good} = 500.0 \times 0.96 = 480.0 \text{ kg}$$ Good grain in reject (lost to waste stream): $$M_{R,good} = 40.0 \times 0.525 = 21.0 \text{ kg}$$ $$\begin{aligned} L\% &= \frac{M_{R,good}}{M_{in,good}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{21.0}{480.0} \times 100 \\ &= 4.375 \ \% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute Output Purity.** $$P_{out} = \frac{M_{good,out}}{M_{total,out}} \times 100 = \frac{460.0 \times 0.995}{460.0} \times 100 = 99.5 \ \%$$ **Step 6 — Compute Throughput Capacity.** $$Q = \frac{M_{feed}}{t} = \frac{500.0 \text{ kg}}{10/60 \text{ h}} = \frac{500.0}{0.1667} = 3000 \text{ kg/h} = 3.0 \text{ t/h}$$ **Step 7 — Compute Normalized Efficiency.** $$\eta_{norm} = \frac{\eta_c - L\%}{100 - L\%} \times 100 = \frac{88.5 - 4.375}{100 - 4.375} \times 100 = \frac{84.125}{95.625} \times 100 = 87.98 \ \%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note:** Reporting $\eta_c$ alone is incomplete. KTU 2024 ESE answers **must** include (a) $\eta_c$, (b) $L\%$, (c) $P_{out}$, and (d) a mass balance check. A student reporting only $\eta_c$ loses 4–5 marks out of 14. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Terminal Velocity for Air-Screen Aspirator Design **Step 1 — Identify particle regime using Reynolds Number.** $$Re_p = \frac{\rho_a \, v \, d}{\mu}$$ For a wheat kernel with $d = 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_p = 1300$ kg/m³, in air with $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, and trial $v = 5$ m/s: $$Re_p = \frac{1.2 \times 5 \times 0.006}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 2000$$ This is in the **Newton's Law regime** ($Re_p > 1000$), so $C_d \approx 0.44$. **Step 2 — Apply Newton's Law Terminal Velocity.** $$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, d}{C_d \, \rho_a}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v_t &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times (1300 - 1.2) \times 0.006}{0.44 \times 1.2}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 1298.8 \times 0.006}{0.528}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{152.91}{0.528}} \\ &= \sqrt{289.6} \\ &\approx 17.02 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Design the aspirator air velocity.** For clean separation, the air velocity $v_{air}$ is set between the $v_t$ of chaff and that of good grain: - Chaff terminal velocity $\approx 1.5$ m/s - Good grain terminal velocity $\approx 17.0$ m/s - **Operating window:** $1.5 \text{ m/s} < v_{air} < 17.0 \text{ m/s}$ - **Practical selection:** $v_{air} \approx 8$–$10$ m/s for wheat/paddy cleaning. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Screen Effectiveness for Sieve Analysis The KTU 2024 syllabus frequently tests the **modified degree of separation** (also called **screen effectiveness** $E$). Given: - $W_p$ = mass fraction of feed *smaller* than aperture (theoretical pass) - $W_t$ = actual mass fraction of feed that passed through **Step 1 — Define oversize in feed.** $$\text{Oversize in feed} = (1 - W_p)$$ **Step 2 — Define oversize in throughs.** $$\text{Oversize in throughs} = (1 - W_t)$$ **Step 3 — Formulate the Effectiveness.** $$E_{screen} = \dfrac{(W_p - W_t)(1 - W_t/W_p)}{W_p(1 - W_t/W_p)}$$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A screen has $W_p = 0.80$ and the actual $W_t = 0.72$. $$\begin{aligned} E_{screen} &= \dfrac{(0.80 - 0.72)(1 - 0.72/0.80)}{0.80(1 - 0.72/0.80)} \\ &= \dfrac{0.08 \times (1 - 0.9)}{0.80 \times 0.1} \\ &= \dfrac{0.08 \times 0.1}{0.08} \\ &= 0.10 \ \text{ or } \ 10 \ \% \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Metric When? | Scenario | Primary Metric to Report | Secondary Metric | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Evaluating a **new cleaner** at a procurement godown | Cleaning Efficiency $\eta_c$ | Loss % | Quantifies impurity removal performance | | Validating an **established separator** in a seed plant | Output Purity $P_{out}$ | Grade Efficiency Curve | Seed certification demands purity | | Comparing **two similar machines** | Normalized Efficiency $\eta_{norm}$ | Throughput $Q$ | Allows fair comparison at same loss level | | Diagnosing a **faulty machine** | Loss % | $\eta_c$ | High loss indicates mis-calibration | | **Design** of aspirator duct | Terminal Velocity $v_t$ | Reynolds Number $Re_p$ | Determines fan selection | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — A Typical Grain Cleaning Train (KTU Lab / Pilot Plant) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Rough Dirty Lot
Harvested Paddy or Wheat] --> B[Pre-Cleaner Scalper
Removes large straw] B --> C[Bucket Elevator
Lifts to top of plant] C --> D[Air-Screen Cleaner
Size + Aerodynamic separation] D --> E[Aspirator Column
Light chaff removal] E --> F[Disc Separator
Length-based separation] F --> G[Specific Gravity Table
Density-based separation] G --> H[Indented Cylinder
Length classification of seeds] H --> I[Magnetic Drum
Removes ferrous fragments] I --> J[Optical Colour Sorter
Removes discoloured kernels] J --> K[Cleaned Graded Lot
Ready for storage or milling] D --> R1[Reject Stream 1
Oversize trash] E --> R2[Reject Stream 2
Light chaff dust] F --> R3[Reject Stream 3
Long straw pieces] G --> R4[Reject Stream 4
Stones and heavy impurities] H --> R5[Reject Stream 5
Short weed seeds] I --> R6[Reject Stream 6
Ferrous metal scrap] J --> R7[Reject Stream 7
Discoloured defective grains] style A fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#8a6d00 style K fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style R1 fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 style R2 fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 style R3 fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 style R4 fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 style R5 fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 style R6 fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 style R7 fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mass-Balance Around a Single Cleaner ```mermaid flowchart TB FEED[Feed Stream F
Total Mass Mf kg
Impurity xf percent] --> SPLIT SPLIT{Separation
Process} SPLIT -->|Accepted Stream| ACC[Accept A
Mass Ma kg
Impurity xa percent
GOOD PRODUCT] SPLIT -->|Rejected Stream| REJ[Reject R
Mass Mr kg
Impurity xr percent
IMPURITY REMOVED] ACC --> OUT1[To Next Cleaning Stage] REJ --> OUT2[To Waste Disposal] style FEED fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#8a6d00 style ACC fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style REJ fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 style SPLIT fill:#d6e6f5,stroke:#003366 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Performance Evaluation Protocol ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Step 1 Operate Cleaner
at Rated Capacity] --> S2[Step 2 Collect Feed Sample
Weigh Mf and Analyse xf] S2 --> S3[Step 3 Collect Accept Sample
Weigh Ma and Analyse xa] S3 --> S4[Step 4 Collect Reject Sample
Weigh Mr and Analyse xr] S4 --> S5[Step 5 Verify Mass Balance
Mf equals Ma plus Mr] S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compute Cleaning Efficiency etac] S6 --> S7[Step 7 Compute Loss Percent L percent] S7 --> S8[Step 8 Compute Output Purity Pout] S8 --> S9[Step 9 Compute Throughput Q] S9 --> S10[Step 10 Tabulate and Compare
with Manufacturer Specification] S10 --> DEC{Performance
Meets Standard} DEC -->|Yes| OK[Accept and Document] DEC -->|No| ADJ[Adjust Calibration
Re-run Trial] ADJ --> S1 style S1 fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366 style S10 fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#8a4500 style DEC fill:#d6e6f5,stroke:#003366 style OK fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style ADJ fill:#f8d6d6,stroke:#8b0000 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Separation Principle Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{What is the
dominant impurity type} Q1 -->|Stones and heavy metal| P1[Density Separator
Specific Gravity Table] Q1 -->|Straw and chaff| P2[Air Screen Cleaner
Aspirator] Q1 -->|Weed seeds| P3[Indented Cylinder
Disc Separator] Q1 -->|Iron filings| P4[Magnetic Drum
Magnetic Plate] Q1 -->|Discoloured kernels| P5[Optical Colour Sorter] Q1 -->|Insect damaged seeds| P6[Electrostatic Separator] P1 --> NOTE[Principle Difference in Density] P2 --> NOTE2[Principle Difference in Terminal Velocity] P3 --> NOTE3[Principle Difference in Length Shape] P4 --> NOTE4[Principle Difference in Magnetic Susceptibility] P5 --> NOTE5[Principle Difference in Reflected Light] P6 --> NOTE6[Principle Difference in Electrical Charge] style Q1 fill:#d6e6f5,stroke:#003366 style NOTE fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style NOTE2 fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style NOTE3 fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style NOTE4 fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style NOTE5 fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 style NOTE6 fill:#d4f5d4,stroke:#006400 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1.** Differentiate between a **cleaner** and a **separator** with one suitable example of each. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - A **cleaner** is a pre-processing device that removes foreign matter (FM) from a bulk agricultural commodity to improve its purity and storage stability. *Example: Air-Screen Cleaner removes chaff and dust from harvested paddy.* - A **separator** is a device that divides the cleaned material into two or more **graded fractions** based on a single dominant physical property, often for value addition. *Example: Indented Cylinder Separator separates long wheat kernels from short rye-grass seeds; gravity table separates heavy mature kernels from lighter immature ones for seed grading.* - **Key distinction:** *Cleaner* emphasizes *impurity removal*; *Separator* emphasizes *fractionation into usable grades*. --- > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]** > **Q2.** List the **performance evaluation parameters** used for assessing an agricultural grain cleaner. State the formula for cleaning efficiency. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The four standard KTU-mandated parameters are: 1. **Cleaning Efficiency** $\eta_c = \dfrac{M_{in,imp} - M_{out,imp}}{M_{in,imp}} \times 100$ [%] 2. **Loss Percentage** $L\% = \dfrac{M_{good,reject}}{M_{good,feed}} \times 100$ [%] 3. **Output Purity** $P_{out} = \dfrac{M_{good,accept}}{M_{total,accept}} \times 100$ [%] 4. **Throughput Capacity** $Q = \dfrac{M_{feed}}{t}$ [kg/h] ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE gives an **internal choice** between two Part B questions. Both options are provided below with complete sub-parts and model solutions. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]* **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of an **Air-Screen Cleaner cum Aspirator**. List the impurities it removes and the principles it exploits. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** - **Construction:** (1) Feed hopper with flow-control gate; (2) Two vibrating screens — *scalper* (top, larger aperture) and *sieve* (bottom, smaller aperture); (3) Centrifugal fan with air duct; (4) Aspirator column with adjustable damper; (5) Four discharge spouts — overs, throughs, light rejects, clean accept. - **Working Principle:** Material flows by gravity over the scalper (removing oversize straw), then over the sieve (removing undersize sand/grit). The aspirator column blows air upward, lifting low-density chaff while heavier good grain falls. - **Principles exploited:** **Size** (screening) + **Aerodynamic property** (terminal velocity difference in aspirator). - **Impurities removed:** Oversize straw, undersize grit, light chaff, dust, empty glumes. - **Block diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 (Air-Screen Cleaner block). --- **Q. (b)** During performance evaluation of a paddy cleaner operated for 8 minutes, the following data was recorded: | Stream | Mass (kg) | Impurity (%) | |---|---|---| | Feed | 400 | 5.0 | | Accept | 372 | 0.8 | | Reject | 28 | 64.0 | Calculate: **(i) Cleaning efficiency, (ii) Loss percentage, (iii) Output purity, (iv) Throughput in kg/h.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **(i) Cleaning Efficiency** (1.5 Marks): $$M_{in,imp} = 400 \times 0.05 = 20.0 \text{ kg}, \quad M_{out,imp} = 372 \times 0.008 = 2.976 \text{ kg}$$ $$\eta_c = \frac{20.0 - 2.976}{20.0} \times 100 = \frac{17.024}{20.0} \times 100 = 85.12 \ \%$$ **(ii) Loss Percentage** (1.5 Marks): $$M_{good,feed} = 400 \times 0.95 = 380.0 \text{ kg}, \quad M_{good,reject} = 28 \times 0.36 = 10.08 \text{ kg}$$ $$L\% = \frac{10.08}{380.0} \times 100 = 2.65 \ \%$$ **(iii) Output Purity** (2 Marks): $$M_{good,accept} = 372 \times 0.992 = 369.024 \text{ kg}, \quad P_{out} = \frac{369.024}{372.0} \times 100 = 99.2 \ \%$$ **(iv) Throughput** (2 Marks): $$Q = \frac{400 \text{ kg}}{8/60 \text{ h}} = \frac{400 \times 60}{8} = 3000 \text{ kg/h} = 3.0 \text{ t/h}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):** > 1. Do **not** divide by the *total feed* (400 kg) when computing $\eta_c$ — the denominator is **impurity in feed (20 kg)**, otherwise marks are slashed by 50 %. > 2. The 0.992 in (iii) comes from $1 - 0.008 = 0.992$ (accept is 0.8 % impurity, hence 99.2 % good). > 3. Always state the **units** in the final answer (kg/h or t/h) — a numerical answer without units is treated as incomplete (–0.5 mark). > 4. A **mass balance check** showing $F \approx A + R$ (here $400 = 372 + 28 = 400$ ✓) earns a **bonus half-mark** if mentioned. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Internal Choice Alternative]* **Q. (a)** Describe the construction, working, and applications of a **Specific Gravity (Gravity Table) Separator**. What is the role of air flow and deck vibration? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** - **Construction:** (1) Vibrating rectangular deck covered with porous mesh or perforated plate; (2) Eccentric drive mechanism producing longitudinal vibration; (3) Centrifugal fan blowing air *upward* through the deck; (4) Adjustable inclination and feed-end height; (5) Three discharge zones — heavy discharge (stones), middling, light discharge (chaff, immature). - **Working:** A stratified bed forms as upward air fluidizes the granular mass; heavier particles (stones, metal) sink to the deck and move *uphill* by vibration, while lighter particles (chaff, immature grain) ride the air cushion and move *downhill* by gravity + deck slope. - **Role of air flow:** Creates a *quasi-fluidized bed* separating particles by **density** rather than size. - **Role of deck vibration:** Provides *directional transport* of the heavy fraction toward the stone discharge. - **Applications:** Removal of stones from wheat, paddy, and pulses; separation of mature vs. immature seeds; removal of glass and metal fragments. --- **Q. (b)** A gravity table receives 1000 kg/h of a wheat–sand mixture containing 8 % sand. After separation: - **Heavy discharge (stones):** 75 kg/h, 95 % sand, 5 % wheat - **Light discharge (cleaned wheat):** 925 kg/h, 0.5 % sand, 99.5 % wheat Calculate: **(i) Mass balance check, (ii) Cleaning efficiency for sand removal, (iii) Loss of wheat to stone stream, (iv) Output purity of cleaned wheat.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **(i) Mass Balance Check** (1 Mark): $$F = 1000 \text{ kg/h}, \quad A + R = 925 + 75 = 1000 \text{ kg/h} \ \checkmark$$ **(ii) Cleaning Efficiency for Sand Removal** (2 Marks): $$M_{sand,feed} = 1000 \times 0.08 = 80.0 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$M_{sand,accept} = 925 \times 0.005 = 4.625 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\eta_c = \frac{80.0 - 4.625}{80.0} \times 100 = \frac{75.375}{80.0} \times 100 = 94.22 \ \%$$ **(iii) Loss of Wheat to Stone Stream** (2 Marks): $$M_{wheat,feed} = 1000 \times 0.92 = 920.0 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$M_{wheat,reject} = 75 \times 0.05 = 3.75 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$L\% = \frac{3.75}{920.0} \times 100 = 0.408 \ \%$$ **(iv) Output Purity of Cleaned Wheat** (2 Marks): $$P_{out} = \frac{925 \times 0.995}{925} \times 100 = 99.5 \ \%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):** > 1. In a binary mixture (wheat + sand), the *good* fraction is **92 %** in feed ($100 - 8$), **not 92 %** in accept — students frequently write the wrong denominator for loss. > 2. The cleaning efficiency here is computed with respect to **sand**, not total impurity — explicitly state *which impurity* is being removed. > 3. Round to **2 decimal places** in the final answer; reporting $94.21875$ % without rounding is not penalized but is bad practice. ## 5.3 KTU 2024 Common Pitfalls — Top 5 Reasons for Mark Deduction > [!WARNING] > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $v_t$ in cm/s when the formula is in m/s — silent but lethal error. > 2. **Denominator Confusion:** Using $M_{feed}$ in $\eta_c$ formula — full-mark loss in sub-part (i) of every numerical. > 3. **No Mass Balance Check:** Examiners award an *easy half-mark* for stating $F = A + R$ — missing it = direct loss. > 4. **Reporting Only One Metric:** The KTU rubric awards marks *per sub-part* — answering only $\eta_c$ from a 4-part numerical yields only 1.5 / 7. > 5. **Confusing "Purity" with "Efficiency":** Purity is the *quality* of the output stream; efficiency is the *fraction of impurity removed*. These are **not** the same. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Cleaner** removes impurities; **Separator** fractionates into grades. - [x] Eight major principles: size, aerodynamic, density, shape, magnetic, optical, electrostatic, surface texture. - [x] **Cleaning efficiency** $\eta_c$ denominator = *impurity in feed* (NOT total feed). - [x] **Loss %** denominator = *good product in feed*; numerator = *good product in reject*. - [x] **Output purity** denominator = *total accept stream*; numerator = *good product in accept*. - [x] **Throughput** $Q = M_{feed} / t$, in kg/h or t/h. - [x] **Normalized efficiency** corrects for unavoidable losses: $\eta_{norm} = (\eta_c - L\%) / (100 - L\%)$. - [x] **Terminal velocity** for aerodynamic separator: $v_t = \sqrt{2g(\rho_p - \rho_a)d / (C_d \rho_a)}$. - [x] **Screen effectiveness** $E_{screen}$ quantifies how well a sieve actually separates vs. ideal separation. - [x] Always perform a **mass balance check** $F = A + R$ before reporting any efficiency. - [x] Air-Screen Cleaner is the **most common first-stage** equipment in every grain cleaning line. - [x] Gravity Table removes **stones and heavy impurities**; cannot remove chaff or light impurities. - [x] Magnetic Separator handles **ferrous metal** only — non-ferrous metals need an **eddy-current separator**. - [x] Optical sorter achieves **> 99.5 % accuracy** for colour defects in rice, pulses, coffee, and nuts. - [x] Real industrial cleaning lines use **3–5 machines in series** (the "cleaning train" concept). - [x] Cleaning improves **storage life by 60–70 %** and reduces **milling wear by 30 %** — a direct economic justification. - [x] KTU answer must include **all four** metrics ($\eta_c$, $L\%$, $P_{out}$, $Q$) for full marks in a numerical.

topics.title: Performance Evaluation of Different Size Reduction Machines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation of Different Size Reduction Machines

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Size Reduction** is a unit operation in agricultural processing that involves the mechanical breakdown of solid food materials (grains, cereals, spices, oilseeds, feed ingredients) into smaller fragments through the application of crushing, impact, attrition, shear, or cutting forces. The **performance evaluation** of size reduction machines is the systematic quantification of how effectively a given mill or crusher converts feed material of known initial size into a product of desired size, measured against standard indices of energy efficiency, throughput, fineness, and product uniformity.

**Performance Evaluation** is defined as the experimental and analytical process of measuring the operational and product-quality parameters of size reduction equipment — including **capacity**, **specific energy consumption**, **reduction ratio**, **fineness modulus**, and **grinding efficiency** — to compare different machines, optimize process conditions, and select appropriate equipment for a target particle size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For the Module Experiment 2, students are expected to *operate* (or simulate the operation of) at least two different size reduction machines (commonly a **hammer mill** and a **roller mill**, or a **jaw crusher** and a **ball mill**), record the **feed rate**, **discharge rate**, **particle size distribution** (via sieve analysis), **power drawn** by the motor, and then compute the **reduction ratio**, **specific energy consumption**, and **grinding efficiency** using the three classical laws of comminution: **Kick's Law, Rittinger's Law, and Bond's Law**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Think of size reduction like **grinding spices in your kitchen** versus using an industrial **chakki (stone mill)** at a flour mill.

- When you crush a few coriander seeds with the bottom of a heavy glass on a *chakla* (rolling pin and flat stone), you are doing a **slow, low-capacity** size reduction. The product is coarse, irregular, and uses **a lot of your hand energy** for very little output. This is analogous to a **jaw crusher** or **roller mill** in coarse crushing.
- Now imagine a **mixer-grinder** at home that pulverizes the same coriander into fine powder in 30 seconds. It uses **high speed impact** and produces a **uniform fine product** very fast — analogous to a **hammer mill** or **ball mill** in fine grinding.

The question that agricultural engineers face is: *"Which machine gives me the best product size, with the lowest energy cost per kilogram, at the highest throughput?"* That is exactly what **performance evaluation** answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Performance Metrics to Remember (Five Pillars):**
> 1. **Reduction Ratio (RR)** — How much the feed size is reduced.
> 2. **Capacity (Q)** — How much material is processed per unit time (kg/h or t/h).
> 3. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — Energy used per unit mass (kWh/tonne).
> 4. **Grinding Efficiency (η_g)** — Ratio of theoretical energy (from Rittinger/Kick/Bond) to actual energy input.
> 5. **Fineness Modulus (FM)** — A single number representing the overall coarseness of the ground product.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Units

The following standard units and constants are used throughout size reduction performance calculations:

| Parameter | Standard SI Unit | CGS / Practical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Particle diameter (D) | **meter (m)** | **micrometer (µm)** or **millimeter (mm)** |
| Energy (E) | **joule (J)** | **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** |
| Work Index (W_i) | **kWh/tonne** | kWh/short ton |
| Power (P) | **watt (W)** or **kilowatt (kW)** | horsepower (hp) |
| Capacity (Q) | **kg/s** | **tonne/hour (t/h)** or **kg/h** |
| Reduction Ratio (RR) | **dimensionless** | — |
| Specific Surface | **m²/kg** | cm²/g |
| Bulk Density (ρ_b) | **kg/m³** | g/cm³ |
| Moisture Content (MC) | **% w.b.** (wet basis) or **% d.b.** (dry basis) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Work Index (W_i)** is a material property that represents the **energy required to reduce a unit mass of material from infinite feed size to 80% passing 100 µm (or 100 microns)**. It is the most important constant in Bond's Law.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Size Reduction Machines

Size reduction equipment in agricultural processing is classified primarily by the **mechanism of force application** and the **size range** of the feed/product.

### A. Based on Mechanism of Size Reduction

| Mechanism | Force Type | Typical Machines | Typical Feed Size | Typical Product Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Crushing** | Compression | Jaw crusher, Gyratory crusher, Roll crusher | 100–1500 mm | 10–100 mm |
| **Impact** | Dynamic impact | Hammer mill, Impact crusher, Disintegrator | 5–50 mm | 0.1–5 mm |
| **Attrition** | Friction / Shear | Ball mill, Pebble mill, Rod mill, Attrition mill | 0.1–5 mm | 10–500 µm |
| **Cutting / Shearing** | Sharp blades | Rotary cutter, Knife mill, Burr mill | 1–50 mm | 0.1–10 mm |

### B. Based on Product Fineness (Industry Convention)

| Class | Product Size | Machines | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coarse grinding** | 100 µm – 1 mm | Hammer mill, Roller mill | Animal feed, coarse flour |
| **Fine grinding** | 10 µm – 100 µm | Ball mill, Attrition mill | Spice powders, fine flour |
| **Ultra-fine grinding** | < 10 µm | Jet mill, Fluid energy mill | Specialty chemicals, pigments |

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Laws of Comminution

These three laws form the theoretical backbone of every KTU question on size reduction performance.

### 2.2.1 Kick's Law (1885)

Kick observed that the **energy required** for size reduction is **proportional to the ratio of the initial to final size**, raised to a logarithmic form.

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

- **Best suited for**: **Coarse crushing** (large particles, ratio > 4).
- **Physical meaning**: The energy goes mainly into **deformation of bulk volume**, not into creating new surface.

### 2.2.2 Rittinger's Law (1867)

Rittinger proposed that the **energy required** is **proportional to the new surface area created** during size reduction.

$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

- **Best suited for**: **Fine grinding** (small particles, ratio < 4, product < 200 µm).
- **Physical meaning**: Energy is consumed in **creating new surface**, hence proportional to the *change in specific surface*.

### 2.2.3 Bond's Law (1952) — The Industry Standard

Bond's law is a **compromise** between Kick and Rittinger and is the most widely used in industry. The energy is proportional to the **square root of the difference in reciprocals of the square roots** of the sizes.

$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

- **Best suited for**: **Intermediate grinding** (most ball mill and rod mill applications).
- **Work Index (W_i)** — measured in **kWh/tonne** — is the energy required to reduce one tonne of material from *infinite* size to 80% passing 100 µm.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU question in Module 2 reduces to one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reduction Ratio (RR)** | $RR = \dfrac{D_f}{D_p}$ | dimensionless | Where $D_f$ = feed size, $D_p$ = product size |
| 2 | **Differential Reduction Ratio** | $RR_i = \dfrac{D_{i,\text{feed}}}{D_{i,\text{product}}}$ | dimensionless | Per-screen reduction |
| 3 | **Average Particle Size (mass mean)** | $\bar{D} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \cdot \bar{d}_i)}{\sum m_i}$ | mm or µm | Geometric mean preferred |
| 4 | **Geometric Mean Diameter** | $d_{geo} = \sqrt{d_1 \cdot d_2}$ | mm | For log-normal distribution |
| 5 | **Kick's Law Energy** | $E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ | kWh/tonne | Coarse crushing |
| 6 | **Rittinger's Law Energy** | $E = K_R \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | kWh/tonne | Fine grinding |
| 7 | **Bond's Law Energy** | $E = W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$ | kWh/tonne | Intermediate grinding |
| 8 | **Bond's Work Index** | $W_i = \dfrac{1.1 \cdot 44.5}{P_i^{0.82} \cdot G^{0.5}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{80}}}\right)}$ | kWh/tonne | For ball mill grinding |
| 9 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m} = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | kWh/tonne | Performance metric |
| 10 | **Grinding Efficiency** | $\eta_g = \dfrac{E_{\text{theoretical}}}{E_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Performance metric |
| 11 | **Capacity (mass basis)** | $Q = \dfrac{m}{t}$ | tonne/h | Throughput |
| 12 | **Capacity (volumetric)** | $Q_v = \dfrac{V}{t} = A \cdot v$ | m³/h | Belt/feeder systems |
| 13 | **Fineness Modulus (FM)** | $FM = \dfrac{\sum (\text{cumulative \% retained on std sieves})}{100}$ | dimensionless | Aggregate/flour grading |
| 14 | **Uniformity Index (n)** | $y = 100 \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^n$ | dimensionless | Rosin–Rammler distribution |
| 15 | **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | For capacity and silo design |
| 16 | **Moisture Content (w.b.)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Pre-drying effect |
| 17 | **Power Draw (Gross)** | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W or kW | From motor torque/speed |
| 18 | **New Surface Created (Rittinger)** | $\Delta S = K' \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | m²/kg | New surface per kg |
| 19 | **Specific Surface (sphere)** | $S_v = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p \cdot D}$ | m²/kg | Per unit mass |
| 20 | **Energy Efficiency Ratio** | $EER = \dfrac{\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}}}{\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}}}$ | dimensionless | Comparative metric |

> **Note on notation:** Throughout this module, $D$ and $d$ are used **interchangeably** for particle diameter. Subscripts: $_1$ = feed (initial), $_2$ = product (final), $_{80}$ = 80% passing size.

## 2.4 The Why and How Behind Each Parameter

### 2.4.1 Reduction Ratio (RR) — The Most Quoted Number

**Why?** Because it tells you **at one glance** how aggressive the machine is. A jaw crusher may have RR = 4–7, while a ball mill may have RR = 20–200.

**How it is measured experimentally?**
1. Take a representative feed sample (≥ 1 kg).
2. Perform **sieve analysis** using a stack of standard sieves (e.g., 4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, 150 µm, 75 µm).
3. Determine the **80% passing size** ($D_{80}$) of the feed and the product.
4. $RR = \dfrac{D_{80,\text{feed}}}{D_{80,\text{product}}}$

### 2.4.2 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) — The Energy Number

**Why?** It is the **single most important economic parameter** in size reduction. Milling can account for **60–70% of total energy** in a feed mill. A reduction of 1 kWh/tonne in SEC can save a large feed mill **lakhs of rupees per year**.

**How it is measured?**
1. Record the **no-load power** ($P_{nl}$) of the mill motor with empty hopper.
2. Record the **loaded power** ($P_L$) during steady-state operation.
3. Net milling power: $P_{\text{net}} = P_L - P_{nl}$
4. Weigh the mass of material processed in time $t$: $m$ (kg or tonnes).
5. $SEC = \dfrac{P_{\text{net}} \cdot t}{m}$ — units: kWh/tonne

### 2.4.3 Grinding Efficiency (η_g) — The Performance Number

**Why?** It tells you **how close the mill is operating to the theoretical minimum energy** predicted by the comminution laws. Most mills operate at η_g between **1% and 25%** — most of the input energy is lost as heat, sound, and friction in bearings.

**How it is calculated?**
1. Calculate the **theoretical energy** $E_{\text{th}}$ using Rittinger's, Kick's, or Bond's law.
2. Measure the **actual energy input** $E_{\text{act}} = SEC$.
3. $\eta_g = \dfrac{E_{\text{th}}}{E_{\text{act}}} \times 100\%$

### 2.4.4 Fineness Modulus (FM) — The Quality Number

**Why?** In flour and animal-feed grading, FM gives a **single number** to compare batches. Higher FM = coarser product.

**How?** Sum the cumulative percentages retained on six standard sieves (150 µm, 300 µm, 600 µm, 1.18 mm, 2.36 mm, 4.75 mm) and divide by 100.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These performance parameters are the **bedrock of equipment selection** in:

- **Feed mills** — selecting hammer mills vs. roller mills for poultry/cattle feed.
- **Flour mills** — evaluating roller mill stands for wheat, maize, ragi.
- **Spice processing** — selecting pulverizers for turmeric, chilli, coriander.
- **Oil extraction plants** — flaking vs. crushing vs. grinding of oilseeds.
- **Bioprocess industry** — particle size of substrate for enzymatic hydrolysis.
- **Dairy and food industry** — grinding sugar, salt, milk powder reconstitution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference Values (for context only, do not memorize all):**
> - Hammer mill for maize: SEC ≈ 5–15 kWh/tonne; RR = 10–25.
> - Ball mill for spice: SEC ≈ 15–40 kWh/tonne; RR = 50–200.
> - Roller mill for wheat: SEC ≈ 3–8 kWh/tonne; RR = 3–6.
> - Bond's Work Index for wheat: ~7–9 kWh/tonne; for limestone: ~10–12 kWh/tonne; for iron ore: ~12–15 kWh/tonne.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kick's Law from First Principles

### Assumption
The energy required for size reduction is **proportional to the volumetric strain** of the particle, and the volumetric strain is **proportional to the natural log of the size ratio**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a cube of side $D_1$ being crushed to a cube of side $D_2$.

**Step 1:** The volumetric strain $\epsilon$ in elastic compression theory is:
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$$

where $V_1 = D_1^3$ and $V_2 = D_2^3$ (volumes of the equivalent cubes).

**Step 2:** Substitute:
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{D_1^3}{D_2^3}\right) = 3 \ln\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The energy per unit volume is proportional to strain (from elastic theory):
$$\frac{E}{V} \propto \epsilon$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, for unit mass, the energy per unit mass is:
$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

where $K_K$ is the **Kick's constant** (kWh/tonne) — a material property determined experimentally.

$$\boxed{\,E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Rittinger's Law from First Principles

### Assumption
The energy required is **proportional to the new surface area created** (because new surface requires breaking molecular bonds).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a single spherical particle of diameter $D_1$ and density $\rho_p$. Its mass is:
$$m_1 = \rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D_1^3$$

**Step 2:** Its surface area is:
$$A_1 = \pi D_1^2$$

**Step 3:** The specific surface (surface per unit mass) is:
$$S_1 = \frac{A_1}{m_1} = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D_1^3} = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot D_1}$$

**Step 4:** After size reduction to $D_2$, new specific surface is:
$$S_2 = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot D_2}$$

**Step 5:** The change in specific surface (new surface created per unit mass):
$$\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = \frac{6}{\rho_p} \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Rittinger's postulate: Energy $\propto$ new surface created:
$$E = K_R \cdot \Delta S = \frac{6 K_R}{\rho_p} \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Combining constants: $K_R' = \dfrac{6 K_R}{\rho_p}$, so:
$$\boxed{\,E = K_R' \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Bond's Law (The Geometric Mean of Kick and Rittinger)

### The Logic

Bond noticed empirically that **real energy consumption** lies *between* the predictions of Kick's law (coarse) and Rittinger's law (fine). He proposed that the energy is proportional to the **geometric mean** of the energy terms of the two laws.

**Step 1:** Express Kick's energy: $E_K = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$

**Step 2:** Express Rittinger's energy: $E_R = K_R \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$

**Step 3:** Bond's energy is the **product** raised to the **0.5 power** (geometric mean):
$$E_B = \sqrt{E_K \cdot E_R}$$

**Step 4:** For the limiting case where $D_1 \to \infty$ (the standard reference condition for Work Index), the algebra simplifies to:
$$E_B = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

where $W_i$ is the **Bond Work Index** (kWh/tonne).

$$\boxed{\,E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bond's Standard Reference Condition:**
> By definition, the Work Index is the energy (kWh) required to reduce **1 tonne** of material from **infinite feed size** to **80% passing 100 µm**. This is why the formula uses $D_{80}$ sizes in practice.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Complete Hammer Mill Evaluation

### Problem Statement

A hammer mill is used to grind **maize (corn)** for animal feed. The following data were recorded during a steady-state run:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Feed rate | 500 kg/h |
| Feed $D_{80}$ (80% passing size) | 4.0 mm |
| Product $D_{80}$ | 600 µm |
| No-load motor power | 1.2 kW |
| Loaded motor power | 7.5 kW |
| Density of maize | 720 kg/m³ |

**Calculate:** (a) Reduction Ratio, (b) Specific Energy Consumption, (c) Theoretical energy by Rittinger's Law (assume $K_R = 12$ kWh/tonne for maize), (d) Grinding efficiency.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Reduction Ratio**

$$RR = \frac{D_{80,\text{feed}}}{D_{80,\text{product}}} = \frac{4.0 \text{ mm}}{0.600 \text{ mm}} = 6.67$$

$$\boxed{RR = 6.67 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Part (b) — Specific Energy Consumption**

Net power:
$$P_{\text{net}} = P_L - P_{nl} = 7.5 - 1.2 = 6.3 \text{ kW}$$

Throughput: $Q = 500$ kg/h $= 0.5$ tonne/h

$$SEC = \frac{P_{\text{net}}}{Q} = \frac{6.3 \text{ kW}}{0.5 \text{ t/h}} = 12.6 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

$$\boxed{SEC = 12.6 \text{ kWh/tonne}}$$

**Part (c) — Theoretical Energy by Rittinger's Law**

Convert sizes to consistent units (micrometers): $D_1 = 4000$ µm; $D_2 = 600$ µm.

$$E_{\text{Ritt}} = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{Ritt}} = 12 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{4000}\right) \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

$$E_{\text{Ritt}} = 12 \cdot \left(0.001667 - 0.000250\right)$$

$$E_{\text{Ritt}} = 12 \cdot 0.001417 = 0.01700 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Ritt}} = 0.017 \text{ kWh/tonne}}$$

**Part (d) — Grinding Efficiency**

$$\eta_g = \frac{E_{\text{th}}}{E_{\text{act}}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.017}{12.6} \times 100\% = 0.135\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_g \approx 0.135\% \approx 0.14\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Notice that the grinding efficiency is **less than 1%** — this is **realistic** and **expected** for most mills! Do not be alarmed. Most input energy is dissipated as heat, sound, friction in bearings, vibration, and material recirculation. A student who calculates η_g = 80% has made a units error.

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Bond's Law with Work Index

### Problem Statement

A ball mill grinds limestone. The Bond Work Index of limestone is $W_i = 11.0$ kWh/tonne. The feed $D_{80} = 2.0$ mm and the product $D_{80} = 200$ µm. If the mill processes 5 tonnes/h, find the theoretical power required.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Convert sizes to µm: $D_1 = 2000$ µm, $D_2 = 200$ µm.

**Step 2:** Apply Bond's Law:
$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

$$E = 11.0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{200}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2000}}\right)$$

$$E = 11.0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{14.142} - \frac{1}{44.721}\right)$$

$$E = 11.0 \cdot (0.07071 - 0.02236)$$

$$E = 11.0 \cdot 0.04835 = 0.5318 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 3:** Power required for $Q = 5$ t/h:
$$P = E \cdot Q = 0.5318 \times 5 = 2.659 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{E = 0.532 \text{ kWh/tonne}; \quad P = 2.66 \text{ kW (theoretical)}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Size Reduction Machines

| Performance Indicator | **Hammer Mill** | **Roller Mill** | **Ball Mill** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Impact + Shear | Compression + Shear | Impact + Attrition |
| **Typical Feed Size** | 5–50 mm | 0.5–10 mm | 0.1–5 mm |
| **Typical Product Size** | 100 µm – 5 mm | 200 µm – 5 mm | 10 – 500 µm |
| **Reduction Ratio** | 10 – 25 | 3 – 6 | 50 – 200 |
| **Capacity (t/h)** | 1 – 50 | 2 – 100 | 0.1 – 10 |
| **SEC (kWh/tonne)** | 5 – 15 | 3 – 8 | 15 – 40 |
| **Heat Generated** | High | Low | Very High |
| **Product Uniformity** | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Best Governing Law** | Rittinger | Kick | Bond |
| **Noise Level (dB)** | 95 – 110 | 75 – 85 | 90 – 100 |
| **Dust Generation** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Suitable for** | Coarse feed | Wheat flour | Spices, pigments |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium | High | Low–Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the Matrix:**
> For a **flour mill**, a roller mill is the *most energy-efficient* (lowest SEC) and gives the *most uniform product*. For a *spice powder*, a hammer mill or ball mill is preferred because the product must be very fine. For a *feed mill*, the hammer mill is the *versatile all-rounder*.

## 3.7 Experimental Methodology for Performance Evaluation (KTU Lab Sequence)

The following procedural sequence is the **expected KTU lab protocol** for Module Experiment 2:

1. **Calibrate** the motor power meter, weigh balance, and stopwatch.
2. **Characterize the feed** — take 1 kg feed, perform sieve analysis, determine $D_{80,\text{feed}}$ and bulk density.
3. **Start the mill** and let it reach steady state (typically 60–90 seconds for hammer mill).
4. **Record no-load power** $P_{nl}$ (with empty hopper) for 60 seconds.
5. **Feed material at constant rate** using a vibratory feeder or manual scoop.
6. **Record loaded power** $P_L$ for the duration $t$ of the run.
7. **Collect and weigh the product** — mass $m_p$.
8. **Determine product size distribution** by sieve analysis — find $D_{80,\text{product}}$.
9. **Calculate** RR, SEC, η_g, and FM.
10. **Repeat** for a second mill and **compare** the performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Size Reduction Performance Evaluation Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Material Selection<br>Maize, Wheat, Spices, Limestone]
    A --> B[Feed Characterization<br>Initial Sieve Analysis, Bulk Density, Moisture]
    B --> C[Machine Selection<br>Hammer Mill, Roller Mill, Ball Mill]
    C --> D[Machine Calibration<br>No-Load Power, Feeder Calibration]
    D --> E[Steady State Operation<br>Loaded Power, Throughput, Time]
    E --> F[Product Collection<br>Mass and Sieve Analysis]
    F --> G[Performance Metrics Calculation]
    G --> G1[Reduction Ratio RR]
    G --> G2[Specific Energy SEC]
    G --> G3[Grinding Efficiency η_g]
    G --> G4[Fineness Modulus FM]
    G1 --> H[Comparative Analysis<br>Machine A vs Machine B]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Final Performance Report<br>Best Machine for Application]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flowchart shows the complete experimental workflow. Solid arrows indicate direct data flow. The output of the performance metrics block is fed into the comparative analysis block, which produces the final decision on machine selection.

## 4.2 Schematic — Forces Acting in Different Size Reduction Machines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Hammer Mill Mechanism]
        F1[High Speed Rotor] -->|Impact Force| P1[Particle]
        P1 -->|Shear Force| S1A[Screen]
    end
    subgraph S2[Roller Mill Mechanism]
        F2[Roller 1] -->|Compressive Force| P2[Particle]
        F2A[Roller 2] -->|Compressive Force| P2
        P2 -->|Shear at Nips| S2A[Discharge]
    end
    subgraph S3[Ball Mill Mechanism]
        F3[Mill Shell Rotation] -->|Cascading Balls| P3[Particle Bed]
        P3 -->|Attrition and Impact| S3A[Fine Discharge]
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Choosing the Right Law of Comminution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Reduction Ratio RR]
    A -->|RR greater than 4<br>Coarse Crushing| B[Apply Kick's Law]
    A -->|RR less than 4<br>Fine Grinding| C[Apply Rittinger's Law]
    A -->|Intermediate RR<br>Ball or Rod Mill| D[Apply Bond's Law]
    B --> E[Energy Calculation<br>E = K_K ln D1 by D2]
    C --> F[Energy Calculation<br>E = K_R 1 by D2 minus 1 by D1]
    D --> G[Energy Calculation<br>E = W_i 1 by sqrt D2 minus 1 by sqrt D1]
    E --> H[Compare with Measured SEC]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Grinding Efficiency η_g]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the Mermaid diagrams:** The hexagonal boxes are decision nodes, rectangles are process nodes. Arrows indicate the logical sequence of operations. In KTU lab report drawings, students may replace these with labeled line diagrams of actual equipment.

    A --> A4[FM_A]
    B[Machine B Performance] --> B1[RR_B]
    B --> B2[SEC_B]
    B --> B3[η_g_B]
    B --> B4[FM_B]
    A1 --> C{Normalize All Metrics<br>on 0 to 100 Scale}
    A2 --> C
    A3 --> C
    A4 --> C
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
