# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Reduction Ratio" in size reduction. A hammer mill is fed with maize having $D_{80} = 5$ mm and produces a product with $D_{80} = 800$ µm. Calculate the reduction ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reduction Ratio (RR)** is defined as the ratio of the **80% passing size of the feed** ($D_{80,\text{feed}}$) to the **80% passing size of the product** ($D_{80,\text{product}}$). It is a **dimensionless** number that quantifies the extent of size reduction in a single pass.

$$RR = \frac{D_{80,\text{feed}}}{D_{80,\text{product}}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$RR = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{0.800 \text{ mm}} = 6.25$$

$$\boxed{RR = 6.25}$$

This means each particle is, on average, **6.25 times smaller** in diameter after passing through the hammer mill.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Rittinger's Law of size reduction. Mention its area of application and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Rittinger's Law (1867)** states that the **energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the new surface area created** during the operation. Mathematically:

$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

where $E$ is the specific energy (kWh/tonne), $K_R$ is Rittinger's constant, $D_1$ is the initial particle size, and $D_2$ is the final particle size.

**Area of Application:** Rittinger's law is most accurate for **fine grinding operations** (product size < 200 µm), such as ball milling of spices, pigments, and pharmaceutical powders.

**Limitation:** It **overestimates the energy** required for coarse crushing and is not applicable when the feed size is large, because the energy in coarse crushing is dominated by **bulk deformation** rather than surface creation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Comprehensive Performance Evaluation Problem **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A feed mill evaluates **two hammer mills** (Mill A and Mill B) for grinding soybean meal. The following experimental data were recorded:

| Parameter | Mill A | Mill B |
|---|---|---|
| Feed $D_{80}$ | 3.5 mm | 3.5 mm |
| Product $D_{80}$ | 450 µm | 700 µm |
| Throughput (kg/h) | 800 | 1200 |
| No-load power (kW) | 1.5 | 1.8 |
| Loaded power (kW) | 9.2 | 11.0 |

**Assume Bond's Work Index for soybean = 8.5 kWh/tonne.**

#### Part (a) — Calculate the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Reduction Ratio (RR) for **both mills**. **Which mill has a higher RR? Which has lower SEC? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mill A:**

Reduction Ratio:
$$RR_A = \frac{D_{80,\text{feed}}}{D_{80,\text{product}}} = \frac{3500 \text{ µm}}{450 \text{ µm}} = 7.78$$

Net power:
$$P_{\text{net},A} = P_L - P_{nl} = 9.2 - 1.5 = 7.7 \text{ kW}$$

Throughput: $Q_A = 800$ kg/h $= 0.8$ tonne/h.

$$SEC_A = \frac{P_{\text{net},A}}{Q_A} = \frac{7.7 \text{ kW}}{0.8 \text{ t/h}} = 9.625 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Mill B:**

Reduction Ratio:
$$RR_B = \frac{3500 \text{ µm}}{700 \text{ µm}} = 5.00$$

Net power:
$$P_{\text{net},B} = 11.0 - 1.8 = 9.2 \text{ kW}$$

Throughput: $Q_B = 1200$ kg/h $= 1.2$ tonne/h.

$$SEC_B = \frac{9.2 \text{ kW}}{1.2 \text{ t/h}} = 7.667 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\boxed{RR_A = 7.78 > RR_B = 5.00} \quad \text{(Mill A gives finer product)}$$

$$\boxed{SEC_B = 7.67 < SEC_A = 9.63 \text{ kWh/tonne}} \quad \text{(Mill B is more energy efficient)}$$

#### Part (b) — Calculate the **theoretical energy** required by **Bond's Law** for the size reduction achieved by Mill A. Compare with its actual SEC and compute the **grinding efficiency**. **Comment on the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Apply Bond's Law with $D_1 = 3500$ µm and $D_2 = 450$ µm.

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = 8.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{450}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3500}}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = 8.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{21.213} - \frac{1}{59.161}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = 8.5 \cdot (0.04714 - 0.01690)$$

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = 8.5 \cdot 0.03024$$

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = 0.2570 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 2:** Actual SEC for Mill A = 9.625 kWh/tonne.

**Step 3:** Grinding efficiency:

$$\eta_g = \frac{E_{\text{Bond}}}{SEC_A} \times 100\% = \frac{0.2570}{9.625} \times 100\% = 2.67\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_g = 2.67\%}$$

**Comment:** The grinding efficiency of only **2.67%** indicates that more than **97% of the input energy is wasted** as heat, noise, vibration, friction, and material recirculation. This is a typical real-world result for hammer mills. Improvements can be made by optimizing screen size, hammer tip speed, and feed moisture to push η_g toward 5–10%. **Mill B** has lower SEC (7.67 kWh/tonne) but produces a coarser product — the choice depends on whether **fineness or energy cost** is the priority.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract no-load power** from loaded power is the most common error — it inflates SEC by 15–25%. Always do $P_{\text{net}} = P_L - P_{nl}$.
> 2. **Inconsistent units** — Bond's Law works only when $D$ is in the same units on both sides (micrometers is safest). Mixing mm and µm leads to wrong answers.
> 3. **Forgetting throughput conversion** — $Q$ must be in **tonne/h** to give SEC in **kWh/tonne**. If $Q$ is in kg/h, divide by 1000.
> 4. **Assuming η_g will be high** — it will be 1–5% for most real mills. Do not doubt your calculation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Conceptual and Design-Oriented Question **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Compare and contrast the three laws of size reduction — Kick's, Rittinger's, and Bond's — with respect to their underlying assumptions, mathematical forms, and range of applicability. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Kick's Law (1885)** | **Rittinger's Law (1867)** | **Bond's Law (1952)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Originator** | Kick | Rittinger | Bond |
| **Assumption** | Energy ∝ volumetric strain | Energy ∝ new surface area | Energy is geometric mean of Kick & Rittinger |
| **Mathematical Form** | $E = K_K \cdot \ln(D_1/D_2)$ | $E = K_R \cdot (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ | $E = W_i \cdot (1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$ |
| **Underlying Physics** | Bulk deformation / fracture initiation | Molecular bond breaking at new surface | Empirical compromise; crack propagation |
| **Best for** | **Coarse crushing** (RR > 4, D > 1 mm) | **Fine grinding** (D < 200 µm) | **Intermediate grinding** (most common, ball/rod mills) |
| **Constant** | $K_K$ (Kick constant) | $K_R$ (Rittinger constant) | $W_i$ (Work Index, kWh/tonne) |
| **Limitation** | Fails for fine sizes | Fails for coarse sizes | Empirical; needs experimental $W_i$ |
| **Industrial Use** | Rarely used directly | Used in fine-grinding calculations | **Industry standard** for mill sizing |

**Summary statement:** Kick's law dominates at large particle sizes where deformation is bulk; Rittinger's dominates at small sizes where new surface creation matters most; Bond's law is the practical middle ground used by over 80% of industrial mill designers.

---

**(b) A ball mill with a Bond Work Index requirement of 10 kWh/tonne is to grind a feed of $D_{80} = 2.5$ mm to a product of $D_{80} = 150$ µm at a rate of 8 t/h. Calculate (i) the power required, and (ii) the expected grinding efficiency if the motor draws 35 kW under load. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical Energy (Bond's Law):**

$$E_{\text{th}} = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

$D_1 = 2500$ µm, $D_2 = 150$ µm.

$$E_{\text{th}} = 10 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{150}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2500}}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{th}} = 10 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{12.247} - \frac{1}{50.000}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{th}} = 10 \cdot (0.08165 - 0.02000)$$

$$E_{\text{th}} = 10 \cdot 0.06165 = 0.6165 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Power Required:**

$$P_{\text{th}} = E_{\text{th}} \cdot Q = 0.6165 \text{ kWh/t} \times 8 \text{ t/h} = 4.93 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{th}} \approx 4.93 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Energy per Tonne (Actual SEC):**

$$SEC_{\text{act}} = \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{Q} = \frac{35 \text{ kW}}{8 \text{ t/h}} = 4.375 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 4 — Grinding Efficiency:**

$$\eta_g = \frac{E_{\text{th}}}{SEC_{\text{act}}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.6165}{4.375} \times 100\% = 14.09\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_g \approx 14.1\%}$$

**Comment:** A grinding efficiency of **14%** is in the typical range for an industrial ball mill. The remaining **86%** of the power is dissipated as heat, sound, friction in bearings, and other losses. Improvements can be made by optimizing ball size distribution, slurry density (for wet grinding), and mill speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls on Bond's Law Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg** — Bond's constant $W_i$ is always in **kWh/tonne**, so mass must be in tonnes (or kWh/kg with adjusted $W_i$).
> 2. **Using loaded power directly** — If no-load power is given, **subtract it first** before computing SEC. Here it was not given separately, so loaded = net.
> 3. **Mixing up $D_1$ and $D_2$** — $D_1$ is always the **larger (feed)** size; $D_2$ is the **smaller (product)** size. Reversing them gives a **negative** energy.
> 4. **Misinterpreting grinding efficiency** — η_g is *theoretical/actual*, NOT *actual/theoretical*. Many students invert this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Read This Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Reduction Ratio (RR)** = $D_{80,\text{feed}} / D_{80,\text{product}}$; high RR = aggressive size reduction.
- ✅ **Kick's Law** → coarse crushing (RR > 4); energy ∝ $\ln(D_1/D_2)$.
- ✅ **Rittinger's Law** → fine grinding ($D_2 < 200$ µm); energy ∝ $(1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$.
- ✅ **Bond's Law** → intermediate / industrial standard; energy ∝ $(1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$.
- ✅ **Work Index $W_i$** = kWh needed to grind 1 tonne from infinite size to 80% passing 100 µm.
- ✅ **Net Power** = $P_L - P_{nl}$ (always subtract no-load!).
- ✅ **SEC** = $P_{\text{net}} / Q$ in kWh/tonne; **Q must be in tonnes/h**.
- ✅ **Grinding Efficiency** = $(E_{\text{theoretical}} / E_{\text{actual}}) \times 100\%$; expect 1–15% in practice.
- ✅ **Fineness Modulus (FM)** = sum of cumulative % retained on standard sieves ÷ 100; higher FM = coarser.
- ✅ **Sieve analysis** uses standard sieves: 4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, 150 µm, 75 µm.
- ✅ **$D_{80}$** is the size at which 80% of the material passes — the standard size descriptor in Bond's law.
- ✅ **Hammer mill** = best all-rounder; **Roller mill** = most energy-efficient for flour; **Ball mill** = finest product.
- ✅ **Specific Surface** for a sphere = $6 / (\rho_p D)$ — useful for Rittinger derivations.
- ✅ **Energy efficiency is always low** (< 20%) because most energy becomes heat and noise — this is **not** a calculation error.
- ✅ **Units check** — always make $D$ in same units (µm recommended) and $Q$ in tonne/h for SEC in kWh/tonne.
- ✅ **Always remember** to convert mm to µm (× 1000) when using Bond's or Rittinger's equations.

> **Final Note:** A student who masters the **five performance metrics (RR, SEC, η_g, FM, Capacity)**, the **three comminution laws (Kick, Rittinger, Bond)**, and the **work index concept** will comfortably score full marks on any KTU question in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Fineness Modulus and Uniformity Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of Fineness Modulus and Uniformity Index # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fineness Modulus (FM) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition** > **Fineness Modulus (FM)** is an empirical, dimensionless numerical index that quantifies the average particle size of a granular agricultural or food material. It is mathematically defined as the **sum of cumulative percentages of the sample retained on a specified series of standard sieves, divided by 100**. For fine-grained agricultural materials (ground grains, cattle feed, flour, mash, etc.), the standard sieve series used is: **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, and 150 µm**. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Average" Idea** Imagine you are a teacher with a class of 100 students and you want *one single number* that summarizes how "tall" the class is. Rather than listing 100 heights, you find the average. **Fineness Modulus does the same thing for a pile of powder or crushed grain.** Instead of describing a feed by saying "30 % is coarse, 25 % is medium, 45 % is fine…", a single number (say, FM = 2.85) tells the miller, the feed formulator, or the engineer the **overall coarseness** of the product in one shot. A **higher FM means a coarser product**, a **lower FM means a finer product** — just like a higher average height means a class of older/taller students. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Quick Facts** > - Typical FM range for **fine agricultural powders / feed meals**: **2.20 – 3.20** > - FM has **no units** (it is a pure ratio). > - The **pan (< 150 µm)** fraction is **NOT** included when summing cumulative percentages for FM. > - A change in FM of **0.20** is generally considered practically significant for feed milling. --- ## 1.2 Uniformity Index (UI) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition** > The **Uniformity Index (UI)**, more rigorously called the **Coefficient of Uniformity (Cᵤ)**, is a dimensionless shape parameter of the particle-size distribution curve. It is defined as the **ratio of the particle diameter corresponding to 60 % cumulative finer (D₆₀) to the particle diameter corresponding to 10 % cumulative finer (D₁₀)**. $$ UI \;=\; C_u \;=\; \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} $$ **Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Spread" Idea** Suppose two highways carry 100 cars each, and we want one number to express how *spread out* the cars are by size. We measure the size below which 10 % of cars fall (very small cars) and the size below which 60 % of cars fall (medium cars). If these two sizes are nearly the same, all cars are similar in size (uniform). If the 60 %-size is much larger than the 10 %-size, the cars are wildly varied. **The ratio D₆₀ / D₁₀ is exactly that "spread" indicator for ground grain.** A UI close to **1.0 → uniform particles**. A UI of **4.0–6.0 → very wide size range** (poorly graded material). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Quick Facts** > - UI is **dimensionless**. > - For uniform feed particles (e.g., crumbled poultry feed), UI ≈ **1.0 – 1.5** is desired. > - For cattle mash or soil, UI = **4 – 6** is acceptable. > - The complementary parameter **Coefficient of Curvature, C_c = (D₃₀)² / (D₁₀ · D₆₀)** is used alongside UI for complete gradation classification (well-graded criteria: Cᵤ ≥ 4 for sands, **1 ≤ C_c ≤ 3**). --- ## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in Agricultural Processing > [!TIP] > **Industrial Relevance at a Glance** > - **Feed Mill Quality Control:** Uniform particle size ensures consistent pellet durability and animal feed intake. > - **Flour Milling:** FM dictates the type of end product (atta, maida, suji). > - **Grain Drying & Aeration:** Uniformity index predicts airflow resistance through grain beds. > - **Storage & Flow:** High UI (poor uniformity) leads to segregation, bridging in silos, and uneven moisture migration. > - **Standards:** IS 1607, ASAE S319.4, and BIS specifications all reference FM/UI for processed agricultural products. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Principle of Sieve Analysis The experiment rests on the principle of **mechanical separation by aperture size**. A representative sample is placed on the topmost (largest aperture) sieve of a stacked column, and the entire stack is agitated mechanically. Particles smaller than each sieve aperture pass through; particles larger are retained. After a fixed shaking duration (typically **10 – 15 minutes** on a **rotap sieve shaker** at ~**280 rpm with ~**38 mm stroke**), the mass retained on each sieve is measured gravimetrically. ### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning 1. **Sampling:** Obtain a representative sample by the **coning-and-quartering method** or by a **riffle sampler** to avoid bias. The minimum mass recommended for fine agricultural powders is **100 g** (or per IS 2720-Part 4). 2. **Oven Drying:** Dry the sample to constant mass in a hot-air oven at **110 °C ± 5 °C** to remove free moisture that would otherwise cause clumping and clog sieves. 3. **Sieve Stacking:** Arrange sieves in **descending order of aperture** from top to bottom. Add a **lid** on top and a **receiving pan** at the bottom. 4. **Sieving:** Place the stack in the mechanical sieve shaker and run for the prescribed time. 5. **Weighing:** Carefully remove each sieve, tap gently, and weigh the **retained mass (Wᵢ)** on each sieve to the nearest **0.1 g**. 6. **Mass Balance Check:** Verify that the sum of all retained masses + pan loss is within **± 1 %** of the original sample mass. If not, repeat the test. 7. **Computation:** Calculate cumulative percentages and plot the **grain size distribution curve** (% passing vs log of sieve size). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbols / Units | Application | |---|---------|-----------------|-------------| | 1 | $\%\, Retained_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{W_i}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | $W_i$ = mass retained on sieve *i* (g); $W_{total}$ = total sample mass (g) | Per-sieve retention | | 2 | $\%\, Cumulative\,Retained_{i} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{i} \%\, Retained_{j}$ | Dimensionless (%) | Running total of retention | | 3 | $\%\, Passing_{i} \;=\; 100 - \%\,Cumulative\,Retained_{i}$ | Dimensionless (%) | Finer fraction | | 4 | $\boxed{\,FM \;=\; \dfrac{\sum\, \%\,Cumulative\,Retained}{100}\,}$ | Sum taken over **all standard sieves EXCEPT the pan**; FM is dimensionless | Overall fineness | | 5 | $\boxed{\,UI \;=\; C_u \;=\; \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}\,}$ | $D_{60}, D_{10}$ in mm or µm (must be consistent) | Particle-size spread | | 6 | $C_c \;=\; \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$ | Dimensionless | Gradation shape (well-graded check) | | 7 | $\log D_x \;=\; \log D_{a} + \dfrac{(\%\,Pass_{a} - x)}{(\%\,Pass_{a} - \%\,Pass_{b})} \times \log\!\left(\dfrac{D_{b}}{D_{a}}\right)$ | Log-interpolation between two adjacent sieves *a* (coarser) and *b* (finer) | Finding D₁₀, D₃₀, D₆₀ | --- ## 2.3 Choice of Sieve Series — The "Why" The standard sieve numbers follow a **geometric progression** with a constant ratio of √2 ≈ 1.414 (or ⁴√2 ≈ 1.189 for half-size). This logarithmic spacing is essential because **particle sizes in nature and in grinding follow a log-normal distribution**, not a linear one. A linear series of sieves would cluster too many openings in the small-size range and miss important intermediate sizes. Hence, **plotting % passing vs log (sieve size)** produces the familiar **semi-log S-curve** of grain size distribution. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where FM and UI Are Used in the Real World > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Applications** > 1. **Animal Feed Manufacturing** — Poultry birds prefer uniform particles (UI ≈ 1.0) for uniform intake; cattle accept wider range (UI ≈ 3–5). > 2. **Flour Mills** — Atta (FM ≈ 2.7), Maida (FM ≈ 2.2), Sooji/Rava (FM ≈ 3.0+) require different FM targets. > 3. **Pellet & Extrusion Industry** — Lower FM + lower UI gives denser pellets; higher FM improves durability but reduces digestibility. > 4. **Soil Mechanics in Agricultural Fields** — UI classifies tillage soils as well-graded or poorly-graded, governing water retention and root penetration. > 5. **Silo & Bin Design** — High UI (poor uniformity) causes fine particles to migrate to the center during filling, leading to **rat-holing** and flow problems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Worked Example & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Worked-Out Numerical Example **Aim:** Determine the Fineness Modulus and Uniformity Index of a ground maize (corn) sample. **Given:** Oven-dry sample mass $W_{total} = 500\text{ g}$ sieved through the standard series. The mass retained on each sieve is tabulated below. ### 3.1.1 Observation Table (Raw Data) | Sieve Aperture (mm) | Mass Retained, $W_i$ (g) | |---|---| | 4.75 | 0 | | 2.36 | 5 | | 1.18 | 35 | | 0.600 | 165 | | 0.300 | 195 | | 0.150 | 75 | | 0.075 | 20 | | Pan (< 0.075) | 5 | | **Total** | **500** | **Mass Balance Check:** $\sum W_i = 0 + 5 + 35 + 165 + 195 + 75 + 20 + 5 = 500\text{ g}$ ✅ (matches input mass → valid test). --- ### 3.1.2 Derivation of % Retained, Cumulative % Retained, and % Passing For sieve with aperture $d_i$ and retained mass $W_i$: $$ \%\,Retained_i \;=\; \frac{W_i}{W_{total}} \times 100 $$ For the **2.36 mm** sieve: $$ \%\,Retained_{2.36} \;=\; \frac{5}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 1.00\,\% $$ For the **1.18 mm** sieve: $$ \%\,Retained_{1.18} \;=\; \frac{35}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 7.00\,\% $$ For the **0.600 mm** sieve: $$ \%\,Retained_{0.600} \;=\; \frac{165}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 33.00\,\% $$ For the **0.300 mm** sieve: $$ \%\,Retained_{0.300} \;=\; \frac{195}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 39.00\,\% $$ For the **0.150 mm** sieve: $$ \%\,Retained_{0.150} \;=\; \frac{75}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 15.00\,\% $$ For the **0.075 mm** sieve: $$ \%\,Retained_{0.075} \;=\; \frac{20}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 4.00\,\% $$ For the **Pan**: $$ \%\,Retained_{Pan} \;=\; \frac{5}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 1.00\,\% $$ **Verification of % Retained Sum:** $$ \sum \%\,Retained \;=\; 0 + 1.00 + 7.00 + 33.00 + 39.00 + 15.00 + 4.00 + 1.00 \;=\; 100.00\,\% \;\checkmark $$ **Now build the cumulative table:** Cumulative % Retained is the running sum of % Retained from the largest sieve downward: | Sieve (mm) | $W_i$ (g) | % Retained | Cumulative % Retained | % Passing (100 − Cum % Ret) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 4.75 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 100.00 | | 2.36 | 5 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 99.00 | | 1.18 | 35 | 7.00 | 8.00 | 92.00 | | 0.600 | 165 | 33.00 | 41.00 | 59.00 | | 0.300 | 195 | 39.00 | 80.00 | 20.00 | | 0.150 | 75 | 15.00 | 95.00 | 5.00 | | 0.075 | 20 | 4.00 | 99.00 | 1.00 | | Pan | 5 | 1.00 | 100.00 | 0.00 | --- ### 3.1.3 Calculation of Fineness Modulus (FM) By definition, the **pan is excluded** from the FM sum. Using the **standard six-sieve series** (4.75, 2.36, 1.18, 0.600, 0.300, 0.150 mm): $$ FM \;=\; \frac{\sum_{standard\,sieves} \%\,Cum\,Retained}{100} $$ $$ FM \;=\; \frac{0.00 + 1.00 + 8.00 + 41.00 + 80.00 + 95.00}{100} $$ $$ FM \;=\; \frac{225.00}{100} \;=\; 2.25 $$ **Interpretation:** FM = **2.25** falls at the **lower end of the fine-aggregate range (2.20 – 3.20)**. The ground maize is therefore classified as a **fine product**, suitable for **young poultry feed, fish feed, or fine flour** rather than cattle mash. > [!NOTE] > **Alternative — IS Fine Sieve Series (4.75, 2.36, 1.18, 0.600, 0.300, 0.150):** Always check which sieve series the KTU question specifies. If only four sieves (2.36, 1.18, 0.600, 0.300) are used, the denominator of the sum is 400 instead of 100's individual values; the FM is then **Sum / 100** of those specific cumulative % retentions. The result is always the *same numerical value* when using a standard consistent series. --- ### 3.1.4 Calculation of Uniformity Index (UI = Cᵤ) We need $D_{10}$ and $D_{60}$ — particle diameters corresponding to **10 % passing** and **60 % passing**, respectively. These are read off the grain-size distribution curve. Since the percentages fall between sieve intervals, we use **logarithmic interpolation**. **General interpolation formula** (between two consecutive sieves $a$ = coarser and $b$ = finer): $$ \log D_x \;=\; \log D_a + \left[\frac{(\%\,Pass)_a - x}{(\%\,Pass)_a - (\%\,Pass)_b}\right] \times \log\!\left(\frac{D_b}{D_a}\right) $$ where $x$ is the target % passing (10, 30, or 60). --- #### Step A — Finding D₁₀ (10 % passing) Look at the data table: 10 % passing lies **between the 1.18 mm sieve (92 % passing) and the 0.600 mm sieve (59 % passing)**. So $D_a = 1.18\text{ mm},\ (\%\,Pass)_a = 92$; $D_b = 0.600\text{ mm},\ (\%\,Pass)_b = 59$; $x = 10$. $$ \log D_{10} \;=\; \log(1.18) + \left[\frac{92 - 10}{92 - 59}\right] \times \log\!\left(\frac{0.600}{1.18}\right) $$ $$ \log(1.18) \;=\; 0.07188 \qquad \log\!\left(\frac{0.600}{1.18}\right) \;=\; \log(0.5085) \;=\; -0.29367 $$ $$ \frac{92 - 10}{92 - 59} \;=\; \frac{82}{33} \;=\; 2.4848 $$ $$ \log D_{10} \;=\; 0.07188 + 2.4848 \times (-0.29367) $$ $$ \log D_{10} \;=\; 0.07188 - 0.72975 $$ $$ \log D_{10} \;=\; -0.65787 $$ $$ \boxed{D_{10} \;=\; 10^{-0.65787} \;\approx\; 0.2198\text{ mm} \;\approx\; 219.8\,\mu m} $$ --- #### Step B — Finding D₆₀ (60 % passing) 60 % passing lies **between the 0.600 mm sieve (59 % passing) and the 0.300 mm sieve (20 % passing)**. So $D_a = 0.600\text{ mm},\ (\%\,Pass)_a = 59$; $D_b = 0.300\text{ mm},\ (\%\,Pass)_b = 20$; $x = 60$. $$ \log D_{60} \;=\; \log(0.600) + \left[\frac{59 - 60}{59 - 20}\right] \times \log\!\left(\frac{0.300}{0.600}\right) $$ $$ \log(0.600) \;=\; -0.22185 \qquad \log\!\left(\frac{0.300}{0.600}\right) \;=\; \log(0.500) \;=\; -0.30103 $$ $$ \frac{59 - 60}{59 - 20} \;=\; \frac{-1}{39} \;=\; -0.02564 $$ $$ \log D_{60} \;=\; -0.22185 + (-0.02564) \times (-0.30103) $$ $$ \log D_{60} \;=\; -0.22185 + 0.00772 $$ $$ \log D_{60} \;=\; -0.21413 $$ $$ \boxed{D_{60} \;=\; 10^{-0.21413} \;\approx\; 0.6108\text{ mm} \;\approx\; 610.8\,\mu m} $$ --- #### Step C — Compute UI $$ UI \;=\; \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} $$ $$ UI \;=\; \frac{0.6108}{0.2198} $$ $$ \boxed{UI \;\approx\; 2.78} $$ --- #### Optional — Coefficient of Curvature C_c (for full gradation classification) Find $D_{30}$ (30 % passing). 30 % passing lies between 0.600 mm (59 % passing) and 0.300 mm (20 % passing). $$ \log D_{30} \;=\; \log(0.600) + \left[\frac{59 - 30}{59 - 20}\right] \times \log(0.500) $$ $$ \log D_{30} \;=\; -0.22185 + \left(\frac{29}{39}\right) \times (-0.30103) $$ $$ \log D_{30} \;=\; -0.22185 + 0.7436 \times (-0.30103) $$ $$ \log D_{30} \;=\; -0.22185 - 0.22386 $$ $$ \log D_{30} \;=\; -0.44571 $$ $$ D_{30} \;\approx\; 10^{-0.44571} \;\approx\; 0.3586\text{ mm} $$ $$ C_c \;=\; \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} \;=\; \frac{(0.3586)^2}{0.2198 \times 0.6108} $$ $$ C_c \;=\; \frac{0.1286}{0.1343} \;\approx\; 0.96 $$ Since **1 ≤ C_c ≤ 3** is **NOT satisfied** (C_c = 0.96 < 1), the material is **poorly graded** even though the UI is moderate. --- ## 3.2 Summary of Results | Parameter | Value | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **Fineness Modulus (FM)** | **2.25** | Fine-textured product (lower end of fine range) | | **D₁₀** | 0.220 mm | Effective minimum size | | **D₃₀** | 0.359 mm | Median control size | | **D₆₀** | 0.611 mm | Effective maximum size | | **Uniformity Index (UI)** | **2.78** | Moderately graded, not uniform | | **Coefficient of Curvature (C_c)** | 0.96 | Slightly deficient in intermediate sizes | --- ## 3.3 The Grain Size Distribution Curve — Construction Logic The semi-log plot uses: - **X-axis (log scale):** Sieve aperture in mm. - **Y-axis (linear scale):** % Passing (0 to 100). - **Plotted points:** $(d_i, \,\%\,Pass_i)$ for each sieve. - **Smooth S-curve** is drawn connecting the points. Reading the curve: - **D₁₀** is the X-coordinate where the curve crosses the 10 % horizontal line. - **D₃₀** is the X-coordinate at 30 %. - **D₆₀** is the X-coordinate at 60 %. This curve is the **single most important graphical output** of the experiment and must always be included in the KTU lab record. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Chart — Complete Experimental Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start Experiment]) --> s1[Step 1: Collect Representative Sample] s1 --> s2[Step 2: Oven Dry at 110 C for 24 hr] s2 --> s3[Step 3: Record Initial Mass Wtotal] s3 --> s4[Step 4: Assemble Sieve Stack
4.75 to 0.075 mm with Pan Below] s4 --> s5[Step 5: Place Sample on Top Sieve] s5 --> s6[Step 6: Clamp Stack in Mechanical Sieve Shaker] s6 --> s7[Step 7: Shake for 10 to 15 minutes] s7 --> s8[Step 8: Disassemble Stack Carefully] s8 --> s9[Step 9: Weigh Mass Retained on Each Sieve to 0.1 g] s9 --> s10[Step 10: Mass Balance Check
Sum of Wi within 1 percent of Wtotal] s10 --> s11{Within Tolerance?} s11 -- No --> s12[Repeat Test with Fresh Sample] s12 --> s1 s11 -- Yes --> s13[Step 11: Calculate Percent Retained and Cumulative Percent] s13 --> s14[Step 12: Compute Fineness Modulus FM] s14 --> s15[Step 13: Compute Percent Passing at Each Sieve] s15 --> s16[Step 14: Plot Semi Log Grain Size Curve] s16 --> s17[Step 15: Read D10 D30 D60 from Curve] s17 --> s18[Step 16: Compute UI equals D60 over D10] s18 --> s19[Step 17: Interpret Results and Compare with Standards] s19 --> endB([End and Report]) classDef decisionBox fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc7a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processBox fill:#b4d8f0,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 classDef terminalBox fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class s11 decisionBox class s1,s2,s3,s4,s5,s6,s7,s8,s9,s10,s12,s13,s14,s15,s16,s17,s18,s19 processBox class startA,endB terminalBox ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Apparatus Setup Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph shakerUnit["MECHANICAL SIEVE SHAKER UNIT"] motor[Electric Motor
280 rpm] --> eccentric[Eccentric Cam
38 mm stroke] eccentric --> platform[Sieve Platform Holder] end subgraph sieveStack["SIEVE COLUMN ASSEMBLY - TOP TO BOTTOM"] lid[Top Lid] --> sieve1[Sieve 4.75 mm] sieve1 --> sieve2[Sieve 2.36 mm] sieve2 --> sieve3[Sieve 1.18 mm] sieve3 --> sieve4[Sieve 0.600 mm] sieve4 --> sieve5[Sieve 0.300 mm] sieve5 --> sieve6[Sieve 0.150 mm] sieve6 --> sieve7[Sieve 0.075 mm] sieve7 --> pan[Receiving Pan] end platform -.holds.-> sieveStack sample[Ground Maize Sample
500 g oven dry] --> lid subgraph balanceUnit["MEASURING AND ANALYSIS STATION"] balance[Electronic Balance
Accuracy 0.1 g] oven[Hot Air Oven
110 C] plotter[Grain Size Curve
Semi Log Graph Paper] end sieveStack --> balance sample --> oven balance --> plotter classDef shakerGroup fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#a0522d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sieveGroup fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef balanceGroup fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef materialItem fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 class motor,eccentric,platform shakerGroup class lid,sieve1,sieve2,sieve3,sieve4,sieve5,sieve6,sieve7,pan sieveGroup class balance,oven,plotter balanceGroup class sample materialItem ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Description — Sieve Stack Order (For Lab Record Drawing) ``` ┌──────────────┐ │ TOP LID │ ← Prevents sample loss during shaking ├──────────────┤ │ 4.75 mm │ ← Sieve #1 (Largest aperture) ├──────────────┤ │ 2.36 mm │ ← Sieve #2 ├──────────────┤ │ 1.18 mm │ ← Sieve #3 ├──────────────┤ │ 0.600 mm │ ← Sieve #4 (600 µm) ├──────────────┤ │ 0.300 mm │ ← Sieve #5 (300 µm) ├──────────────┤ │ 0.150 mm │ ← Sieve #6 (150 µm) ├──────────────┤ │ 0.075 mm │ ← Sieve #7 (75 µm) — optional ├──────────────┤ │ RECEIVING PAN│ ← Collects all fines passing 75 µm └──────────────┘ ▼ MECHANICAL SHAKER BASE ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** > **Q1. Define Fineness Modulus. State its significance in agricultural feed processing.** **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Fineness Modulus (FM) is a dimensionless numerical index that indicates the overall coarseness or fineness of a granular material. It is calculated as the sum of the cumulative percentages of the sample retained on a standard series of sieves, divided by 100. *Mathematically:* $FM = \sum(\%\,Cum.\,Retained) / 100$ **[1 mark]**. The pan fraction is excluded from this sum. **[0.5 mark]** **Significance in agricultural feed processing:** **[1.5 marks]** - Controls **pellet quality and durability** in feed mills. - Determines **digestibility** — finer particles (lower FM) are more digestible for young animals. - Used as a **quality control parameter** to ensure batch-to-batch consistency of ground grain, mash, and flour. - Standard reference ranges: Fine feed 2.2 – 2.6; Medium 2.6 – 2.9; Coarse > 2.9. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** > **Q2. What is the Uniformity Index? Explain what UI = 1.5 implies about the particle size distribution.** **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The Uniformity Index (UI), also called the Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$), is defined as the ratio of the particle diameter at 60 % finer ($D_{60}$) to that at 10 % finer ($D_{10}$). **[1 mark]** $$ UI = C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} \quad \text{[0.5 mark]} $$ **Interpretation of UI = 1.5:** **[1.5 marks]** - Since the ideal uniform material has UI = 1.0 (all particles the same size), a value of **1.5 is very close to uniform**. - It indicates a **narrowly graded, well-controlled grinding operation** — typical of crumbled or pelleted poultry feed. - Such material exhibits **predictable flow behavior, minimal segregation in silos, and uniform animal intake**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice Pattern]** > **Q3. The following data were obtained from a sieve analysis of a ground rice sample (initial oven-dry mass = 400 g). Determine the Fineness Modulus and the Uniformity Index. Comment on the gradation.** | Sieve (mm) | Mass Retained (g) | |---|---| | 4.75 | 0 | | 2.36 | 8 | | 1.18 | 52 | | 0.600 | 140 | | 0.300 | 120 | | 0.150 | 50 | | 0.075 | 20 | | Pan | 10 | --- ### **OPTION A — Alternative Selection (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compute the Fineness Modulus and tabulate the grain-size distribution.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Mass balance check** (1 mark) $$ \sum W_i = 0+8+52+140+120+50+20+10 = 400\text{ g} \;=\; W_{total}\;\checkmark $$ **Step 2: Calculate % Retained** (2 marks) | Sieve (mm) | $W_i$ (g) | % Retained = $W_i / 400 \times 100$ | |---|---|---| | 4.75 | 0 | 0.00 | | 2.36 | 8 | 2.00 | | 1.18 | 52 | 13.00 | | 0.600 | 140 | 35.00 | | 0.300 | 120 | 30.00 | | 0.150 | 50 | 12.50 | | 0.075 | 20 | 5.00 | | Pan | 10 | 2.50 | **Step 3: Calculate Cumulative % Retained and % Passing** (2 marks) | Sieve (mm) | Cum % Ret | % Passing | |---|---|---| | 4.75 | 0.00 | 100.00 | | 2.36 | 2.00 | 98.00 | | 1.18 | 15.00 | 85.00 | | 0.600 | 50.00 | 50.00 | | 0.300 | 80.00 | 20.00 | | 0.150 | 92.50 | 7.50 | | 0.075 | 97.50 | 2.50 | | Pan | 100.00 | 0.00 | **Step 4: Fineness Modulus** (2 marks) $$ FM = \frac{0.00+2.00+15.00+50.00+80.00+92.50}{100} = \frac{239.50}{100} = \mathbf{2.40} $$ **Comment:** FM = 2.40 indicates a **fine product**, suitable for young-animal feed or fine flour. --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Determine $D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$ and compute UI and $C_c$.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Find $D_{10}$ (10 % passing)** — 10 % lies between 1.18 mm (85 % passing) and 0.600 mm (50 % passing). (1 mark for setup) $$ \log D_{10} = \log(1.18) + \frac{85-10}{85-50} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{0.600}{1.18}\right) $$ $$ \log D_{10} = 0.0719 + \frac{75}{35} \cdot (-0.2937) = 0.0719 - 0.6294 = -0.5575 $$ $$ D_{10} = 10^{-0.5575} \approx 0.277\text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark for calculation)} $$ **Step 2: Find $D_{30}$ (30 % passing)** — 30 % lies between 0.600 mm (50 % passing) and 0.300 mm (20 % passing). $$ \log D_{30} = \log(0.600) + \frac{50-30}{50-20} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{0.300}{0.600}\right) $$ $$ \log D_{30} = -0.2218 + \frac{20}{30} \cdot (-0.3010) = -0.2218 - 0.2007 = -0.4225 $$ $$ D_{30} = 10^{-0.4225} \approx 0.378\text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ **Step 3: Find $D_{60}$ (60 % passing)** — 60 % lies between 0.600 mm (50 % passing) and 0.300 mm (20 % passing). $$ \log D_{60} = \log(0.600) + \frac{50-60}{50-20} \cdot \log(0.500) $$ $$ \log D_{60} = -0.2218 + \frac{-10}{30} \cdot (-0.3010) = -0.2218 + 0.1003 = -0.1215 $$ $$ D_{60} = 10^{-0.1215} \approx 0.756\text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ **Step 4: Uniformity Index** (1.5 marks) $$ UI = C_u = \frac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \frac{0.756}{0.277} = \mathbf{2.73} $$ **Step 5: Coefficient of Curvature** (1.5 marks) $$ C_c = \frac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \frac{(0.378)^2}{0.277 \times 0.756} = \frac{0.1429}{0.2094} = \mathbf{0.68} $$ **Step 6: Comment on gradation (1 mark)** Since $C_c = 0.68$ lies **outside** the range $1 \le C_c \le 3$, the material is **poorly graded** despite a moderate UI. It lacks sufficient intermediate-size particles. Suitable for fine feed applications but may segregate in storage. --- ### **OPTION B — Alternative Selection (14 Marks)** **Q3 (B). With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure to determine the Fineness Modulus of a given agricultural product by sieve analysis. List any four standard sieves used and discuss how the value of FM influences the selection of grinding equipment in a feed mill.** **[14 Marks]** **Model Solution Outline:** **(a) Description of the experimental procedure (with sketch) — [8 marks]** 1. Sample collection by coning-and-quartering (1 mark) 2. Oven drying at 110 °C to constant weight (1 mark) 3. Sieve stack assembly (4.75 mm at top → 150 µm → pan) with lid (1 mark) 4. Mechanical shaking for 10–15 minutes (1 mark) 5. Weighing retained mass on each sieve to 0.1 g (1 mark) 6. Mass balance verification (1 mark) 7. Computation of % retained, cumulative % retained, and FM = Σ/100 (2 marks — formula and explanation) **Include the sieve stack sketch as shown in Section 4.3 of these notes for full marks.** **(b) Four standard sieves used — [2 marks]** - 4.75 mm - 2.36 mm - 1.18 mm - 600 µm (0.6 mm) *(Additional acceptable answers: 300 µm, 150 µm)* **(c) Influence of FM on grinding equipment selection in a feed mill — [4 marks]** | FM Range | Product Type | Recommended Equipment | |---|---|---| | 2.2 – 2.5 | Very fine (poultry, fish feed) | Hammer mill with fine screen (1–2 mm), roller mill with corrugated rolls | | 2.5 – 2.9 | Medium (pig, calf feed) | Hammer mill with medium screen (3–4 mm) | | 2.9 – 3.2 | Coarse (cattle mash) | Hammer mill with coarse screen (6–8 mm), or roller mill | **Equipment selection logic (2 marks):** Lower FM requires **finer screen aperture** in hammer mills, **higher specific energy consumption** (kWh/tonne), and may necessitate **secondary grinding or pulverizer stages**. Higher FM allows **higher throughput** and **lower energy cost** but reduces digestibility. Hence the target animal species dictates the desired FM, which in turn dictates the mill configuration. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Forgetting the mass balance check.** If $\sum W_i$ differs from $W_{total}$ by more than **1 %**, the entire test is invalid. Examiners specifically look for this check — failure to do so costs 0.5 – 1 mark. > 2. **Including the pan in the FM sum.** The pan is **excluded**. Adding it is a frequent, costly mistake. > 3. **Using linear interpolation instead of log interpolation for D₁₀, D₃₀, D₆₀.** Particle sizes follow a log-normal distribution; therefore **log-interpolation is mandatory**. Linear interpolation will give wrong UI values and loss of 2–3 marks. > 4. **Inverted ratio for UI.** Some students compute $D_{10}/D_{60}$ instead of $D_{60}/D_{10}$. The correct order is **D₆₀ in numerator**. > 5. **Wrong % Passing definition.** % Passing = 100 − Cumulative % Retained. Do not confuse with cumulative % retained. > 6. **Unit mismatch.** Keep $D_{10}$ and $D_{60}$ in **the same units** (both mm or both µm). Mixing them produces dimensionless error of order 1000. > 7. **Omitting the grain-size distribution curve.** Even in calculation-based questions, examiners expect at least a hand-sketched or described plot of % passing vs log(sieve size) — this is worth 1–2 marks. > 8. **Not stating the standard sieve series used.** Always mention (e.g., "IS 4.75 mm – 150 µm six-sieve series") to demonstrate alignment with BIS / ASAE standards. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read Before Exam** > - **Fineness Modulus (FM)** = Σ(Cumulative % Retained) / 100 — *exclude the pan*. > - FM is **dimensionless**; **higher = coarser**, **lower = finer**. > - Typical FM range for fine agricultural powders: **2.20 – 3.20**. > - A change of **0.20 in FM** is significant in feed-mill practice. > - **Uniformity Index (UI = Cᵤ)** = $D_{60} / D_{10}$ — read from semi-log plot. > - UI ≈ **1.0** → uniform particles; UI > 4 → wide size spread. > - **C_c = (D₃₀)² / (D₁₀ · D₆₀)** — for *well-graded* materials, $1 \le C_c \le 3$. > - Always use **log-interpolation** to find D₁₀, D₃₀, D₆₀ from tabular data. > - **Mass balance** must be within **± 1 %** for the test to be valid. > - Standard sieve series for fine powders: **4.75, 2.36, 1.18, 0.600, 0.300, 0.150 mm**. > - Sieve apertures follow **√2 geometric progression** for proper log-spacing. > - **Semi-log plot** — X-axis: log of sieve aperture; Y-axis: % Passing (linear). > - Lower FM → higher specific energy consumption in grinding → finer pellets → better digestibility for young animals. > - Higher UI (poor uniformity) → **segregation in silos, rat-holing, uneven feed intake**. > - Always dry the sample at **110 °C ± 5 °C** to constant mass before sieving. > - Mechanical sieve-shaking time: **10 – 15 minutes** at ~280 rpm with ~38 mm stroke. > - For ground maize example worked out: **FM = 2.25**, **UI = 2.78**, **C_c = 0.96** → fine and poorly-graded.

topics.title: Measurement of Moisture Content of Grains: Dry Basis and Wet Basis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Moisture Content of Grains: Dry Basis and Wet Basis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Moisture Content (MC)** of a grain is defined as the quantity of water present in the grain matrix, expressed as a ratio of the mass of water removed during drying to either the original (wet) mass of the grain or the bone-dry mass of the solid. It is one of the most critical quality parameters in post-harvest agricultural processing because it directly governs **storability, milling yield, seed viability, microbial safety, and trade grading**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, moisture content is reported in one of two conventions:

- **Wet Basis Moisture Content ($M_{wb}$):** Mass of water divided by the total (wet) mass of the grain sample.
- **Dry Basis Moisture Content ($M_{db}$):** Mass of water divided by the dry mass of the grain solids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGL607 – Module 2):** Students must clearly distinguish between wet-basis and dry-basis expressions, perform unit conversions correctly, and identify the standard air-oven reference method (typically **103 °C for 72 h** or **130 °C for 1 h**) prescribed for cereal grains.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Wet Sponge" Picture

Imagine you pick up a soaked kitchen sponge. It feels heavy because it is holding water inside its porous structure.

- **Wet Basis thinking:** "Out of the *total* weight I am holding in my hand, how much is water?" — this is intuitive but misleading, because the denominator shrinks as the sponge dries.
- **Dry Basis thinking:** "Compared to the *completely dry* skeleton of the sponge, how much water was there?" — this is the engineer's perspective, because the dry solid mass does **not** change during drying. It is the **invariant reference**.

> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Use **dry basis** for engineering calculations (mass and energy balances, drying-rate equations), because the dry solid mass is constant throughout the process. Use **wet basis** for commercial, trading, and regulatory specifications (e.g., FCI's 14 % wb limit for safe storage of paddy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (FAO/ASAE S352.2):** Cereal grains for safe long-term storage should have moisture content $\leq 14\% \text{ wb}$ (equivalent to $16.3\% \text{ db}$). This single value is a universally accepted quality benchmark and frequently tested in KTU viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Setting Up the Mass Balance

Let us denote the three experimentally measured masses obtained from the **standard air-oven drying method**:

- $W_1$ = mass of empty moisture can (g)
- $W_2$ = mass of can + wet grain sample before drying (g)
- $W_3$ = mass of can + completely dry grain sample after oven drying (g)

From these, we deduce:

$$W_w = W_2 - W_3 \quad \text{(mass of water evaporated)}$$

$$W_s = W_3 - W_1 \quad \text{(mass of dry solids)}$$

$$W_t = W_2 - W_1 \quad \text{(total wet mass of sample)}$$

### 2.2 Why Two Bases Exist – The Logical Reasoning

The same physical experiment yields **two different numerical values** depending on the choice of denominator:

1. **Wet Basis** normalises the water by the *changing total mass* — convenient for commerce because the value can never exceed 100 %.
2. **Dry Basis** normalises the water by the *constant dry solid mass* — convenient for engineering because it linearly tracks drying progress (e.g., $M_{db}$ goes from a high value down to zero, making rate calculations clean).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Governing Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass of water evaporated | $W_w$ | $W_2 - W_3$ | g | Always |
| Mass of dry solid | $W_s$ | $W_3 - W_1$ | g | Always |
| Wet basis moisture content | $M_{wb}$ | $\dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_s}\times 100$ | % | Trade / FCI / Regulatory |
| Dry basis moisture content | $M_{db}$ | $\dfrac{W_w}{W_s}\times 100$ | % | Drying-rate calcs / Energy balances |
| Conversion (wb → db) | — | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ | — | Engineering |
| Conversion (db → wb) | — | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}}$ | — | Commerce |
| Equilibrium moisture content | $EMC$ | Tabulated vs. $\phi, T$ | % db | Storage design |
| Critical moisture content | $M_c$ | Empirical (grain-dependent) | % db | Drying-rate transition |
| Gravimetric (oven) accuracy | — | $\pm 0.1$ % wb | — | Reference method |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Since $W_w \geq 0$ and $W_s > 0$, we always have $0 \leq M_{wb} < 1$ (i.e., $< 100\%$), but $M_{db}$ can (and routinely does) **exceed 100 %** for very wet freshly harvested grains (e.g., paddy at $25\%$ wb $\approx 33.3\%$ db). This asymmetry is the single most common cause of confusion in KTU exams.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Agricultural Processing

- **Drying Equipment Design:** All thin-layer, deep-bed, and fluidised-bed dryer models (Page, Thompson, Lewis, Henderson-Pabis) use $M_{db}$ because the dry solid mass is the conserved quantity.
- **Storage Aeration & Silo Design:** Safe storage time is predicted using the *equilibrium relative humidity* (ERH) corresponding to the grain's $M_{db}$ at the storage temperature — again favouring dry-basis expression.
- **Milling & Processing Yield:** For rice, every 1 % drop in moisture (wb) below 14 % causes ~1.2 % drop in head-rice recovery; engineering corrections are computed in $M_{db}$.
- **Quality Control & Trade:** FCI procurement, Codex Alimentarius, and ISO 712 all quote moisture on a **wet basis** because the value is bounded between 0 and 100 %.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Wet-Basis Formula

Starting from the definition, where the denominator is the **total wet mass** (water + dry solids):

$$M_{wb} = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{total wet mass}} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_s}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 100 converts the dimensionless ratio into a percentage:

$$M_{wb}\,(\%) = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_s}\times 100$$

**Interpretation:** This expresses water mass as a fraction of what the grain *currently* weighs. If we keep removing water, the numerator decreases **and** the denominator decreases — the ratio decreases non-linearly with time, which is why a wet-basis drying curve is curved.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Dry-Basis Formula

The dry-basis convention normalises water mass by the **dry solids only**:

$$M_{db} = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{mass of dry solid}} = \frac{W_w}{W_s}$$

In percentage form:

$$M_{db}\,(\%) = \frac{W_w}{W_s}\times 100$$

**Interpretation:** During drying, $W_s$ remains constant (no solid is lost), and only $W_w$ decreases — so $M_{db}$ decreases **linearly** with the mass of water removed. This is the engineer's preferred form.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Wet-Basis → Dry-Basis Conversion

We start from the two definitions and take their ratio:

$$\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} = \frac{W_w / W_s}{W_w / (W_w + W_s)}$$

The $W_w$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} = \frac{W_w + W_s}{W_s} = 1 + \frac{W_w}{W_s} = 1 + M_{db}$$

Rearranging this implicit equation for $M_{db}$:

$$M_{db} = M_{wb} \cdot (1 + M_{db})$$

$$M_{db} = M_{wb} + M_{wb}\cdot M_{db}$$

$$M_{db} - M_{wb}\cdot M_{db} = M_{wb}$$

$$M_{db}(1 - M_{wb}) = M_{wb}$$

$$\boxed{M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}}$$

This is the **primary KTU formula** — write it from memory.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Dry-Basis → Wet-Basis Conversion

Starting from the boxed result, invert algebraically:

$$M_{db}(1 - M_{wb}) = M_{wb}$$

$$M_{db} - M_{db}\cdot M_{wb} = M_{wb}$$

$$M_{db} = M_{wb} + M_{db}\cdot M_{wb} = M_{wb}(1 + M_{db})$$

$$\boxed{M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}}}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

A grain sample is dried in a hot-air oven.
- Mass of empty moisture can, $W_1 = 28.50$ g
- Mass of can + wet grain, $W_2 = 78.50$ g
- Mass of can + dry grain (after 72 h at 103 °C), $W_3 = 73.70$ g

**Step 1 — Compute the three key masses:**

$$W_w = W_2 - W_3 = 78.50 - 73.70 = 4.80 \text{ g}$$

$$W_s = W_3 - W_1 = 73.70 - 28.50 = 45.20 \text{ g}$$

$$W_t = W_2 - W_1 = 78.50 - 28.50 = 50.00 \text{ g}$$

**Verification:** $W_w + W_s = 4.80 + 45.20 = 50.00$ g $= W_t$ ✓ (mass balance closes).

**Step 2 — Compute wet-basis moisture content:**

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_s}\times 100 = \frac{4.80}{50.00}\times 100 = 9.60\,\% \text{ wb}$$

**Step 3 — Compute dry-basis moisture content:**

$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_s}\times 100 = \frac{4.80}{45.20}\times 100 = 10.62\,\% \text{ db}$$

**Step 4 — Verify using the conversion formula:**

$$M_{db,\text{calc}} = \frac{0.0960}{1 - 0.0960} = \frac{0.0960}{0.9040} = 0.1062 = 10.62\,\%$$

Both routes agree — the conversion is internally consistent. ✓

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

The grain at $9.60\%$ wb is **safely below** the FCI 14 % wb storage limit and is ready for long-term storage in a sealed silo without aeration. From an engineer's standpoint, only $10.62\%$ db of "internal water" remains, comfortably below the critical moisture content for respiration suppression.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | $M_{wb}$ | $M_{db}$ | $M_{db} > M_{wb}$? | Typical Situation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bone-dry grain | $0\,\%$ | $0\,\%$ | Equal | After exhaustive oven drying |
| Safe storage limit (cereals) | $14\,\%$ | $16.28\,\%$ | db > wb | FCI procurement grade |
| Freshly harvested paddy | $22\,\%$ | $28.21\,\%$ | db > wb | Field-fresh, needs drying |
| Wet forage / silage | $70\,\%$ | $233\,\%$ | db $\gg$ wb | Green fodder, ensiling |
| Pure liquid water | $100\,\%$ | Undefined ($\infty$) | wb = 100 % | Degenerate limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For every physically realistic grain sample, $M_{db} > M_{wb}$ because the dry-basis denominator is always **smaller** than the wet-basis denominator. The two values coincide only at zero moisture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Gravimetric (Oven) Method for Grain Moisture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Grain sample received] --> stepA[Weigh empty moisture can W1]
    stepA --> stepB[Add ~10-15 g grain to can]
    stepB --> stepC[Weigh can + wet grain W2]
    stepC --> stepD[Place can in hot-air oven]
    stepD --> stepE{Oven temperature}
    stepE --> stepF1[103 deg C for 72 h - cereals standard]
    stepE --> stepF2[130 deg C for 1 h - rapid method]
    stepE --> stepF3[Vacuum oven 70 deg C - heat-sensitive]
    stepF1 --> stepG[Cool in desiccator 30 min]
    stepF2 --> stepG
    stepF3 --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH[Weigh can + dry grain W3]
    stepH --> stepI{Weight constant within 0.001 g on re-drying}
    stepI -->|No| stepD
    stepI -->|Yes| stepJ[Calculate Ww, Ws, Wt]
    stepJ --> stepK[Report Mwb in percent wb]
    stepJ --> stepL[Report Mdb in percent db]
    stepK --> endA[End: Result documented]
    stepL --> endA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Wet Basis vs Dry Basis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    water((Mass of water Ww)) --> wb{What is the denominator?}
    water --> db{What is the denominator?}
    wb --> tot[Total wet mass Ww + Ws]
    db --> dry[Dry solid mass Ws only]
    tot --> ansA[Mwb - used in trade and storage specs]
    dry --> ansB[Mdb - used in drying design and energy balance]
    ansA --> conv[Convert: Mdb = Mwb / 1 - Mwb]
    ansB --> conv
    conv --> end2((KTU Exam Ready))
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Moisture Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    samp[Grain sample loaded into cell] --> cond[Conditioning chamber temp and RH stabilised]
    cond --> sens{Measurement principle}
    sens --> s1[Capacitance sensor]
    sens --> s2[Resistance sensor]
    sens --> s3[NIR reflectance]
    sens --> s4[Pinless RF resonator]
    s1 --> cal[Internal calibration curve grain-specific]
    s2 --> cal
    s3 --> cal
    s4 --> cal
    cal --> disp[Digital display reports Mwb in percent]
    cal --> comp[Comparator: Mwb versus safe storage limit 14 percent]
    comp -->|Pass| ok[Green LED - SAFE TO STORE]
    comp -->|Fail| warn[Red LED - DRY FURTHER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Experiment 2 in PCAGL607 typically uses either an **air-oven** (gravimetric, primary standard) or a **digital grain moisture meter** (capacitance-based, secondary/field method). The functional architecture above helps you answer viva questions on the *principle of operation* of indirect meters, which is a frequent Part-A question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define moisture content of a grain on dry basis and wet basis. Why is dry-basis moisture content preferred for engineering calculations?

**Model Answer:**
Moisture content of a grain is the ratio of the mass of water present in the grain to a reference mass, expressed as a percentage. On the **wet basis**, the reference is the total (wet) mass of the grain:

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_s}\times 100\,\%$$

On the **dry basis**, the reference is the mass of dry solids only:

$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_s}\times 100\,\%$$

The dry-basis expression is preferred for engineering calculations because the mass of dry solids $W_s$ remains constant throughout the drying process. This makes the dry-basis moisture content a linear function of the mass of water removed, simplifying mass and energy balances, drying-rate analysis, and the use of standard thin-layer drying equations such as the Page and Henderson-Pabis models. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A grain sample weighs 50 g before drying and 45 g after complete drying in a hot-air oven. Calculate its moisture content on both wet and dry basis.

**Model Answer:**

$$W_w = 50 - 45 = 5 \text{ g}, \quad W_s = 45 \text{ g}$$

$$M_{wb} = \frac{5}{50}\times 100 = 10\,\% \text{ wb}$$

$$M_{db} = \frac{5}{45}\times 100 = 11.11\,\% \text{ db}$$

**Verification using conversion formula:** $M_{db} = 0.10/(1-0.10) = 0.1111 = 11.11\%$ ✓ **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** Moisture Content Measurement of Paddy

**(a)** Describe in detail the **standard air-oven method** for determination of moisture content of paddy grains. Include the apparatus, sample preparation, drying conditions, and the sequence of weighing. State the relevant IS/ASAE standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a laboratory experiment, 12.00 g of paddy (wet) was dried in a hot-air oven at 103 °C for 72 hours. The dry mass was found to be 10.08 g. Calculate (i) moisture content on wet basis, (ii) moisture content on dry basis, (iii) the mass of water removed per kg of dry paddy, and (iv) comment on whether the paddy is safe for long-term storage as per FCI norms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The standard air-oven method is the **primary reference (gravimetric) method** accepted by ASAE Standard S352.2 and IS 4333 (Part II) for cereal grains including paddy.

**Apparatus required:**

- Hot-air oven capable of maintaining $103\pm 2\,^\circ\text{C}$ with thermostatic control and forced-air circulation.
- Moisture cans (aluminium, with tight-fitting lids).
- Desiccator charged with silica gel for cooling.
- Analytical balance with accuracy $\pm 0.001$ g.
- Sample divider (riffle-type) for sub-sampling.

**Sample preparation:**

- Draw a representative sample of at least 100 g from the bulk lot using a grain sampler.
- Reduce by coning-and-quartering or a riffle divider to obtain a working sample of $\approx 15\text{–}20$ g.
- If grains are larger than 5 mm (e.g., maize), pre-grind to pass through a 1 mm sieve; for paddy, whole grains are used.

**Procedure:**

1. Clean, dry, and weigh the empty moisture can with its lid: record $W_1$.
2. Transfer $\approx 10\text{–}15$ g of the prepared grain to the can and weigh immediately: record $W_2$.
3. Place the can (lid removed or placed under) in the pre-heated oven at **103 °C**.
4. Dry for **72 hours** for paddy (per IS/ASAE for cereals).
5. Remove, cover with lid, transfer to desiccator, cool for 30 minutes.
6. Weigh: record $W_3$.
7. Re-dry for 1 hour, cool, and weigh again. Repeat until two consecutive weighings differ by less than **0.001 g** — this confirms constant mass.
8. Calculate moisture content using the wet-basis or dry-basis formula as required. **(7 marks – full marks awarded for apparatus 2, procedure 3, standard reference 1, and weighing sequence 1)**

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given data:**
- $W_2 - W_1 = 12.00$ g (wet paddy mass)  ⟹  $W_w + W_s = 12.00$ g
- $W_3 - W_1 = 10.08$ g (dry paddy mass)  ⟹  $W_s = 10.08$ g

**Step 1 — Mass of water evaporated:**

$$W_w = 12.00 - 10.08 = 1.92 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Moisture content on wet basis:**

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_s}\times 100 = \frac{1.92}{12.00}\times 100 = 16.00\,\% \text{ wb}$$

**Step 3 — Moisture content on dry basis:**

$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_s}\times 100 = \frac{1.92}{10.08}\times 100 = 19.05\,\% \text{ db}$$

**Verification using the conversion formula:**

$$M_{db} = \frac{0.16}{1 - 0.16} = \frac{0.16}{0.84} = 0.1905 = 19.05\,\% \text{ db} \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Mass of water removed per kg of dry paddy:**

$$\text{Water per kg dry solid} = \frac{W_w}{W_s}\times 1000 = \frac{1.92}{10.08}\times 1000 = 190.48 \text{ g per kg dry paddy}$$

**Step 5 — Storage safety verdict:**

FCI's safe-storage limit for paddy is **14 % wb**. The measured value of **16.00 % wb exceeds this limit by 2 percentage points**, meaning the paddy is **NOT safe** for long-term storage. It must be dried further to remove at least:

$$\text{Additional water to remove} = \frac{16 - 14}{100 - 14}\times 100 = 2.33\,\% \text{ db}$$

i.e., the dry-basis moisture must be reduced from 19.05 % to approximately 16.72 % db before the grain can be safely stored. **(7 marks – 2 marks each for parts i and ii, 1 mark for part iii, 2 marks for part iv including conversion and storage verdict)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** Moisture Content – Methods and Conversions

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and working of a **capacitance-type digital grain moisture meter**. List its advantages and limitations compared with the standard air-oven method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheat lot has moisture content of **18 % db**. The grain is to be dried down to a safe storage level of **13 % wb**. Calculate (i) the wet-basis moisture content corresponding to 18 % db, (ii) the mass of water that must be removed per 1000 kg of wet grain to achieve the target moisture, and (iii) verify your final wet-basis value using the conversion formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

A capacitance-type moisture meter exploits the large difference between the **dielectric constant of water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$)** and that of dry grain solids ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2\text{–}5$).

**Principle:** The grain sample is packed into a test cell that forms the dielectric of a capacitor. As moisture increases, the overall dielectric constant rises, increasing the measured capacitance $C$. A built-in microcontroller converts this capacitance to a moisture reading using a grain-specific calibration curve.

**Block diagram (described verbally for the answer sheet):**

Sample cell $\rightarrow$ Oscillator (sine wave ~10 MHz) $\rightarrow$ Capacitance-to-voltage converter $\rightarrow$ Microcontroller (with calibration lookup table for wheat/paddy/maize) $\rightarrow$ LCD display in % wb $\rightarrow$ Comparator $\rightarrow$ Pass/Fail LED.

**Advantages over the air-oven method:**

1. Rapid (10–30 seconds vs. 72 hours).
2. Non-destructive — the same grain can be used for further tests.
3. Portable, battery-operated, suitable for field and procurement-centre use.
4. Operator-skill independent once calibrated.

**Limitations:**

1. Calibration drifts with grain variety, bulk density, and temperature — requires regular re-calibration against the oven method.
2. Lower accuracy ($\pm 0.5\%$ wb) compared with the oven ($\pm 0.1\%$ wb).
3. Affected by grain temperature; must be used with a built-in temperature compensation or after sample tempering.
4. Surface moisture (e.g., from rain) gives falsely high readings because the meter responds strongly to water on grain surfaces.
5. Not a primary/standard method; legally, trade disputes are still resolved using the oven method. **(7 marks – 2 for principle, 2 for block diagram description, 1.5 for advantages, 1.5 for limitations)**

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** Initial moisture $M_{db,i} = 18\%$, target moisture $M_{wb,f} = 13\%$.

**Part (i) — Convert 18 % db to wet basis:**

Using the conversion formula:

$$M_{wb,i} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}} = \frac{0.18}{1 + 0.18} = \frac{0.18}{1.18} = 0.1525 = 15.25\,\% \text{ wb}$$

**Part (ii) — Mass of water to remove per 1000 kg of wet grain:**

Let $B$ = mass of dry solid in 1000 kg of wet grain. From the initial moisture:

$$W_{w,i} = 1000 - B = 0.1525 \times 1000 = 152.54 \text{ kg}$$

$$B = 1000 - 152.54 = 847.46 \text{ kg of dry solids}$$

Final moisture in dry basis (so we know exactly where we land):

$$M_{db,f} = \frac{0.13}{1 - 0.13} = \frac{0.13}{0.87} = 0.1494 = 14.94\,\% \text{ db}$$

Final water mass:

$$W_{w,f} = M_{db,f}\times B = 0.1494\times 847.46 = 126.64 \text{ kg}$$

**Mass of water to be removed:**

$$\Delta W_w = W_{w,i} - W_{w,f} = 152.54 - 126.64 = 25.90 \text{ kg per 1000 kg of wet wheat}$$

**Final wet mass of grain:**

$$W_{t,f} = B + W_{w,f} = 847.46 + 126.64 = 974.10 \text{ kg}$$

**Check (Part iii) — Verify the final wb value:**

$$M_{wb,f} = \frac{126.64}{974.10}\times 100 = 13.00\,\% \text{ wb} \checkmark$$

This matches the target moisture, confirming the calculation is consistent. **(7 marks – 1.5 for part i, 4 for part ii, 1.5 for part iii)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the denominator:** Writing $M_{wb} = W_w/W_s$ is the single most common error; this is the dry-basis formula, not wet-basis. Always re-read the subscript before substituting numbers.
> 2. **Forgetting the *100 factor:** Many students report $M_{wb} = 0.096$ instead of $9.6\%$ and lose 1–2 marks for missing units.
> 3. **Ignoring the mass of the can ($W_1$):** A can weighing ~25–30 g is *not* negligible. Always subtract $W_1$ to obtain $W_w$ and $W_s$.
> 4. **Reporting "wb" for engineering calculations:** In drying-rate equations (Page, Henderson-Pabis), always quote moisture on a dry basis; the KTU examiner deducts 1 mark if the wrong basis is used in Part B derivations.
> 5. **Not checking the conversion round-trip:** If your wet-basis → dry-basis → wet-basis conversion does not return the original value, you have made an arithmetic slip — fix it before submitting.
> 6. **Mis-stating the FCI limit:** The safe-storage limit for **paddy is 14 % wb**, but for **wheat it is 12 % wb**, and for **maize it is 14 % wb**. Quote the correct limit for the grain in the question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two bases, one experiment:** Wet basis uses total mass as denominator; dry basis uses dry solid mass as denominator.
- **Conversion is algebraic, not approximate:** $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(1 - M_{wb})$ and $M_{wb} = M_{db}/(1 + M_{db})$ are **exact** conversions.
- **Always satisfy:** $M_{db} \geq M_{wb}$ for any real grain (equality only at zero moisture).
- **Standard oven conditions for cereals (memorise):** $103 \pm 2\,^\circ\text{C}$ for **72 h** in a forced-convection oven; cool in desiccator 30 min before weighing.
- **Rapid alternative:** $130\,^\circ\text{C}$ for 1 h gives equivalent results for routine quality control but is not an arbitration method.
- **Mass-balance closure:** Always verify that $W_w + W_s = W_t$ — this catches arithmetic mistakes in 30 seconds.
- **Reference method:** Air-oven (gravimetric) is the **primary** standard; capacitance, resistance, and NIR are **secondary** (faster but less accurate).
- **Safe storage limit (FCI / Codex):** $14\%$ wb for paddy and maize, $12\%$ wb for wheat, $11\%$ wb for rice (milled).
- **Field meters:** Capacitance meters report $M_{wb}$ directly because the calibration curve is built-in; do not convert unless the question explicitly asks.
- **Engineering preference:** Always use $M_{db}$ in drying-rate equations, energy balances, EMC isotherms, and sorption studies.
- **Trapped grains:** For large grains (maize, sorghum), pre-grinding to <1 mm is mandatory to release bound/internal moisture, otherwise the oven method **underestimates** moisture.
- **Desiccator step:** Hot samples gain moisture rapidly from laboratory air; cooling in a sealed desiccator is **not optional**, it is a critical step in the SOP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying Characteristics of Grains and Drying Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Drying Characteristics of Grains and Drying Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Unit Operations in Agricultural Processing**, the **drying characteristics of grains** refer to the time-dependent behavior of moisture removal from a hygroscopic biological material (such as paddy, wheat, maize, or millet) when it is exposed to a stream of hot, dry air under controlled laboratory conditions. These characteristics are typically expressed as a plot of **Moisture Content versus Drying Time** (and the corresponding **Drying Rate versus Moisture Content** curve), which together form the *thin-layer drying curve* of the grain.

The **Drying Constant** (often denoted by the symbol $\mathbf{k}$ with units of $\mathbf{min^{-1}}$ or $\mathbf{hr^{-1}}$) is the first-order rate constant that appears in the classical **Lewis thin-layer drying model**. It quantifies how fast moisture migrates from the interior of the grain to its surface and is subsequently evaporated into the surrounding air. A higher value of $k$ indicates a faster drying grain; a lower $k$ indicates a more diffusion-limited, slower drying behavior.

Formally, the Lewis equation is written as:

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = -k (M - M_e)
$$

where $M$ is the instantaneous moisture content (dry basis), $M_e$ is the equilibrium moisture content, and $t$ is the elapsed drying time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module Experiment 2, the student is expected to experimentally determine the **drying constant $k$** for a given grain sample (typically **paddy or wheat**) using a **thin-layer dryer**, plot the **drying rate curve**, and identify the **constant-rate and falling-rate drying periods**. This experiment directly tests **Course Outcome CO3**: *Apply the principles of drying, size reduction, and separation to agricultural produce.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a fully soaked kitchen sponge (representing a freshly harvested wet grain). If you leave it on a table in a sunny, breezy room, you will observe two distinct stages of water loss:

1. **Stage 1 – The "Freely Wiping" Stage (Constant Rate Period):** As long as the surface of the sponge remains visibly wet, water evaporates at a roughly constant rate — the surface is the bottleneck, not the inside. The rate of drying depends almost entirely on the air's ability to absorb vapor.

2. **Stage 2 – The "Hard Squeeze" Stage (Falling Rate Period):** Once the surface looks dry, water now has to *migrate* from the deep inner pores to the surface before it can evaporate. This internal migration is much slower, and the drying rate progressively drops.

The **drying constant $k$** in this analogy is like a "stubbornness coefficient" of the sponge: a low $k$ means the sponge holds onto its water tenaciously (like a dense corn kernel), while a high $k$ means the water escapes easily (like a thin slice of mushroom). In the **Lewis model**, this behavior is captured by a simple, elegant **exponential decay** equation — moisture difference falls off like a thermometer cooling to room temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary You Must Memorize for KTU Viva:**
> - **Dry Basis Moisture Content (MC, db):** Mass of water ÷ mass of dry solid (units: kg water/kg dry solid)
> - **Wet Basis Moisture Content (MC, wb):** Mass of water ÷ total mass of wet grain (units: %)
> - **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($M_e$):** The moisture content at which the grain neither gains nor loses water to the surrounding air.
> - **Free Moisture Content ($M - M_e$):** The "driving force" for drying at any instant.
> - **Thin-Layer Drying:** A single kernel-thick layer of grain exposed uniformly to drying air — the standard laboratory protocol for determining $k$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Experiment

The following bold constants/metrics are universally used in the KTU 2024 lab manual for grain drying:

- **Standard hot-air temperature for paddy drying:** $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $60^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Standard hot-air temperature for wheat drying:** $45^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $65^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Typical relative humidity of drying air:** $10\%$ to $30\%$
- **Superimposed air velocity:** $0.5$ to $2.0\ \text{m/s}$
- **Recommended initial moisture content for the experiment:** $20\%$ to $35\%$ (wb)
- **Target safe storage moisture content:** $12\%$ to $14\%$ (wb) for cereals
- **Drying constant $k$ for paddy (typical range):** $0.01$ to $0.05\ \text{min}^{-1}$
- **Drying constant $k$ for wheat (typical range):** $0.02$ to $0.08\ \text{min}^{-1}$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Grain Drying — Step-by-Step Logic

Drying of a moist grain kernel is **not** a single, uniform process. It is a coupled **heat-mass transfer** operation that proceeds through the following sequential micro-steps:

1. **Heat Transfer from Air to Grain:** Hot drying air delivers sensible heat to the cool grain surface. This raises the grain temperature and supplies the **latent heat of vaporization** needed to convert liquid water into vapor.

2. **Internal Moisture Migration:** Inside the kernel, moisture moves from the high-moisture core toward the drier surface. This internal transport is governed by three mechanisms, in order of importance:
   - **Liquid diffusion** through the porous solid matrix (dominant in the falling rate period).
   - **Capillary flow** through the micro-pores (dominant in the constant rate period).
   - **Thermal diffusion** (Soret effect) — usually minor.

3. **Vaporization at the Surface:** Liquid water at or near the grain surface evaporates into the surrounding air film.

4. **Vapor Removal by Air:** The boundary-layer air, now humidified, is swept away by the bulk drying airstream, maintaining the vapor-pressure gradient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Constant-Rate Period** is **rarely observed** in real grain drying because the hygroscopic solid matrix holds water tightly and the surface dries out almost immediately. **Most agricultural grain drying experiments show only the falling-rate period**, and the Lewis model is applied over the entire drying curve. This is a common viva question: *"Why don't you see a constant-rate period in paddy drying?"*

### 2.2 Thin-Layer Drying Models

To mathematically describe the falling-rate drying curve, KTU students are expected to know the following three models:

**Model 1 — Lewis / Newton's Model (Lath & Churchill, 1948):**
This is the **fundamental, default model** used in Module Experiment 2.

$$
\text{MR} = \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k t)
$$

where $M_0$ is the initial moisture content, $M$ is the moisture at time $t$, $M_e$ is the equilibrium moisture content, and $k$ is the drying constant (min$^{-1}$).

**Model 2 — Page Model (Page, 1949):**
An empirical correction to the Lewis model that fits grain data more accurately by introducing an exponent $n$.

$$
\text{MR} = \exp(-k t^{n})
$$

**Model 3 — Henderson-Pabis Model (Henderson & Pabis, 1961):**
Adds a coefficient $a$ to the Lewis decay.

$$
\text{MR} = a \cdot \exp(-k t)
$$

For the standard KTU lab experiment, the **Lewis model** is mandatory; understanding the Page model gives bonus marks in viva.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Units** |
|:-----------|:------------|:-------------------------|:------------------|
| $M_t$ | Moisture content at time $t$ (db) | $M_t = \dfrac{w_t - w_d}{w_d}$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| $M_0$ | Initial moisture content (db) | $M_0 = \dfrac{w_0 - w_d}{w_d}$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| $M_e$ | Equilibrium moisture content | Read from EMC curve or sorption isotherm | kg water / kg dry solid |
| $M_{wb}$ | Wet basis moisture content | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_t}{1 + M_t} \times 100$ | % |
| $\text{MR}$ | Moisture Ratio (dimensionless) | $\text{MR} = \dfrac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$ | — |
| $k$ | Drying constant (Lewis) | $k = -\dfrac{\ln(\text{MR})}{t}$ | min$^{-1}$ |
| $N$ | Drying Rate | $N = -\dfrac{dM}{dt} = \dfrac{M_{t} - M_{t+\Delta t}}{\Delta t}$ | kg water / (kg dry solid $\cdot$ min) |
| $L$ | Latent heat of vaporization | $L \approx 2400 - 2.4\,T$ (with $T$ in °C) | kJ / kg |
| $D$ | Effective moisture diffusivity | $D = \dfrac{k \cdot r^2}{\pi^2}$ | m$^2$ / s |
| $n$ | Page model exponent | $n = 0.7$ to $1.5$ (typical) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Trap:** Many students confuse db and wb moisture content in the KTU exam. **Always state clearly** whether your moisture content is on a dry basis or wet basis. The Lewis model **must** use dry-basis values, while most practical farm reporting uses wet basis.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The drying constant $k$ is the single most important design parameter used by **agricultural process engineers** when sizing commercial grain dryers (batch dryers, continuous-flow LSU dryers, fluidized-bed dryers). It is also used to:

- **Predict drying time** required to bring a grain from $M_0$ to a target safe-storage $M_f$.
- **Estimate energy consumption** of the dryer in kWh per tonne of grain.
- **Assess diffusivity** $D$ for use in finite-element simulations of coupled heat and mass transfer.
- **Optimize drying temperature** — too high a temperature causes **crack formation** in paddy (a major KTU viva question), reducing mill yield.
