## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Thin-Layer Drying Equation

**Starting Point — Newton's Law of Cooling Analogy:**

The rate at which the *free moisture* (the part above equilibrium) decreases is assumed to be **directly proportional to the free moisture present**. This is mathematically analogous to Newton's law of cooling.

**Step 1:** Write the rate of change of free moisture.

$$
\frac{d(M - M_e)}{dt} = -k (M - M_e)
$$

The negative sign is included because free moisture decreases with time. We assume $M_e$ is approximately constant during the experiment (the EMC does not shift appreciably at constant air conditions).

**Step 2:** Since $M_e$ is constant, we can pull it out of the differential.

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = -k (M - M_e)
$$

**Step 3:** Separate the variables to integrate.

$$
\frac{dM}{(M - M_e)} = -k \, dt
$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from the initial state $(t = 0,\ M = M_0)$ to an arbitrary state $(t = t,\ M = M)$.

$$
\int_{M_0}^{M} \frac{dM}{(M - M_e)} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the left integral (standard logarithm form).

$$
\big[ \ln(M - M_e) \big]_{M_0}^{M} = -k t
$$

This gives:

$$
\ln(M - M_e) - \ln(M_0 - M_e) = -k t
$$

**Step 6:** Combine the logarithms.

$$
\ln\!\left( \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} \right) = -k t
$$

**Step 7:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the canonical Lewis equation.

$$
\boxed{\ \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k t)\ }
$$

**Step 8 (Linearization for Experimental Use):**

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides gives a **straight-line equation** in $t$:

$$
\ln(\text{MR}) = \ln\!\left( \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} \right) = -k t
$$

If we plot $\ln(\text{MR})$ on the y-axis and $t$ (in minutes) on the x-axis, we get a **straight line passing through the origin with slope $-k$**. This is the **standard KTU lab graph** for the experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Drying Constant $k$ is simply the absolute value of the slope** of the line $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$. The intercept must be **zero** if the Lewis model is valid. Any deviation from zero is experimental error.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Diffusivity $D$

The drying constant $k$ can be related to the **effective moisture diffusivity** $D$ inside the grain kernel using Fick's second law of diffusion. For a spherical grain (a fair assumption for paddy and wheat kernels), the first-term solution is:

$$
\text{MR} = \frac{6}{\pi^{2}} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{n^{2} \pi^{2} D t}{r^{2}} \right)
$$

For long drying times, only the first term ($n=1$) is significant. Comparing this with the Lewis equation, we get the equivalence:

$$
k = \frac{\pi^{2} D}{r^{2}}
$$

Rearranging for $D$:

$$
\boxed{\ D = \frac{k \, r^{2}}{\pi^{2}}\ }
$$

where $r$ is the equivalent radius of the grain kernel (in meters). For a paddy grain, $r \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$. For a wheat kernel, $r \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Sample Numerical Calculation

**Given Experimental Data** (paddy, drying air at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$):

| Time $t$ (min) | Mass of sample $w_t$ (g) | Dry mass $w_d = 80\ \text{g}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 120.0 | 80 |
| 15 | 115.2 | 80 |
| 30 | 111.1 | 80 |
| 45 | 107.5 | 80 |
| 60 | 104.3 | 80 |
| 90 | 99.0 | 80 |
| 120 | 95.0 | 80 |

Assume $M_e = 0.04$ (kg/kg db) at the given air conditions.

**Step A — Compute $M_t$ (dry basis) at each time:**

$$
M_t = \frac{w_t - w_d}{w_d}
$$

Let us tabulate the values:

| $t$ (min) | $w_t$ (g) | $M_t$ (kg/kg db) | $\text{MR} = \dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$ | $\ln(\text{MR})$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 120.0 | 0.500 | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| 15 | 115.2 | 0.440 | 0.870 | $-0.139$ |
| 30 | 111.1 | 0.389 | 0.760 | $-0.274$ |
| 45 | 107.5 | 0.344 | 0.663 | $-0.411$ |
| 60 | 104.3 | 0.304 | 0.577 | $-0.550$ |
| 90 | 99.0 | 0.238 | 0.433 | $-0.837$ |
| 120 | 95.0 | 0.188 | 0.326 | $-1.122$ |

**Step B — Linear Regression of $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$:**

Using the least-squares method:

$$
\text{slope} = -k = \frac{N \sum t \ln(\text{MR}) - \sum t \sum \ln(\text{MR})}{N \sum t^{2} - (\sum t)^{2}}
$$

Plugging in the seven data points and solving:

$$
-k \approx -0.00932\ \text{min}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ k \approx 0.00932\ \text{min}^{-1}\ }
$$

**Step C — Compute the Effective Diffusivity:**

Using $r = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$ for paddy:

$$
D = \frac{k \cdot r^{2}}{\pi^{2}} = \frac{0.00932 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{\pi^{2}}
$$

$$
D = \frac{0.00932 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696}
$$

$$
D = \frac{2.097 \times 10^{-8}}{9.8696}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ D \approx 2.12 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}\ }
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Check:** The value of $D$ for food grains at $40$–$60^{\circ}\text{C}$ typically lies in the range $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$. Our computed value of $2.12 \times 10^{-9}$ falls comfortably in this range, confirming the result is physically reasonable.

### 3.4 Drying Rate $N$ Calculation (Analytical Matrix)

| $t$ (min) | $M_t$ | $M_{t+\Delta t}$ | $\Delta t$ | $N = (M_t - M_{t+\Delta t}) / \Delta t$ (kg/kg db per min) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.500 | 0.440 | 15 | 0.00400 |
| 15 | 0.440 | 0.389 | 15 | 0.00340 |
| 30 | 0.389 | 0.344 | 15 | 0.00300 |
| 45 | 0.344 | 0.304 | 15 | 0.00267 |
| 60 | 0.304 | 0.238 | 30 | 0.00220 |
| 90 | 0.238 | 0.188 | 30 | 0.00167 |

The decreasing $N$ values confirm that the entire experiment lies in the **falling-rate drying period** — exactly the expected behavior for hygroscopic grains.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Thin-Layer Drying Experimental Setup (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clean Grain Sample 100 g] --> B[Weigh Initial Wet Mass w0]
    B --> C[Determine Dry Mass wd via Oven at 130 C for 24 h]
    C --> D[Compute Initial Moisture M0 db]
    D --> E[Switch ON Hot Air Blower + Heater]
    E --> F[Set Drying Temperature 50 C and Air Velocity 1.0 m/s]
    F --> G[Spread Grain as Single Layer in Tray]
    G --> H[Record Mass wt at Fixed Intervals 0 15 30 45 60 90 120 min]
    H --> I{Continue Drying Until Mass Change less than 0.1 g per 10 min}
    I -->|No| H
    I -->|Yes| J[Compute Mt db and Moisture Ratio MR]
    J --> K[Plot ln MR vs t on Semi Log Paper]
    K --> L[Determine Drying Constant k from Slope]
    L --> M[Compute Effective Diffusivity D]
    M --> N[Plot Drying Rate N vs Mt]
    N --> O[Identify Constant Rate and Falling Rate Periods]
    O --> P[End: Tabulate Results and Submit Report]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Drying Curve Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Moist Grain M0] --> B[Stage 1: Initial Transient 0 to 5 min]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Constant Rate Period Rare for Grains]
    C --> D[Stage 3: First Falling Rate Period]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Second Falling Rate Period Dominant in Grains]
    E --> F[Equilibrium Moisture Me Drying Stops]
    F --> G[Report Drying Constant k from Slope of ln MR vs t]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Data Reduction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawData[Raw wt vs t Data] --> CalcM[Compute Mt db at Each t]
    CalcM --> CalcMR[Compute Moisture Ratio MR]
    CalcMR --> CalcLnMR[Compute ln MR]
    CalcLnMR --> PlotSemi[Plot ln MR vs t Semi Log]
    PlotSemi --> FitLine[Linear Fit to Obtain Slope]
    FitLine --> GetK[Extract k min to the power of 1]
    GetK --> GetD[Compute D from r and k]
    GetD --> GetN[Compute Drying Rate N]
    GetN --> FinalReport[Generate Drying Curve and Drying Rate Curve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three diagrams?** The first represents the **physical apparatus workflow**, the second represents the **conceptual drying stages** (high-yield viva content), and the third represents the **data reduction pipeline** the student will actually follow when writing the KTU lab report.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the term "Drying Constant" as applied to thin-layer drying of agricultural grains.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The drying constant $k$ is the first-order rate constant appearing in the Lewis thin-layer drying equation:

$$
\text{MR} = \exp(-k t)
$$

where $\text{MR} = (M - M_e) / (M_0 - M_e)$ is the dimensionless moisture ratio. It represents the rate at which free moisture is removed from the grain per unit time, and has units of min$^{-1}$. A higher $k$ indicates faster drying, while a lower $k$ indicates diffusion-limited slow drying. It is determined experimentally as the absolute value of the slope of the straight line obtained by plotting $\ln(\text{MR})$ versus drying time $t$.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for units/physical meaning.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Distinguish between dry-basis and wet-basis moisture content. Why is dry-basis preferred in drying kinetics studies?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Dry Basis (db) | Wet Basis (wb) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Mass of water ÷ mass of dry solid | Mass of water ÷ total wet mass |
| Value at 100% water | Infinite | 100% |
| Value at 0% water | 0% | 0% |
| Typical range for grains | 0.10 to 0.35 | 9% to 26% |

Dry-basis is preferred in drying kinetics because it remains finite as the grain approaches dry state and because the mass of dry solid is invariant during drying, making the moisture ratio $(M - M_e)/(M_0 - M_e)$ a clean exponential decay. *(2 marks for table, 1 mark for justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions of 14 marks have **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are presented below — the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the Lewis thin-layer drying equation starting from the assumption that the rate of moisture removal is proportional to the free moisture content. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Grain is exposed as a single uniform thin layer.
2. Air temperature, humidity, and velocity are constant during drying.
3. Equilibrium moisture content $M_e$ is constant.
4. Internal moisture migration is the rate-limiting step (falling-rate period).
5. The grain shrinks negligibly during drying.
6. The drying rate is directly proportional to the free moisture content $(M - M_e)$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The basic rate equation is:

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} = -k(M - M_e)
$$

Since $M_e$ is constant, this rewrites as:

$$
\frac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k \, dt
$$

Integrating from $t=0$ ($M=M_0$) to $t=t$ ($M=M$):

$$
\int_{M_0}^{M} \frac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\big[ \ln(M - M_e) \big]_{M_0}^{M} = -kt
$$

$$
\ln(M - M_e) - \ln(M_0 - M_e) = -kt
$$

$$
\boxed{\ \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-kt)\ }
$$

This is the **Lewis thin-layer drying equation**. Linearization gives $\ln(\text{MR}) = -kt$, so $k$ is the slope of $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$.

---

**(b)** In a thin-layer drying experiment on paddy, the following data were obtained at an air temperature of $50^{\circ}\text{C}$. Initial moisture content $M_0 = 0.45$ (db), equilibrium moisture content $M_e = 0.04$ (db).

| $t$ (min) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $M$ (db) | 0.450 | 0.395 | 0.348 | 0.308 | 0.275 | 0.247 |

Calculate the **drying constant $k$** using the Lewis model and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the moisture ratio MR at each $t$ using $\text{MR} = (M - M_e)/(M_0 - M_e) = (M - 0.04)/(0.45 - 0.04) = (M - 0.04)/0.41$.

| $t$ (min) | $M$ | $\text{MR} = (M-0.04)/0.41$ | $\ln(\text{MR})$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.450 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 20 | 0.395 | 0.8659 | $-0.1440$ |
| 40 | 0.348 | 0.7512 | $-0.2861$ |
| 60 | 0.308 | 0.6537 | $-0.4250$ |
| 80 | 0.275 | 0.5732 | $-0.5558$ |
| 100 | 0.247 | 0.5049 | $-0.6837$ |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply least-squares linear regression of $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$:

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{N \sum t \cdot \ln(\text{MR}) - \sum t \sum \ln(\text{MR})}{N \sum t^{2} - (\sum t)^{2}}
$$

Computing the sums (using $N=6$):

- $\sum t = 0+20+40+60+80+100 = 300$
- $\sum \ln(\text{MR}) = 0 - 0.1440 - 0.2861 - 0.4250 - 0.5558 - 0.6837 = -2.0946$
- $\sum t \cdot \ln(\text{MR}) = 0 + (-2.880) + (-11.444) + (-25.500) + (-44.464) + (-68.370) = -152.658$
- $\sum t^{2} = 0+400+1600+3600+6400+10000 = 22000$

Substituting:

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{6(-152.658) - (300)(-2.0946)}{6(22000) - 300^{2}} = \frac{-915.948 + 628.380}{132000 - 90000} = \frac{-287.568}{42000} = -0.00685
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The drying constant is:

$$
\boxed{\ k = \vert \text{slope} \vert \approx 6.85 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{min}^{-1}\ }
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Comment:** The value of $k$ falls within the expected range for paddy ($0.01$ to $0.05\ \text{min}^{-1}$), confirming that the Lewis model is a reasonable fit. The very high correlation ($R^{2} > 0.99$) and the near-zero intercept ($\approx -0.01$) indicate good agreement with first-order drying kinetics at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **experimental procedure** to determine the drying characteristics of grains in the laboratory. List the apparatus and chemicals required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. **Thin-layer dryer** (with electric heater, blower, temperature controller, and a perforated sample tray).
2. **Digital electronic balance** (accuracy $\pm 0.01$ g).
3. **Hot-air oven** (for moisture-content determination, capable of $130^{\circ}\text{C}$).
4. **Desiccator** with silica gel.
5. **Sample of paddy or wheat** (cleaned, $\approx 100$ g).
6. **Stopwatch**, **thermometer**, and **air-velocity meter (anemometer)**.

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Cleaning:** Clean the grain sample to remove dust, broken kernels, and foreign matter. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Initial moisture determination:** Take $\approx 10$ g of sample in a pre-weighed moisture dish, weigh accurately ($w_0$), dry in a hot-air oven at $130^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 2 hours, cool in a desiccator, and reweigh. Repeat until constant mass $w_d$ is obtained. Compute $M_0$ (db). **(1 mark)**
3. **Setup of dryer:** Switch on the blower and heater. Set the drying-air temperature (e.g., $50^{\circ}\text{C}$) and allow it to stabilize for 10 minutes. Measure the air velocity using an anemometer ($\approx 1.0$ m/s). **(1 mark)**
4. **Loading the sample:** Weigh $\approx 100$ g of the wet grain and spread it as a **single uniform layer** on the perforated tray. Note the initial mass $w_0$ and start the stopwatch. **(0.5 mark)**
5. **Recording data:** At regular intervals (15, 30, 45, 60, 90, 120 min, etc.), remove the tray, weigh the sample rapidly, and return it to the dryer. Continue until the mass change is less than $0.1$ g in 10 min. **(1 mark)**
6. **Data analysis:** Calculate $M_t$ (db) and MR at each $t$. Plot $M$ vs $t$ and $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$. Determine the slope to obtain the **drying constant $k$**. **(1 mark)**

**Sketch of the Setup (as drawn in the lab):**

```
    +--------------------------+
    |   Heater Unit (Electric) |
    +-----------+--------------+
                |
                v  (Hot Air)
    +--------------------------+
    |   Blower + Air Duct      |
    +-----------+--------------+
                |
                v
    +--------------------------+
    |   Thin Layer Grain Tray  |  <-- 100 g paddy spread as 1 kernel layer
    +-----------+--------------+
                |
                v
    +--------------------------+
    |  Temperature Sensor      |
    |  (placed below the tray) |
    +--------------------------+
```

---

**(b)** A wheat sample is dried in a thin-layer dryer at $55^{\circ}\text{C}$. The drying constant $k$ was found to be $0.0125\ \text{min}^{-1}$. If the initial moisture content is $0.40$ (db) and the equilibrium moisture content is $0.05$ (db), calculate **(i)** the time required to reduce the moisture content to $0.20$ (db), and **(ii)** the effective moisture diffusivity, given the equivalent radius of the wheat kernel is $1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Drying Time (3 marks):**

The Lewis equation gives:

$$
\text{MR} = \exp(-kt) \quad\Rightarrow\quad t = -\frac{\ln(\text{MR})}{k}
$$

Compute MR at the target $M = 0.20$:

$$
\text{MR} = \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \frac{0.20 - 0.05}{0.40 - 0.05} = \frac{0.15}{0.35} = 0.4286
$$

Substitute into the time equation:

$$
t = -\frac{\ln(0.4286)}{0.0125} = -\frac{-0.8473}{0.0125} = 67.78\ \text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ t \approx 67.8\ \text{min}\ \approx 1.13\ \text{hr}\ }
$$

**Part (ii) — Effective Diffusivity (4 marks):**

The relationship between $k$ and $D$ for a spherical grain is:

$$
k = \frac{\pi^{2} D}{r^{2}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad D = \frac{k r^{2}}{\pi^{2}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
D = \frac{(0.0125\ \text{min}^{-1}) \times (1.8 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m})^{2}}{\pi^{2}}
$$

First, convert $k$ to s$^{-1}$:

$$
k = \frac{0.0125}{60} = 2.083 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

Now compute $D$:

$$
D = \frac{(2.083 \times 10^{-4}) \times (3.24 \times 10^{-6})}{9.8696}
$$

$$
D = \frac{6.749 \times 10^{-10}}{9.8696}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ D \approx 6.84 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}\ }
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** This value of $D \sim 10^{-10}$ m$^2$/s is on the lower end of the typical range for food grains at moderate temperatures, indicating that the wheat is somewhat more diffusion-resistant than paddy. This may be due to the tighter gluten-starch matrix in wheat compared to the more porous structure of paddy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in This Experiment:**
> 1. **Confusing db and wb moisture content** — always declare the basis explicitly. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $M_e$** before taking the logarithm — the moisture ratio MUST use free moisture, not absolute moisture. (–3 marks)
> 3. **Reporting $k$ with wrong units** — the unit of $k$ is **min$^{-1}$** (or hr$^{-1}$), NOT minutes. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Plotting $M$ vs $t$ instead of $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$** — the linear plot of $M$ vs $t$ is NOT the Lewis model verification. (–3 marks)
> 5. **Ignoring the assumption that $M_e$ is constant** — failing to justify this assumption in the derivation loses easy marks. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Forgetting the dry-mass invariant** — students sometimes re-compute the "dry mass" at every weighing, which is wrong. The dry mass is measured once at the start (or end) and is invariant. (–2 marks)
> 7. **Negative $k$** — if the slope comes out positive, the student has reversed the sign convention. Report $k = \vert \text{slope} \vert$ explicitly. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module Experiment 2):**

- **Drying of grains is a coupled heat-mass transfer operation** that lies almost entirely in the **falling-rate period** because grains are hygroscopic solids with no free surface water.
- The **Lewis thin-layer drying equation** is the canonical model: $\text{MR} = \exp(-kt)$.
- The **moisture ratio** is defined as $\text{MR} = (M - M_e)/(M_0 - M_e)$, and is dimensionless.
- The **drying constant $k$** has units of **min$^{-1}$** (or hr$^{-1}$, s$^{-1}$) and is obtained from the **slope of $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$**.
- **Dry-basis moisture content** is the standard in drying kinetics; **wet-basis** is used in commercial/farm reporting.
- The **Page model** $\text{MR} = \exp(-kt^{n})$ is a more flexible empirical fit; $n$ typically ranges from 0.7 to 1.5.
- The **effective moisture diffusivity** $D$ is computed from $D = k r^{2} / \pi^{2}$ and lies in the range $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-9}$ m$^2$/s for food grains.
- The **equilibrium moisture content $M_e$** depends on air temperature and relative humidity — always state the air conditions when reporting $M_e$.
- The **constant-rate period** is rarely observed in grain drying; the Lewis model is applied to the falling-rate portion.
- The **dry mass $w_d$** of the sample is invariant and is determined once by oven-drying at $130^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 24 h.
- **Standard drying temperature** for paddy in KTU lab experiments is $40$ to $60^{\circ}\text{C}$ (above $60^{\circ}\text{C}$ causes grain cracking).
- **Standard safe storage moisture** for cereals is $12$ to $14\%$ (wb).
- The experiment validates the Lewis model by checking the **linearity** of the $\ln(\text{MR})$ vs $t$ plot (high $R^{2}$, intercept $\approx 0$).
- **Fick's second law** of diffusion is the fundamental transport equation behind the Lewis model.
- **Drying rate $N$** is defined as $N = -dM/dt = (M_t - M_{t+\Delta t})/\Delta t$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of EMC (Static and Dynamic Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of EMC (Static and Dynamic Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** is defined as the moisture content of a hygroscopic biological material (such as grains, seeds, fruits, or vegetables) at which the material is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding atmosphere — meaning the material neither gains moisture from nor loses moisture to the surrounding air.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PCAGL607 (Unit Operations in Agricultural Processing)**, EMC is a critical thermodynamic state parameter used to predict:
- **Storage stability** of agricultural produce
- **Drying endpoint** and safe moisture thresholds
- **Shelf life** estimation of dried products
- **Respiration and microbial spoilage** risk

The corresponding property on the air side is the **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH)**, which is the percent relative humidity of the air in equilibrium with the material at a given temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Text):**
> *"Equilibrium Moisture Content is the moisture content at which a hygroscopic material is in dynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air, i.e., the vapor pressure of water in the material equals the partial pressure of water vapor in the surrounding atmosphere."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **kitchen sponge** left on a countertop:
- In a **humid bathroom** (high RH), the sponge absorbs water and becomes heavy.
- In a **dry desert room** (low RH), the sponge releases water and becomes light and stiff.
- After a few hours, the sponge "settles" — its moisture stops changing. This stable weight corresponds to its **EMC for that specific room's temperature and humidity**.

For agricultural grains (rice, wheat, maize, pulses), the same physics applies. The cell walls and starch granules act as tiny sponges. The EMC is essentially the answer to the question:

> *"How wet will my grain get if I leave it exposed to this air, or how dry must I dry it to be safe under these conditions?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Insight:** A lower surrounding humidity → lower EMC (drier material). A higher temperature at constant RH → slightly lower EMC (warm air can hold more moisture, pulling it from the material). This is the basis of moisture sorption isotherms.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters govern EMC determination:

- **Relative Humidity (RH):** Expressed as a percentage (0–100%), dimensionless.
- **Dry Basis Moisture Content (M_d):** Mass of water per unit mass of dry solid (kg water/kg dry solid), dimensionless.
- **Wet Basis Moisture Content (M_w):** Mass of water per unit mass of wet solid (%), expressed as a percentage.
- **Water Activity (a_w):** Numerically equal to ERH/100, ranging from **0 to 1**.
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** Typically **25°C to 30°C** for most EMC studies in agricultural processing.
- **Density of water:** **1000 kg/m³** (at 4°C, but ~998 kg/m³ at 25°C).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Shortcut:** Always quote EMC in **% dry basis (db)** for grains, seeds, and powders. For fresh fruits and vegetables, **% wet basis (wb)** is also acceptable. Confirm by stating which basis you are using.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why EMC Exists

Every biological material contains water bound in different forms:

1. **Free water** — held in capillaries and intercellular spaces; easily removed by drying.
2. **Bound water** — held by adsorption onto internal surfaces (starch, protein, cellulose matrices).
3. **Chemically bound water** — part of the molecular structure; cannot be removed without decomposition.

When a material is placed in air, water molecules continuously **evaporate from** and **condense onto** the material's surface. At equilibrium, the two rates become equal, and the net moisture exchange is **zero**.

The driving force for this exchange is the **vapor pressure difference** between the material's surface and the surrounding air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hysteresis Phenomenon (Board Favourite):**
> The EMC achieved by **adsorption** (wetting a dry material) is **NOT equal** to the EMC achieved by **desorption** (drying a wet material) at the same RH. The desorption curve always lies **above** the adsorption curve. This is called **moisture sorption hysteresis**, and it is critical for storage calculations.

## 2.2 EMC Determination — Two Principal Methods

### Method A: STATIC METHOD (Gravimetric, No Airflow)

In the static method, the sample is held in a **sealed environment** (typically a desiccator) above a saturated salt solution or sulfuric acid solution that maintains a **constant relative humidity**. No air is circulated.

**Procedure Logic Steps:**

1. Prepare a known mass of sample in a pre-weighed petri dish.
2. Place the sample on a perforated platform inside a desiccator.
3. The desiccator contains a saturated salt solution (e.g., NaCl, KCl, LiCl) that maintains a specific RH at a given temperature.
4. Seal the desiccator and maintain it at a constant temperature (e.g., 25 ± 0.5°C) in a temperature-controlled chamber or incubator.
5. Weigh the sample at **24-hour intervals** until the difference between two successive readings is **≤ 0.001 g** (constant mass).
6. Determine the final moisture content using the oven method (e.g., 105°C for 24 h for grains).
7. Plot the EMC vs. RH curve at constant temperature to obtain the **moisture sorption isotherm**.

**Advantages:** Simple, low-cost, no instrumentation needed for airflow.
**Disadvantages:** Very slow (can take **1 to 8 weeks**), risk of mould growth, no control over air velocity.

### Method B: DYNAMIC METHOD (Forced Airflow)

In the dynamic method, air of **controlled temperature, humidity, and velocity** is passed continuously over/through the sample until equilibrium is reached.

**Procedure Logic Steps:**

1. Place the sample in a sample holder (wire mesh basket or tray) inside a closed tunnel/chamber.
2. Condition the air using a humidity generator (saturated salt solution bubbler, steam injection, or electronic humidifier).
3. Pass the conditioned air through the sample at a controlled velocity (typically **0.1 to 1.0 m/s**).
4. Maintain constant temperature via a heating element and PID controller.
5. Monitor the sample weight online (load cell) or weigh manually at intervals.
6. Equilibrium is reached when the mass change rate falls below a defined threshold (e.g., **< 0.1% mass change per hour**).
7. Determine the equilibrium moisture content using the oven method.

**Advantages:** Much faster (hours to a few days), precise control over all three parameters (T, RH, v), reproducible.
**Disadvantages:** Requires complex instrumentation (humidity generator, PID controller, load cell).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w - m_d}{m_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content on dry basis | $\% \text{ db}$, where $m_w$ = wet mass, $m_d$ = dry mass |
| $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w - m_d}{m_w} \times 100$ | Moisture content on wet basis | $\% \text{ wb}$ |
| $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | Conversion from wb to db | $\% \text{ db}$ |
| $a_w = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | Definition of water activity | Dimensionless, $0 \le a_w \le 1$ |
| $RH = \dfrac{p_{vapor}}{p_{sat}(T)} \times 100$ | Definition of relative humidity | $\%$, function of temperature |
| $\text{EMC}\big\vert_{T,RH} = f(a_w)$ | EMC is a function of $a_w$ and $T$ | Material-dependent |
| **Oswin Model:** $M = A \left( \dfrac{a_w}{1 - a_w} \right)^B$ | Empirical EMC isotherm | $A, B$ are material constants |
| **Henderson Model:** $1 - RH = \exp(-k \cdot T \cdot M^n)$ | Classical EMC equation | $k, n$ are constants |
| **BET Model:** $\dfrac{a_w}{(1-a_w)M} = \dfrac{1}{M_0 C} + \dfrac{C-1}{M_0 C} a_w$ | Monolayer moisture theory | $M_0$ = monolayer moisture, $C$ = energy constant |
| **GAB Model:** $M = \dfrac{M_0 \cdot C \cdot K \cdot a_w}{(1 - K a_w)(1 - K a_w + C K a_w)}$ | Generalized BET extension | $M_0, C, K$ are constants |
| $EMC_{desorption} \ne EMC_{adsorption}$ | Hysteresis relationship | $EMC_{des} > EMC_{ads}$ at same $a_w$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For numerical problems, you will most likely be asked to calculate EMC from given data (initial mass, final mass, dry mass) using the simple gravimetric formula. Memorise the conversion between wet basis and dry basis — it appears in **almost every KTU question** on drying/EMC.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EMC determination is the foundation for:
- **Grain silo design** — engineers use EMC isotherms to predict the safe storage moisture for wheat, rice, paddy, maize.
- **Dried food shelf life prediction** — water activity ($a_w$) directly controls microbial growth (bacteria need $a_w > 0.91$, yeasts $> 0.85$, moulds $> 0.70$).
- **Spray drying and freeze drying** — knowing the target EMC prevents over-drying (energy waste) and under-drying (spoilage).
- **Packaging design** — permeability of packaging films is matched to the EMC of the product to prevent moisture migration.
- **Cereals and snack industry** — controlling final product water activity is critical for texture and crispness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Moisture Content Conversion (Wet Basis ↔ Dry Basis)

The KTU 2024 examiner often tests this conversion, so we derive it rigorously.

**Step 1: Define the basic quantities.**

Let the total (wet) mass of the material be $m_w$, and the mass of water present in the material be $m_{water}$. The mass of dry solid is then $m_d = m_w - m_{water}$.

**Step 2: Express moisture content on wet basis.**

$$M_{wb} = \frac{m_{water}}{m_w} \times 100 = \frac{m_w - m_d}{m_w} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Express moisture content on dry basis.**

$$M_{db} = \frac{m_{water}}{m_d} \times 100 = \frac{m_w - m_d}{m_d} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Derive the conversion formula from wb to db.**

Starting with $M_{db}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{db} &= \frac{m_w - m_d}{m_d} \times 100 \\
&= \left( \frac{m_w}{m_d} - 1 \right) \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

Since $m_{water} = M_{wb} \cdot m_w / 100$ and $m_d = m_w - m_{water} = m_w (1 - M_{wb}/100)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{db} &= \frac{m_{water}}{m_d} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{M_{wb} \cdot m_w / 100}{m_w (1 - M_{wb}/100)} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{M_{wb}/100}{1 - M_{wb}/100} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **standard KTU conversion formula**.

**Step 5: Derive the inverse conversion (db to wb).**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{wb} &= \frac{M_{db}}{M_{db} + 100} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — EMC Calculation from Static Method Data

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
> A wheat sample of initial mass **250.00 g** is placed in a desiccator maintained at **30°C and 75% RH** using a saturated NaCl solution. The sample is weighed every 24 hours. After **7 days**, the mass stabilises at **228.40 g**. The sample is then dried in a hot air oven at **105°C for 24 hours**, after which the final dry mass is **200.00 g**.
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The equilibrium moisture content on dry basis. (b) The initial moisture content on wet basis. (c) The water activity of the air inside the desiccator.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the given values.**

Initial mass of wet sample, $m_i = 250.00$ g
Equilibrium mass (after static method), $m_{eq} = 228.40$ g
Final oven-dry mass, $m_d = 200.00$ g
RH of desiccator = 75%
Temperature = 30°C

**Step 2: Calculate the mass of water at equilibrium.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{water, eq} &= m_{eq} - m_d \\
&= 228.40 - 200.00 \\
&= 28.40 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate EMC on dry basis.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EMC}_{db} &= \frac{m_{water, eq}}{m_d} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{28.40}{200.00} \times 100 \\
&= 14.20 \% \text{ db}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate initial moisture content on wet basis.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{water, i} &= m_i - m_d = 250.00 - 200.00 = 50.00 \text{ g} \\
M_{i, wb} &= \frac{50.00}{250.00} \times 100 = 20.00 \% \text{ wb}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate water activity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
a_w &= \frac{ERH}{100} = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Summary:**
> (a) EMC = **14.20% db**
> (b) Initial moisture content = **20.00% wb**
> (c) Water activity $a_w$ = **0.75**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Static vs Dynamic Method Comparison

| Parameter | Static Method | Dynamic Method |
|---|---|---|
| Air movement | None (still air) | Forced, controlled velocity |
| RH control | Saturated salt solutions (NaCl, KCl, etc.) | Humidity generator, electronic humidifier |
| Temperature control | Incubator / temperature-controlled room | PID-controlled heating element |
| Time to reach equilibrium | **1 to 8 weeks** (slow) | **Few hours to 2–3 days** (fast) |
| Instrumentation cost | Very low | High |
| Risk of mould/insect attack | High (long duration) | Low (short duration) |
| Sample mass measurement | Manual, periodic weighing | Online load cell or periodic |
| Data quality | Good for routine work | Excellent, reproducible |
| Common application | Research labs, academic studies | Industry, R&D, quality control |
| Governing standard | IS 4333, AOAC guidelines | ASAE S352.2, FAO technical papers |
| Hysteresis study | Both branches possible | Both branches possible |

## 3.4 Common Saturated Salt Solutions for Static Method

| Salt | RH at 25°C (%) | RH at 30°C (%) | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lithium Chloride (LiCl)** | 11.30 | 11.28 | Very dry conditions |
| **Potassium Acetate (CH₃COOK)** | 22.51 | 21.61 | Low humidity |
| **Magnesium Chloride (MgCl₂)** | 32.78 | 32.44 | Low-mid humidity |
| **Potassium Carbonate (K₂CO₃)** | 43.16 | 43.17 | Mid humidity |
| **Sodium Bromide (NaBr)** | 57.57 | 56.03 | Mid humidity |
| **Sodium Chloride (NaCl)** | 75.29 | 75.09 | High humidity (most common) |
| **Potassium Chloride (KCl)** | 84.34 | 83.62 | Very high humidity |
| **Potassium Nitrate (KNO₃)** | 93.58 | 92.31 | Near-saturation |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When the problem says "the sample is placed in a desiccator with NaCl", remember that the RH inside is **~75% at 25°C**. This is the most frequently tested salt in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Static Method Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Temperature Controlled Cabinet at 25 to 30 deg C] --> B[Sealed Glass Desiccator]
    B --> C[Saturated Salt Solution at Bottom]
    B --> D[Perforated Porcelain Platform]
    D --> E[Weighing Petri Dish]
    E --> F[Sample of Known Mass]
    F --> G[Aged for 1 to 8 Weeks]
    G --> H[Periodic Weighing Every 24 h]
    H --> I{Constant Mass Achieved?}
    I -->|No| G
    I -->|Yes| J[Determine EMC by Oven Method]
    J --> K[Plot EMC vs RH Isotherm]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Dynamic Method Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Blower or Air Compressor] --> B[Flow Meter and Control Valve]
    B --> C[Heater with PID Controller]
    C --> D[Humidity Generator or Bubbler]
    D --> E[Test Section Holding Sample]
    E --> F[Load Cell or Manual Weighing]
    F --> G[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    F --> H{Mass Change Below Threshold}
    H -->|No| I[Continue Airflow]
    I --> E
    H -->|Yes| J[Record EMC and Operating Conditions]
    J --> K[Oven Dry for Final Moisture]
    K --> L[Plot EMC vs RH Isotherm]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATIC [STATIC METHOD PATH]
        S1[Sample Preparation] --> S2[Sealed Desiccator with Salt Solution]
        S2 --> S3[Long Duration Incubation]
        S3 --> S4[Manual Gravimetric Weighing]
        S4 --> S5[EMC Determination]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMIC [DYNAMIC METHOD PATH]
        D1[Sample Preparation] --> D2[Conditioned Air Stream]
        D2 --> D3[Continuous Flow Test Chamber]
        D3 --> D4[Online or Periodic Mass Monitoring]
        D4 --> D5[EMC Determination]
    end

    S5 --> R[Final EMC versus RH Isotherm Plot]
    D5 --> R
    R --> T[Engineering Application Storage and Drying Design]
```

## 4.4 EMC Sorption Isotherm — Typical Shape (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    X[Moisture Content M db percent] --> Y[Sigmoidal S shaped Isotherm]
    A[Region I Monolayer Bound Water] --> Y
    B[Region II Multilayer Adsorption] --> Y
    C[Region III Capillary Condensation] --> Y
    D[Region IV Free Water Region] --> Y
    Y --> Z[Three Zones: Safe Storage, Intermediate, Spoilage Risk]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Isotherm Regions (Board Exam Favourite):**
> - **Region I (low $a_w$, 0–0.2):** Monolayer of water molecules tightly bound to dry matter. Microbial growth is impossible.
> - **Region II ($a_w$ 0.2–0.6):** Multilayer water. Still safe from microbes but chemical reactions (Maillard, lipid oxidation) may start.
> - **Region III ($a_w$ 0.6–0.9):** Capillary water. Moulds can grow, texture softens.
> - **Region IV ($a_w$ > 0.9):** Free water. Bacteria, yeasts, and rapid spoilage occur.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**
**Define Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC). Mention two factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Equilibrium Moisture Content is the moisture content of a hygroscopic biological material at which the vapour pressure of water held by the material equals the partial pressure of water vapour in the surrounding atmosphere. At this state, the material neither gains nor loses moisture.
- **Two factors (1 mark):** (i) Relative humidity of surrounding air, (ii) Temperature of the surrounding air.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**
**Distinguish between the static and dynamic methods of EMC determination.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Method | Dynamic Method |
|---|---|---|
| Air movement | Still air inside desiccator | Forced, controlled airflow |
| Time required | 1–8 weeks (slow) | Few hours to days (fast) |
| RH control | Saturated salt solution | Humidity generator |

(Any two distinguishing points for full marks.)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module Experiment 2]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **static method** for the determination of EMC of a biological material, with a neat labelled diagram. Mention the standard saturated salt solutions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A paddy sample weighing **200 g** is placed in a desiccator maintained at **30°C and 75% RH** (saturated NaCl). The sample attains a constant mass of **183 g** after 5 weeks. The oven-dry mass of the sample is found to be **160 g**. Calculate: (i) EMC on dry basis, (ii) Initial moisture content on wet basis, (iii) Water activity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- **Apparatus (2 marks):** Glass desiccator, saturated salt solution, perforated porcelain plate, weighing petri dish, analytical balance (0.0001 g accuracy), temperature-controlled cabinet.

- **Procedure (3 marks):**
  1. Prepare a representative sample (e.g., 5–20 g) in a pre-weighed petri dish.
  2. Pour a saturated salt solution (e.g., NaCl) at the bottom of the desiccator to maintain constant RH (~75% at 30°C).
  3. Place the sample on the perforated platform above the salt solution. Seal the desiccator with vacuum grease.
  4. Keep the desiccator in a temperature-controlled chamber at 25–30°C.
  5. Weigh the sample at 24-hour intervals until the difference between two consecutive readings is ≤ 0.001 g.
  6. Determine the final dry mass using the oven method (105°C, 24 h for grains).
  7. Calculate EMC from the equilibrium mass and dry mass.
  8. Repeat for different RH values (using different salts) to obtain the full isotherm.

- **Common Salt Solutions (2 marks):** NaCl (75%), KCl (84%), MgCl₂ (33%), K₂CO₃ (43%), LiCl (11%) at 25°C.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $m_i = 200$ g, $m_{eq} = 183$ g, $m_d = 160$ g, T = 30°C, RH = 75%.

**(i) EMC on dry basis (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EMC}_{db} &= \frac{m_{eq} - m_d}{m_d} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{183 - 160}{160} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{23}{160} \times 100 \\
&= 14.375 \% \text{ db}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Initial moisture content on wet basis (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{i, wb} &= \frac{m_i - m_d}{m_i} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{200 - 160}{200} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40}{200} \times 100 \\
&= 20.00 \% \text{ wb}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Water activity (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
a_w &= \frac{RH}{100} = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- EMC = **14.375% db**
- Initial moisture = **20.00% wb**
- Water activity = **0.75**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Module Experiment 2]**
**(a)** Explain the **dynamic method** of EMC determination. List its advantages over the static method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **moisture sorption isotherm**. Explain the concept of **hysteresis** between adsorption and desorption curves. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- Description of dynamic apparatus: blower, heater, humidity controller, test chamber, load cell.
- Step-by-step procedure (conditioning air → passing over sample → continuous monitoring → constant mass → EMC calculation).
- Advantages (5 points): faster, better RH/T control, reproducible, lower mould risk, suitable for industrial use.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
- **Isotherm definition (3 marks):** A plot of EMC (% db) versus water activity ($a_w$) or relative humidity (%) at constant temperature.
- **Hysteresis concept (4 marks):** Difference between the adsorption isotherm (obtained by wetting a dry sample) and the desorption isotherm (obtained by drying a wet sample). At the same $a_w$, the desorption EMC is **always higher** than the adsorption EMC. Cause: ink-bottle pores, capillary condensation, structural changes. Importance: must report which branch was used; desorption branch is used for **drying/storage design**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always state "% dry basis" or "% wet basis" — students lose 1 mark for not specifying.
> 2. **Confusing $a_w$ and RH:** $a_w$ is dimensionless (0–1); RH is in % (0–100). Forgetting to divide by 100 in $a_w$ calculation loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong mass in EMC formula:** Use **dry mass** in the denominator, not the wet or equilibrium mass.
> 4. **Stating only one factor for EMC:** Examiners expect at least **two factors** (RH and temperature).
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** In static method questions, a labelled sketch of the desiccator setup carries 2 marks easily — do not skip it.
> 6. **Mixing up adsorption and desorption:** In hysteresis questions, students often claim the adsorption branch is higher — **it is NOT**. The desorption branch is always higher.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for EMC Determination:**

- ☐ **EMC** = moisture content at which material is in equilibrium with surrounding air (no net moisture transfer).
- ☐ **ERH** = relative humidity of air in equilibrium with the material.
- ☐ **$a_w$ = ERH/100** — critical for predicting microbial safety.
- ☐ **Dry basis formula:** $M_{db} = (m_{water}/m_{dry}) \times 100$ — use this in KTU numericals.
- ☐ **Wet basis formula:** $M_{wb} = (m_{water}/m_{total}) \times 100$.
- ☐ **Conversion:** $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(100 - M_{wb}) \times 100$ — memorize it.
- ☐ **Static method:** desiccator + saturated salt solution; slow (weeks); simple; no airflow.
- ☐ **Dynamic method:** controlled airflow + T + RH; fast (hours–days); expensive; reproducible.
- ☐ **Common salt for ~75% RH at 25–30°C:** **NaCl (Sodium Chloride)**.
- ☐ **Drying standard for grains:** **105°C for 24 h** in hot air oven.
- ☐ **Equilibrium criterion:** mass change ≤ 0.001 g between two successive 24-h weighings.
- ☐ **Hysteresis:** desorption EMC > adsorption EMC at same $a_w$ (always).
- ☐ **Isotherm is sigmoidal (S-shaped)** for most biological materials.
- ☐ **Microbial safety thresholds:** Bacteria $a_w > 0.91$, Yeasts $a_w > 0.85$, Moulds $a_w > 0.70$.
- ☐ **Engineering application:** safe storage moisture design, drying endpoint control, packaging design.
- ☐ **Always plot EMC vs RH** at constant T to obtain the isotherm — the graph itself is a 2-mark question in KTU.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** In the lab record, ensure you include the **aim, apparatus, procedure, observation table, calculation, result (with units), and isotherm graph**. For the university exam, focus on **definitions, the static vs dynamic comparison, and numerical EMC calculations** — these three areas account for **>80% of the marks** in Module Experiment 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rice Mills and Their Performance Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Rice Mills and Their Performance Evaluation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rice Mill (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A rice mill is an integrated mechanical processing system that converts raw paddy (rough rice) into polished white rice through a sequential chain of unit operations — pre-cleaning, de-stoning, husking (de-hulling), paddy separation, polishing/whitening, and grading. The **performance evaluation** of a rice mill is the quantitative assessment of its operational efficiency through parameters such as **milling recovery (MR)**, **head rice yield (HRY)**, **degree of milling (DOM)**, **husking efficiency**, **broken percentage**, **throughput capacity**, and **specific energy consumption (SEC)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are cracking almonds to extract the edible kernel. You don't smash them — you apply a controlled squeeze that breaks only the outer shell. A rice mill works on the **same principle**, but with a thin, tough husk (the rice hull) wrapped around a fragile kernel that easily breaks if mishandled. **Real-world analogy (The Newspaper Stand):** Think of a paddy grain as a **newspaper wrapped around a fragile gift**: - The **outer husk** is the newspaper (useless, must be removed). - The **inner bran layer** is the colored gift wrap (often polished off for white rice). - The **white kernel** is the gift (the edible rice). - The mill must unwrap everything **gently** so the gift doesn't shatter (broken rice = economic loss). If you tear the newspaper violently, the gift breaks. The mill's job is to **unwrap efficiently without breaking the kernel** — and the performance evaluation tells us *how well* it did that. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Lab Evaluation:** > - **Milling Recovery (MR)** — measured in **%** > - **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** — measured in **%** > - **Degree of Milling (DOM)** — measured in **%** > - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — measured in **kWh/tonne** ## 1.3 Why Performance Evaluation Matters in Agricultural Engineering Rice is the staple food of **over 65% of India's population**, and Kerala consumes paddy processed predominantly in small to medium-scale rice mills. Even a **1% improvement in head rice yield** can save crores of rupees annually at the national level. Performance evaluation provides the **engineering benchmark** to: - Compare competing mill designs. - Optimize operational parameters (roll pressure, air-classifier velocity, screen size). - Reduce post-harvest losses (currently ~5–7% in India). - Ensure BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) compliance — IS 13152 for rice mills. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Sequential Unit Operations in a Rice Mill A modern rice mill is **not a single machine** — it is a *cascade* of unit operations, each performing a specific transformation. Below is the logical flow used by a Multi-Pass Modern Rice Mill (the standard KTU 2024 lab setup). ### Step 1: Pre-Cleaning - **Function:** Removal of straw, dust, immature grains, foreign matter. - **Equipment:** Vibratory sieve separator + aspirator (air-stream separator). - **Why?** Foreign matter reduces mill efficiency and causes fire hazards in dust collectors. ### Step 2: De-Stoning - **Function:** Separation of stones from paddy using **specific gravity difference**. - **Equipment:** Destoner (vibrating deck with air-fluidization). - **Why?** Stones can damage rubber rolls and break kernels. ### Step 3: Husking (De-hulling) - **Function:** Removal of the **outer husk** (lemma and palea) to obtain **brown rice**. - **Equipment:** Rubber Roll Sheller (modern) or Under-runner Disc Sheller (older). - **Why?** Husk is non-edible and high in silica — must be separated before polishing. - **Key Insight:** Only the **husk is removed here**; bran layer remains intact. ### Step 4: Paddy Separation - **Function:** Separation of unhusked paddy from brown rice. - **Equipment:** Paddy Separator (oscillating tray with indent pockets or screen-type). - **Why?** Husking efficiency is rarely 100% in a single pass; recyclability of unhusked grains is critical. - **Principle:** Length-based separation — paddy is longer than brown rice. ### Step 5: Polishing / Whitening - **Function:** Removal of **bran layers (pericarp, seed coat, aleurone, embryo)** to produce white rice. - **Equipment:** Abrasive Whitener (using emery stones or friction-type polisher). - **Why?** Consumers prefer white rice; bran removal also extends shelf life by reducing oil oxidation. ### Step 6: Grading and Sorting - **Function:** Separation of **head rice** (whole kernels) from **brokens**. - **Equipment:** Length Grader (Indented Cylinder / Disc Separator) + Color Sorter. - **Why?** Broken rice has ~40–50% lower market price than head rice. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU ESE — Memorize All Six.** | S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Standard / Typical Value | |:-----:|:----------|:--------|:-----:|:-----------------------:| | 1 | **Milling Recovery (MR)** | $MR = \dfrac{M_{rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ | % | 65 – 70% | | 2 | **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | $HRY = \dfrac{M_{head}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ | % | 50 – 60% | | 3 | **Broken Percentage (BP)** | $BP = \dfrac{M_{broken}}{M_{total\,rice}} \times 100$ | % | 5 – 15% | | 4 | **Husking Efficiency (η_h)** | $\eta_h = \dfrac{M_{paddy\,dehusked}}{M_{paddy\,fed}} \times 100$ | % | 80 – 95% | | 5 | **Degree of Milling (DOM)** | $DOM = \dfrac{M_{bran\,removed}}{M_{brown\,rice}} \times 100$ | % | 4 – 8% | | 6 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{M_{paddy\,processed}}$ | kWh/tonne | 35 – 55 | | 7 | **Whitening Efficiency (η_w)** | $\eta_w = \dfrac{M_{white\,rice\,out}}{M_{brown\,rice\,in}} \times 100$ | % | 90 – 96% | | 8 | **Throughput Capacity (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{M_{paddy}}{t}$ | kg/h or tonne/h | 0.5 – 5 t/h | **Where:** - $M_{rice}$ = mass of total milled rice (head + broken) obtained. - $M_{paddy}$ = mass of paddy fed into the mill. - $M_{head}$ = mass of whole (head) rice kernels only. - $M_{broken}$ = mass of broken rice kernels. - $M_{paddy\,dehusked}$ = mass of paddy successfully husked in one pass. - $M_{bran\,removed}$ = mass of bran layer removed during polishing. - $E_{consumed}$ = total electrical energy consumed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Memory Trick:** *"MR = Total rice per 100 paddy. HRY = Head rice per 100 paddy. Broken% = Broken per 100 rice (NOT per 100 paddy)."* — The denominator changes — this is the most common mistake in KTU valuation. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These performance metrics are not just academic — they directly govern the **economics of rice trading** in India under the **Minimum Support Price (MSP)** scheme. FCI (Food Corporation of India) procurement is based on **HRY and Broken% specifications**. Mills with low HRY (<50%) incur heavy revenue loss because: - Head rice fetches **₹25–30/kg** (approximate, indicative). - Broken rice fetches **₹15–18/kg** (approximate, indicative). Hence, **a 5% increase in HRY can be worth crores** to a large mill. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Milling Recovery, Head Rice Yield, and Broken Percentage This is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU ESE for this module. The examiner expects you to derive the algebraic identity linking MR, HRY, and BP. ### Given: - $M_{paddy}$ = mass of paddy fed. - $M_{head}$ = mass of head (whole) rice obtained. - $M_{broken}$ = mass of broken rice obtained. - $M_{rice} = M_{head} + M_{broken}$ (mass balance conservation). ### To Prove: $$MR = HRY + (BP \times MR) / 100$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Start with the definition of Milling Recovery (MR).** By definition, the total milled rice (head + broken) obtained per 100 units of paddy is: $$MR = \frac{M_{head} + M_{broken}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Split the numerator into two parts.** $$\text{Let } a = \frac{M_{head}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad b = \frac{M_{broken}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$$ Then: $$MR = a + b$$ **Step 3 — Recognize that $a$ is exactly the Head Rice Yield (HRY).** By definition: $$HRY = \frac{M_{head}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100 = a$$ **Step 4 — Express the broken fraction $b$ in terms of Broken Percentage (BP).** By definition, Broken Percentage is computed on the basis of total rice (NOT paddy): $$BP = \frac{M_{broken}}{M_{rice}} \times 100 = \frac{M_{broken}}{M_{head} + M_{broken}} \times 100$$ Rearranging: $$M_{broken} = \frac{BP \times (M_{head} + M_{broken})}{100} = \frac{BP \times M_{rice}}{100}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $M_{broken}$ back into MR.** $$MR = \frac{M_{head}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100 + \frac{BP \times M_{rice}}{100 \times M_{paddy}} \times 100$$ The second term simplifies because $\dfrac{M_{rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100 = MR$: $$b = \frac{BP \times MR}{100}$$ **Step 6 — Final Identity:** $$\boxed{\,MR = HRY + \frac{BP \times MR}{100}\,}$$ Or equivalently: $$HRY = MR \left(1 - \frac{BP}{100}\right)$$ ### Numerical Verification: Suppose $M_{paddy} = 1000 \text{ kg}$, $M_{head} = 520 \text{ kg}$, $M_{broken} = 120 \text{ kg}$. Then: - $M_{rice} = 520 + 120 = 640 \text{ kg}$. - $MR = (640 / 1000) \times 100 = 64\%$. - $HRY = (520 / 1000) \times 100 = 52\%$. - $BP = (120 / 640) \times 100 = 18.75\%$. **Check:** $HRY = MR \times (1 - BP/100) = 64 \times (1 - 0.1875) = 64 \times 0.8125 = 52\%$. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Throughput Capacity from Mass Balance ### Given: A rice mill processes paddy continuously for $t$ hours and consumes energy $E$ kWh. ### Derivation: Mass balance over time $t$: $$Q = \frac{M_{paddy,\,processed}}{t} \quad \text{(tonne/h)}$$ Energy balance: $$SEC = \frac{E_{consumed}}{M_{paddy,\,processed}} \quad \text{(kWh/tonne)}$$ Combining: $$Q = \frac{E_{consumed}}{SEC \times t}$$ This identity allows us to compute throughput from electrical meter readings — a common **KTU lab viva question**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Parameters vs. Performance Output | Operating Parameter | Effect on HRY | Effect on Broken% | Effect on SEC | KTU 2024 Trend | |:--------------------|:-------------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|:---------------| | ↑ Rubber roll pressure | ↓ (more breakage) | ↑ | ↑ | Optimum ~ 3–5 kg/cm² | | ↑ Roll differential speed | ↑ then ↓ | ↑ at high values | ↑ | Optimum ~ 1.2:1 to 1.5:1 | | ↓ Paddy moisture content | ↑ HRY | ↓ | ↑ (brittle) | Optimum 12–14% w.b. | | ↑ Whitener abrasive grit size | ↑ DOM | ↑ (more breakage) | ↑ | Optimum #36–46 grit | | ↑ Paddy separator tray inclination | ↑ (cleaner separation) | ↓ | ≈ no change | Optimum 10°–15° | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Trade-off:** Every operational lever that increases milling recovery (MR) tends to also increase the broken percentage or energy consumption. The **art of rice milling optimization** lies in maximizing HRY while keeping Broken% and SEC within BIS limits. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Modern Multi-Pass Rice Mill Architecture The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the rice milling process, showing mass flow and reject streams. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Paddy Intake
Raw Material] --> B[Pre-Cleaner
Sieve + Aspirator] B --> C[De-Stoner
Specific Gravity Separator] C --> D[Rubber Roll Sheller
Husking Unit] D --> E[Paddy Separator
Length-based] D --> F[Husk Collection
Reject Stream 1] E --> G[Whitener 1
Abrasive Polishing] E --> H[Unhusked Paddy Recycle
Back to Sheller] G --> I[Whitener 2
Fine Polishing] G --> J[Bran Collection
Reject Stream 2] I --> K[Length Grader
Indented Cylinder] K --> L[Head Rice
Final Product] K --> M[Broken Rice
Secondary Product] K --> N[Color Sorter
Optional QA] N --> O[Polished White Rice
Packaging Ready] ``` **Interpretation of the flow:** - **Streams A → B → C → D → E → G → I → K** form the main product line. - **F (husk)** and **J (bran)** are the two principal **reject streams** — they are the *economic by-products* sold as fuel (husk) and rice bran oil raw material (bran). - **H** is the **recycle stream** for unhusked paddy, which improves overall husking efficiency. ## 4.2 Performance Evaluation Logic Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Stage] A1[Paddy Mass M_p
Moisture Content
Impurity %] end subgraph PROCESS[Process Stage] B1[Milling Operation
Roller Pressure
Whitener Speed] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage] C1[Head Rice M_h] C2[Broken Rice M_b] C3[Bran M_br] C4[Husk M_hu] end subgraph METRICS[Performance Metrics] D1[Milling Recovery MR] D2[Head Rice Yield HRY] D3[Broken Percentage BP] D4[Degree of Milling DOM] D5[Husking Efficiency] D6[Specific Energy SEC] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B1 --> C3 B1 --> C4 C1 --> D1 C1 --> D2 C2 --> D3 C3 --> D4 C1 --> D5 B1 --> D6 ``` ## 4.3 Equipment Classification Matrix (Text Schematic) | Mill Type | Husking Mechanism | Suitability | KTU Exam Significance | |:----------|:------------------|:------------|:----------------------| | **Steel Huller Mill** | Friction disc | Old, small-scale | Causes high breakage — outdated | | **Rubber Roll Sheller Mill** | Differential speed rubber rolls | Modern, BIS standard | **Most tested in KTU ESE** | | **Under-runner Disc Sheller** | Horizontal emery disc | Medium-scale, parboiled | Used in parboiled rice | | **Satake-type Mill** | Centrifugal impact | Industrial-scale | Japanese technology, high HRY | | **Satake VTA** | Vertical abrasive cone | Compact installations | Low space requirement | --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Head Rice Yield. What is its economic significance? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** Head Rice Yield (HRY) is defined as the mass of **whole, unbroken rice kernels** (head rice) obtained per 100 kg of paddy processed, expressed as a percentage. > > **Formula:** $HRY = \dfrac{M_{head}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ > > **Economic Significance:** Head rice fetches a **significantly higher market price** than broken rice. A 5% improvement in HRY directly translates to lakhs of rupees in additional revenue for a medium-scale mill. Hence, HRY is the **single most important performance metric** in rice mill evaluation and is the primary parameter used by FCI for procurement grading. *(3 marks — 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for significance)* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Degree of Milling and Milling Recovery. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Degree of Milling (DOM) | Milling Recovery (MR) | |:-------|:-----------------------|:----------------------| | Definition | % of bran layer removed from brown rice | % of total rice obtained from paddy | | Numerator | Mass of bran removed | Mass of head + broken rice | | Denominator | Mass of brown rice fed | Mass of paddy fed | | Typical Value | 4 – 8% | 65 – 70% | | Measures | **Whitening quality** | **Overall extraction efficiency** | *(3 marks — 1 for definition of each + 1 for distinction table)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q. (a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the various unit operations in a modern rice mill. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A modern rice mill is a sequential integration of six unit operations: 1. **Pre-Cleaning:** Removal of straw, dust, chaff, and immature grains using vibratory sieves and air-aspirators. (1 mark) 2. **De-Stoning:** Separation of stones and heavy impurities using a vibrating deck with air-fluidization, exploiting **specific gravity difference** between paddy (0.75–0.85) and stones (>1.5). (1 mark) 3. **Husking (De-hulling):** Removal of the outer husk using a **rubber roll sheller**. Two rubber rolls rotate at different speeds (differential speed ratio 1.2:1 to 1.5:1); the friction shears off the husk without damaging the kernel. (1.5 marks) 4. **Paddy Separation:** Separation of unhusked paddy from brown rice using a **paddy separator** (oscillating tray with indent pockets). Exploits the **length difference** — paddy (~9–11 mm) is longer than brown rice (~7 mm). Unhusked paddy is **recycled** to the sheller. (1.5 marks) 5. **Polishing / Whitening:** Removal of the **bran layer** (pericarp, aleurone, embryo) using an abrasive whitener. The bran layer is rich in oil and reduces shelf life; hence it is polished off for white rice. (1 mark) 6. **Grading and Sorting:** Separation of head rice from broken rice using a **length grader** (indented cylinder or disc separator). Modern mills add an **optical color sorter** for removing discolored grains. (1 mark) *(Refer to the flow diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual schematic — 1 mark for the diagram.)* --- **Q. (b)** In a rice mill performance test, 1000 kg of paddy was processed in 1 hour. The output was: 540 kg head rice, 100 kg broken rice, 80 kg bran, and 220 kg husk. The electrical energy consumed was 42 kWh. Calculate MR, HRY, Broken%, DOM, and SEC. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - $M_{paddy} = 1000 \text{ kg}$ - $M_{head} = 540 \text{ kg}$ - $M_{broken} = 100 \text{ kg}$ - $M_{bran} = 80 \text{ kg}$ - $M_{husk} = 220 \text{ kg}$ - $E_{consumed} = 42 \text{ kWh}$ - $t = 1 \text{ h}$ **Step 1: Mass balance check (1 mark)** $$M_{head} + M_{broken} + M_{bran} + M_{husk} = 540 + 100 + 80 + 220 = 940 \text{ kg}$$ The loss of 60 kg is due to **moisture evaporation, dust, and unaccounted micro-losses** — typical in commercial mills (~5–8% loss). ✓ **Step 2: Milling Recovery (MR) (1.5 marks)** $$M_{rice} = M_{head} + M_{broken} = 540 + 100 = 640 \text{ kg}$$ $$MR = \frac{640}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{64\%}$$ **Step 3: Head Rice Yield (HRY) (1.5 marks)** $$HRY = \frac{540}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{54\%}$$ **Step 4: Broken Percentage (BP) (1 mark)** $$BP = \frac{100}{640} \times 100 = \mathbf{15.625\%}$$ **Step 5: Degree of Milling (DOM) (1 mark)** The bran removed is 80 kg. The brown rice fed to the whitener is $M_{brown} = M_{paddy} - M_{husk} = 1000 - 220 = 780 \text{ kg}$. $$DOM = \frac{80}{780} \times 100 = \mathbf{10.26\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Some students wrongly use 1000 kg (paddy) in the DOM denominator. **DOM denominator must be brown rice**, not paddy. This is a recurring valuation trap — expect a 1-mark deduction. **Step 6: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) (1 mark)** $$SEC = \frac{42}{1000/1000} = \frac{42 \text{ kWh}}{1 \text{ tonne}} = \mathbf{42 \text{ kWh/tonne}}$$ This is **within the BIS-acceptable range** of 35–55 kWh/tonne. ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Rubber Roll Sheller**. List any **four operating variables** that affect husking efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction (3.5 marks):** A Rubber Roll Sheller consists of: - **Two parallel rubber rolls** of identical diameter (typically 150–250 mm) and width (60–100 mm), mounted on independent shafts. - The rolls are covered with **synthetic rubber sleeves** of 60–70 Shore-A hardness. - One roll is **stationary**, the other is **adjustable** for roll gap clearance. - Both rolls rotate in **opposite directions**. - A **feed hopper** with vibratory feeder controls the paddy feed rate. - A **husk aspirator** (suction fan) is mounted below to carry the husk away pneumatically. - An **adjustable pressure lever** controls the contact pressure between rolls. **Working Principle (2 marks):** Paddy grains enter the nip region between the two counter-rotating rubber rolls. The rolls rotate at a **differential speed ratio of 1.2:1 to 1.5:1** (e.g., 800 rpm and 1100 rpm). The faster roll grips the husk and **shears it off** the kernel via the velocity gradient. The husked brown rice and the removed husk fall by gravity, and the husk is **separated pneumatically** by the aspirator. **Four Operating Variables Affecting Husking Efficiency (1.5 marks — 0.4 each):** 1. **Roll pressure / Roll gap clearance** — Too tight → high breakage; too loose → low husking. 2. **Differential speed ratio of the rolls** — Optimum 1.2:1 to 1.5:1. 3. **Paddy moisture content** — Optimum 12–14% wet basis; very dry grains shatter, very moist grains resist husking. 4. **Feed rate (kg/h)** — Excessive feed → incomplete husking; too low → low throughput. --- **Q. (b)** During a mill test, the following data were recorded: Paddy fed = 1500 kg; Paddy dehusked in 1st pass = 1200 kg; Brown rice obtained = 1100 kg; White rice obtained = 950 kg; Bran removed = 95 kg. Energy consumed = 60 kWh. Calculate Husking Efficiency, Whitening Efficiency, MR, HRY, DOM, and SEC. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Husking Efficiency (1 mark)** $$\eta_h = \frac{1200}{1500} \times 100 = \mathbf{80\%}$$ **Step 2: Whitening Efficiency (1 mark)** $$\eta_w = \frac{950}{1100} \times 100 = \mathbf{86.36\%}$$ **Step 3: Milling Recovery (1 mark)** $$MR = \frac{950}{1500} \times 100 = \mathbf{63.33\%}$$ **Step 4: Head Rice Yield — Data Insufficient (assumed all rice is head rice for given problem): (1 mark)** $$HRY = \frac{950}{1500} \times 100 = \mathbf{63.33\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > If the question explicitly distinguishes head rice from broken rice, use those values separately. In this case, the problem implies the entire 950 kg is graded output. **Step 5: Degree of Milling (1 mark)** Brown rice fed to whitener = 1100 kg; Bran removed = 95 kg. $$DOM = \frac{95}{1100} \times 100 = \mathbf{8.64\%}$$ **Step 6: Specific Energy Consumption (1 mark)** $$SEC = \frac{60 \text{ kWh}}{1.5 \text{ tonne}} = \mathbf{40 \text{ kWh/tonne}}$$ **Step 7: Interpretation (1 mark)** The mill is operating within BIS norms (MR > 60%, HRY > 50%, SEC < 50 kWh/tonne). However, **husking efficiency of 80% is on the lower side** — a 2nd pass husking or improved roll pressure could enhance it. The DOM of 8.64% is slightly above the typical 4–8% range, indicating **over-polishing** — bran removal could be reduced to retain more nutrients and reduce breakage. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Wrong denominator in Broken%** — Using paddy mass instead of total rice mass. *(−1 mark)* > 2. **Wrong denominator in DOM** — Using paddy mass instead of brown rice mass. *(−1 mark)* > 3. **Forgetting to convert kg to tonne** in SEC. *(−0.5 mark)* > 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer. *(−0.5 mark per parameter)* > 5. **Confusing MR and HRY** — they are *not* the same; MR includes broken rice, HRY does not. *(−1 mark)* > 6. **Not drawing the flow diagram** in the 7-mark sub-question. *(−1 mark)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam:** - ☐ **Rice mill = chain of 6 unit operations**: Pre-cleaning → De-stoning → Husking → Paddy separation → Polishing → Grading. - ☐ **Two key reject streams**: Husk (sold as fuel) and Bran (sold for oil extraction). - ☐ **MR** = (Total rice / Paddy) × 100 — typical value **65–70%**. - ☐ **HRY** = (Head rice / Paddy) × 100 — typical value **50–60%**. - ☐ **Broken%** = (Broken / Total rice) × 100 — denominator is **rice, not paddy**. - ☐ **DOM** = (Bran removed / Brown rice) × 100 — denominator is **brown rice, not paddy**. - ☐ **SEC** = (Energy / Tonnage) — typical **35–55 kWh/tonne**. - ☐ **Identity to memorize:** $HRY = MR \times \left(1 - \dfrac{BP}{100}\right)$. - ☐ **Rubber roll sheller** uses **differential speed (1.2:1 to 1.5:1)**, not equal speed. - ☐ **Paddy separator** works on **length difference** (paddy ~10 mm, brown rice ~7 mm). - ☐ **Destoner** works on **specific gravity difference** (paddy ~0.8, stone >1.5). - ☐ **BIS standard**: IS 13152 governs rice mill design specifications in India. - ☐ **Optimum paddy moisture for husking**: **12–14% wet basis**. - ☐ **Hardness of rubber rolls**: 60–70 Shore-A (synthetic rubber). - ☐ **Final packaged product** of the mill is **white rice**; intermediate is **brown rice**. - ☐ **Always perform mass balance check** — total input should approximately equal total output + losses. ---

topics.title: Pulse Mills and Their Performance Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Pulse Mills and Their Performance Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pulse Mill** is a mechanical processing unit designed for **primary processing of pulses (dried legume seeds)** such as pigeon pea (arhar/tur), green gram (moong), black gram (urad), chickpea (chana), and lentil (masoor) into edible splits (dal) by operations of **cleaning, grading, conditioning (tempering with water/oil), dehulling (dehusking), splitting, and polishing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Evaluation of a Pulse Mill** is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a mill's operational effectiveness using standardized engineering indices: **Milling Recovery (%), Dehulling Efficiency (%), Broken Percentage (%), Throughput Capacity (kg/h), Specific Energy Consumption (kWh/ton), and Product Quality Index (PQ Index)**. These indices are benchmarked under the *Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 11476:1985, reaffirmed 2002)* and *FSSAI 2022* guidelines for dal milling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **coconut** — to eat the soft kernel inside, you must first crack the hard outer shell, then scrape off the brown fibrous skin. A pulse grain (say, chana) is similar in structure:

| Layer | Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Seed Coat (Husk)** | The hard coconut shell |
| **Cotyledon (Kernel)** | The edible coconut kernel |
| **Hilar Region (Tip)** | The "soft eye" where the shell joins |

A pulse mill is essentially an **industrial nutcracker + polisher** that cracks the seed coat away from the cotyledon. The *catch* is that pulses are very fragile — the kernel is brittle, and excessive force produces **brokens and powder (kuttu)**, which fetch a lower market price. Therefore, *performance evaluation* asks: **"How much whole, unbroken dal did the mill recover from 100 kg of raw pulse, and how much energy did it consume to do so?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics You Must Memorize (KTU High-Frequency Terms):**
> - **Milling Recovery (MR):** The single most cited performance indicator. Bench-mark value for an efficient mill: **70–80 %** for tur, **78–85 %** for chana.
> - **BIS Standard Recovery:** **≥ 72 %** for tur dal (IS 11476 specification).
> - **Specific Energy:** **25–40 kWh per ton** of finished dal (typical industrial benchmark).
> - **Moisture Standard for Storage:** **≤ 10 % (w.b.)** as per FSSAI.

## 1.3 Why Pulse Milling Is a Critical Unit Operation

Pulses are the **second-most important staple food in India after cereals**, providing the cheapest source of plant protein (~20–28 %). The hull (seed coat) is **rich in crude fibre, tannins, and polyphenols** that are anti-nutritional and cause flatulence. Hence, dehulling is non-optional. India processes approximately **~22–24 million tonnes of pulses annually** through ~14,000 registered pulse mills (data aligned with Ministry of Food Processing Industries, MoFPI), making this experiment directly relevant to industrial throughput optimization.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "3-C-D-S-P"** for the sequence of operations in a pulse mill:
> **C**leaning → **C**onditioning → **D**ehulling → **S**plitting → **P**olishing.
> Always write these in the same order in exam answers to score the "process-flow" mark.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Pulse Mill

A modern pulse mill operates on the principle of **abrasive dehulling with controlled mechanical impact**. The grain is fed between two abrasive surfaces (one rotating, one stationary) where the seed coat is **abraded (scraped off)** by the relative motion, while the underlying cotyledon remains largely intact due to its higher density and elasticity.

### 2.1.1 Sub-Operations in Sequence

1. **Cleaning (Scalping + Destoning + Magnetic separation)**
   - Removal of foreign matter: stones, chaff, metal pieces, dust.
   - Equipment: Air-screen cleaner, destoner, magnetic drum separator.

2. **Conditioning / Tempering (Moisture + Oil Treatment)**
   - Pulses are sprayed with water (0.5–2 % w/w) and/or edible oil (0.1–0.5 % w/w).
   - **Why?** Hardening of hull and softening of cotyledon → improves dehulling, reduces brokens.
   - Conditioning time: **2–12 hours** depending on pulse type.

3. **Dehulling (Emery roller / Under-runner disk / Roller mill)**
   - Primary dehulling by abrasion and friction.

4. **Splitting (Optional, in case of chana)**
   - Cotyledons pass through a **Pitting machine** and then through **roller mills** to split.

5. **Polishing (Horizontal scourer / Polishing cone)**
   - Removes adhered hull particles, improves shine, luster, and shelf life.

6. **Grading & Packaging**
   - Separation into whole dal, split dal, broken, and powder by **rotary sieve graders**.

## 2.2 Types of Pulse Mills (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Mechanism | Pulses Best Suited | Recovery (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Emery Roller Mill** | Abrasive rollers (emery-coated) | Tur, Moong, Urad | 70–80 |
| **Under-runner Disk Huller (URD)** | Disk-to-disk abrasion | Chana, Chhola | 78–85 |
| **Rubber-Roller Mill** | Differential-speed rubber rolls | Pre-treated pulses | 75–82 |
| **CFTRI Mini Dal Mill** | Integrated IIPR design, emery + polisher | Multi-grain | 72–80 |
| **CIAE Dal Mill (Bhopal)** | Institute-designed, low-cost | Rural & small-scale | 68–75 |
| **Pitting + Roller Mill (Chana dal mill)** | Pitting pre-treatment + splitting | Chickpea (chana) | 80–85 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favourite: CFTRI and CIAE Mill designs.** When asked "explain the working of a pulse mill", default to the **CFTRI process flow** (Pre-cleaner → Pitting → Dehuller → Aspirator → Polisher → Grader).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all formulas, units, and boundary conditions** required for solving pulse mill numericals.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit | Benchmark Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Milling Recovery (MR)** | $MR = \dfrac{M_{dal}}{M_{in}} \times 100$ | % | 70–85 % |
| 2 | **Dehulling Efficiency (η_d)** | $\eta_d = \dfrac{M_{hull\,removed}}{M_{hull\,present}} \times 100$ | % | ≥ 90 % |
| 3 | **Broken Percentage (B%)** | $B = \dfrac{M_{broken}}{M_{total\,dehulled}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 5 % (target) |
| 4 | **Powder / Kuttu (K%)** | $K = \dfrac{M_{powder}}{M_{in}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 2 % |
| 5 | **Capacity / Throughput (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{M_{out}}{t}$ | kg/h | 50–1000 kg/h |
| 6 | **Specific Energy (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{used}}{M_{dal\,produced}}$ | kWh/tonne | 25–40 |
| 7 | **Moisture Content (w.b.)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100$ | % | 8–10 % |
| 8 | **Moisture Content (d.b.)** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ | % | 8.7–11.1 % |
| 9 | **Moisture Pick-Up Ratio (MP)** | $MP = \dfrac{M_{water\,added}}{M_{dry\,grain}}$ | kg/kg | 0.005–0.020 |
| 10 | **Overall Milling Efficiency (OME)** | $OME = MR \times \dfrac{100 - B}{100}$ | % | Combined index |
| 11 | **Husk-to-Cotyledon Ratio (R_hc)** | $R_{hc} = \dfrac{M_{husk}}{M_{cotyledon}}$ | ratio | 0.07–0.12 |
| 12 | **Mechanical Damage Index (MDI)** | $MDI = \dfrac{M_{visible\,cracks}}{M_{sample}} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 3 % |
| 13 | **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | — | Annualized cost |
| 14 | **Break-Even Output (BEO)** | $BEO = \dfrac{FC}{SP - VC}$ | kg/yr | Threshold yield |
| 15 | **Protein Loss in Kuttu** | $\Delta P = P_{kuttu} \times K - P_{dal} \times MR$ | % | Loss in protein |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX note:** In the formulas above, all vertical-bar uses (for absolute value or rate) have been replaced with explicit textual forms (`water added / dry grain`) to comply with the **anti-table-corruption rule**. Whenever you must use a `|` inside an exam, wrap it in `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x - \bar{x} \vert$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The performance indices above are used by:
- **Engineers** to design mill capacity, motor rating, and sieve selection.
- **Procurement agencies (NAFED, FCI)** to set procurement-grade premiums/penalties.
