- **Researchers (IIPR, CIPHET)** to compare prototype vs. control mills.
- **Policy makers (MoFPI)** to fix the **Minimum Support Price (MSP)** quality premiums.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Milling Recovery (MR) from First Principles

**Statement:** The milling recovery is defined as the mass of clean, whole dehulled dal obtained per unit mass of raw pulse fed.

### Step 1 — Define the system boundary

Let the system be the pulse mill from input hopper to output discharge. Let the mass balance hold under steady-state, plug-flow assumption:

$$
M_{in} = M_{dal} + M_{husk} + M_{kuttu} + M_{broken} + M_{loss}
$$

### Step 2 — Define Milling Recovery

By definition, only the **salable, unbroken, properly dehulled cotyledon** qualifies as "dal." Therefore:

$$
MR = \dfrac{M_{dal}}{M_{in}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the mass balance identity

Expanding $M_{in}$ in terms of outputs:

$$
MR = \dfrac{M_{dal}}{M_{dal} + M_{husk} + M_{kuttu} + M_{broken} + M_{loss}} \times 100
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Insight

For a fixed pulse variety, the theoretical maximum MR is bounded by the **husk-to-cotyledon ratio (R_hc)** plus unavoidable kernel loss. For tur (pigeon pea):

$$
MR_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 + R_{hc} + K_{min}} \times 100
$$

If $R_{hc} = 0.11$ and $K_{min} = 0.02$ (minimum unavoidable powder):

$$
MR_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.11 + 0.02} \times 100 = 88.50 \ \text{\%}
$$

The **gap between 88.5 % (theoretical max) and the actual mill recovery (70–80 %)** is what the engineer must minimize through better conditioning and gentler dehulling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dehulling Efficiency (η_d)

### Step 1 — Set up the dehulling mass balance

For a batch of N grains, let $N_h$ grains be fully dehulled (both cotyledons), $N_p$ be partially dehulled, and $N_u$ be un-dehulled:

$$
N = N_h + N_p + N_u
$$

### Step 2 — Convert grain counts to masses (assuming uniform grain mass $\bar{m}$)

$$
M_{hull} = (N_h + 0.5 N_p) \cdot \bar{m}_{husk/grain}
$$

### Step 3 — Express dehulling efficiency

$$
\eta_d = \dfrac{N_h + 0.5 N_p}{N} \times 100
$$

### Step 4 — Practical implementation

In the lab, this is measured by **manual inspection of 100 grains post-dehulling** and counting those with full hull removal. The factor 0.5 in $N_p$ assumes that a partially dehulled grain has on average half its hull removed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Milling Efficiency (OME)

### Step 1 — Combine Recovery and Broken parameters

A mill with high recovery but excessive brokens is **not** efficient because brokens fetch only 30–40 % of the price of whole dal. Define:

$$
OME = MR \times \dfrac{(100 - B)}{100}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
OME = \dfrac{M_{dal}}{M_{in}} \times 100 \times \dfrac{100 - \dfrac{M_{broken}}{M_{total\,dehulled}} \times 100}{100}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$
OME = \dfrac{M_{dal} \times M_{whole}}{M_{in} \times M_{total\,dehulled}} \times 100
$$

where $M_{whole} = M_{total\,dehulled} - M_{broken}$.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> A pulse mill processes **1000 kg of tur** (pigeon pea) per hour. After dehulling and grading, the output streams are: **720 kg whole dal, 80 kg brokens, 50 kg husk, 100 kg kuttu (powder), 50 kg unaccounted loss**. Compute (a) Milling Recovery, (b) Broken %, (c) OME.

**Given:**
$M_{in} = 1000$ kg, $M_{dal} = 720$ kg, $M_{broken} = 80$ kg, $M_{husk} = 50$ kg, $M_{kuttu} = 100$ kg, $M_{loss} = 50$ kg.

**(a) Milling Recovery:**

$$
MR = \dfrac{720}{1000} \times 100 = 72.00 \ \%
$$

**(b) Broken Percentage (out of total dehulled = whole + broken):**

$$
B = \dfrac{80}{720 + 80} \times 100 = \dfrac{80}{800} \times 100 = 10.00 \ \%
$$

**(c) Overall Milling Efficiency:**

$$
OME = 72 \times \dfrac{100 - 10}{100} = 72 \times 0.90 = 64.80 \ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip:** A mill with MR = 72 % but B = 10 % is **worse** than a mill with MR = 70 % and B = 4 %. Always present BOTH recovery and broken % in your answer — never one alone.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

### Step 1 — Define the metric

$$
SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{dal\,produced}} \ \ [\text{kWh/tonne}]
$$

### Step 2 — Decompose total energy

$$
E_{total} = E_{cleaning} + E_{conditioning} + E_{dehulling} + E_{polishing} + E_{auxiliaries}
$$

### Step 3 — Typical share (Emery roller mill)

| Sub-Process | Energy Share |
|---|---|
| Cleaning | 5–8 % |
| Dehulling | 55–65 % |
| Polishing | 15–20 % |
| Aspirators + Graders | 10–15 % |

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical

> A mill draws **average 7.5 kW** from a 3-phase supply and produces **180 kg of whole dal per hour**. Compute SEC.

$$
E_{total} = 7.5 \ \text{kWh in 1 hour}
$$

$$
SEC = \dfrac{7.5 \ \text{kWh}}{0.180 \ \text{tonne}} = 41.67 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
$$

This is **slightly above the ideal range (25–40)**, indicating the mill is energy-inefficient — possibly due to worn emery rollers or an over-rated motor.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables on Performance

| Variable ↓ / Performance → | MR (%) | Broken (%) | SEC (kWh/t) | Hull Removal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Conditioning moisture** (0.5 → 2 %) | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑ slightly | ↑↑ |
| **↑ Conditioning time** (2 → 12 h) | ↑ | ↓↓ | No effect | ↑↑↑ |
| **↑ Emery roller speed** (600 → 1200 rpm) | ↑ initially, then ↓ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ (over-dehulling risk) |
| **↑ Roll gap / clearance** | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ |
| **↑ Feed rate (kg/h)** | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Pre-treatment with oil** (0.1 → 0.5 %) | ↑ | ↓↓ | ↑ slight | ↑↑ |
| **Polishing time** | No effect on MR | Negligible | ↑ | Removes loose hull |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's pattern:** A 7-mark question often gives a table of (input, output, broken) for 3 mill variants and asks you to compute MR, B%, OME for each, and then **state which mill is most efficient with justification.** Always show the **numerator–denominator substitution** in tabular form to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pulse Mill Process Flow (CFTRI Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Pulse Intake Hopper] --> B[Pre-Cleaner Scalper]
    B --> C[Air-Screen Cleaner]
    C --> D[Magnetic Drum Separator]
    D --> E[Destoner]
    E --> F[Conditioning Drum Tempering with Water and Oil]
    F --> G[Hold Bin Resting 2 to 12 hours]
    G --> H[Emery Roller Dehuller]
    H --> I[Aspirator 1 Hull Separation]
    I --> J[Grader Rotary Sieve]
    J --> K[Whole Dal Outlet]
    J --> L[Broken Outlet]
    I --> M[Polishing Cone]
    M --> N[Aspirator 2]
    N --> O[Finished Polished Dal]
    P[3 Phase Motor 7.5 to 15 kW] -.powers.-> H
    P -.powers.-> M
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Pulse Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SUB1[INPUT STAGE]
        IN1[Raw Pulse]
        IN2[Cleaning Subsystem]
        IN3[Conditioning Subsystem]
    end
    subgraph SUB2[PROCESSING STAGE]
        PR1[Dehulling Subsystem]
        PR2[Polishing Subsystem]
        PR3[Grading Subsystem]
    end
    subgraph SUB3[OUTPUT AND QA STAGE]
        OUT1[Whole Dal Grade A]
        OUT2[Split Dal Grade B]
        OUT3[Broken Grade C]
        OUT4[Powder Kuttu Grade D]
        OUT5[Husk to Biogas]
        OUT6[QA Lab Moisture Protein]
    end
    IN1 --> IN2
    IN2 --> IN3
    IN3 --> PR1
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> OUT1
    PR3 --> OUT2
    PR3 --> OUT3
    PR3 --> OUT4
    PR1 --> OUT5
    PR3 --> OUT6
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Dehulling Mechanism in an Emery Roller Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Grain Inlet] --> B[Rotating Emery Roller]
    B -- Abrasion --> C[Stationary Casing]
    C --> D[Dehulled Cotyledon]
    C --> E[Husk Aspiration Port]
    B -.spins at 800-1200 rpm.-> F[Variable Speed Drive]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (for ESE answer):**
> - The grain is fed at point A.
> - The **emery-coated roller (B)** rotates against the **stationary casing (C)**.
> - The **abrasive contact** between B and C removes the husk, which is sucked out by an aspirator at port E.
> - The clean cotyledon (D) exits the bottom for further polishing.
> - The **Variable Speed Drive (F)** is the key control — by changing roller RPM, the operator trades recovery for broken %.

## 4.4 Diagram — Conditioning Pretreatment Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hard Pulse Low Moisture] --> P2[Water Spray 0.5 to 2 percent w per w]
    P1 --> P3[Edible Oil Spray 0.1 to 0.5 percent w per w]
    P2 --> P4[Tempering Bin 2 to 12 hours]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Hull Hard Cotyledon Soft Dehulling Improved]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Milling Recovery of a pulse mill. State the BIS standard recovery value for tur dal.**

**Model Answer:**
Milling Recovery (MR) is defined as the ratio of the mass of clean, whole dehulled dal obtained at the mill output to the mass of raw pulse fed at the input, expressed as a percentage:

$$
MR = \dfrac{M_{dal}}{M_{in}} \times 100
$$

**BIS Standard (IS 11476:1985, reaffirmed 2002):** The minimum acceptable milling recovery for **tur/arhar dal** is **≥ 72 %** under standard conditioning and dehulling protocols.

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for BIS value.)*

---

### **Q2. List any three sub-operations performed in a pulse mill, with one-line justifications.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Sub-operation | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cleaning** | Removes foreign matter (stones, chaff, metal) that contaminates dal and damages emery rollers. |
| 2 | **Conditioning** | Hardens the hull and softens the cotyledon — minimizes brokens and improves hull removal. |
| 3 | **Polishing** | Removes adhered husk particles from dehulled cotyledon, improves luster, shelf life, and consumer appeal. |

> *(3 marks: 1 per row.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024, Module 2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **CFTRI-type pulse mill**. List the major sub-systems and the role of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A CFTRI-type pulse mill is an **integrated, multi-stage system** for converting raw pulses into polished dal. Its working is best explained via the **block diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes) covering three stages: **Input, Processing, and Output-QA**.

**Stage I — Input / Pre-processing:**
1. **Pre-Cleaner:** A scalping screen removes large foreign matter (straw, sticks).
2. **Air-Screen Cleaner:** A combination of aspiration and oscillating screens separates dust, light chaff, and undersize grains.
3. **Magnetic Drum:** Captures ferrous metal fragments.
4. **Destoner:** Uses a vibrating deck with air fluidization to separate stones of similar size to grain.

**Stage II — Conditioning:**
5. **Conditioning Drum:** Pulses are sprayed with **water (0.5–2 % w/w)** and **edible oil (0.1–0.5 % w/w)**. The oil acts as a binder and reduces friction-induced powder formation.
6. **Hold Bin:** Tempering for **2–12 h** allows moisture migration into the hull.

**Stage III — Processing:**
7. **Emery Roller Dehuller:** Hulls are abraded by an emery-coated roller running at **800–1200 rpm** against a stationary casing.
8. **Aspirator-1:** The lighter husk particles are sucked upward, while heavier cotyledons fall through.
9. **Polishing Cone:** A horizontal conical scourer with rotating paddles polishes the cotyledon using water mist and talc.
10. **Aspirator-2 & Grader:** A rotary sieve separates **whole, split, broken, and powder** grades.

**Stage IV — Output / QA:**
11. **Finished Dal Bins:** Four grades (A: whole, B: split, C: broken, D: kuttu) are routed to dedicated outlets.
12. **QA Lab:** Each batch is tested for **moisture (≤ 10 % w.b.)**, **protein**, and **brokens %**.

> *(7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 3 for sub-system roles, 2 for process rationale.)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** During a pulse mill trial, **500 kg of chana** (chickpea) was processed. The outputs were: **whole dal 380 kg, brokens 45 kg, husk 35 kg, kuttu 25 kg, loss 15 kg.** Calculate the **(i) Milling Recovery**, **(ii) Broken Percentage**, **(iii) Overall Milling Efficiency**, and **(iv) Specific Energy Consumption** if the mill consumed **12 kWh** during the run. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $M_{in} = 500$ kg, $M_{dal} = 380$ kg, $M_{broken} = 45$ kg, $M_{husk} = 35$ kg, $M_{kuttu} = 25$ kg, $M_{loss} = 15$ kg, $E = 12$ kWh.

**Mass balance check:** $380 + 45 + 35 + 25 + 15 = 500$ kg ✓ (closed system).

**(i) Milling Recovery:**

$$
MR = \dfrac{M_{dal}}{M_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{380}{500} \times 100 = 76.00 \ \%
$$

**(ii) Broken Percentage** (broken / total dehulled output):

$$
B = \dfrac{M_{broken}}{M_{dal} + M_{broken}} \times 100 = \dfrac{45}{380 + 45} \times 100 = \dfrac{45}{425} \times 100 = 10.59 \ \%
$$

**(iii) Overall Milling Efficiency:**

$$
OME = MR \times \dfrac{100 - B}{100} = 76 \times \dfrac{100 - 10.59}{100} = 76 \times 0.8941 = 67.95 \ \%
$$

**(iv) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$
SEC = \dfrac{E}{M_{dal}} = \dfrac{12 \ \text{kWh}}{0.380 \ \text{tonne}} = 31.58 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
$$

> **Conclusion:** MR = 76 % exceeds the BIS benchmark of 72 % for tur; however, B = 10.59 % is high (target ≤ 5 %), and SEC = 31.58 kWh/tonne is within the desirable range. **Recommended action:** reduce emery roller RPM to lower brokens, with marginal loss in recovery.

> *(7 marks: 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 for the four sub-parts.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the principle of **abrasive dehulling** in an emery roller mill. Discuss how **conditioning, roller speed, and roll gap** affect the dehulling efficiency and broken percentage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Abrasive Dehulling:**
Abrasive dehulling is the removal of the seed coat (hull) of a pulse grain by the **scraping action** of an abrasive emery surface rotating against a stationary casing. The emery particles (Al₂O₃ grit, size 24–36 mesh) have sharp, hard edges that preferentially abrade the **brittle hull** while leaving the **elastic cotyledon** relatively intact due to its higher density (~1.4 g/cm³ vs. ~1.1 g/cm³ for hull) and greater moisture content post-conditioning.

The process is **rate-controlled** by:
- **Normal force** between grain and abrasive.
- **Relative velocity** at the contact zone.
- **Contact time** of the grain in the dehulling zone.
- **Friction coefficient** between hull and emery.

**Effect of Variables:**

| Variable | Effect on η_d | Effect on Broken % | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conditioning moisture (↑)** | ↑↑ | ↓ | Water hardens hull, softens kernel |
| **Conditioning time (↑)** | ↑↑↑ | ↓↓ | More uniform moisture migration |
| **Roller speed (↑)** | ↑ initially | ↑↑ | High RPM = more impact → more brokens |
| **Roll gap (↑)** | ↓ | ↓↓ | Gentle, low-dehulling pass |
| **Emery grit size (↓ finer)** | ↑ | ↑ | Smoother finish, more damage |

> *(7 marks: 2 for principle, 3 for variable effects, 2 for engineering interpretation.)*

---

**Q.B. (b)** A pulse mill trial was conducted on **tur (arhar)**. The following data were recorded:

| Trial | Input (kg) | Whole Dal (kg) | Broken (kg) | Husk (kg) | Kuttu (kg) | Loss (kg) | Time (h) | Energy (kWh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 800 | 552 | 72 | 64 | 80 | 32 | 1.0 | 6.0 |
| 2 | 800 | 568 | 80 | 60 | 60 | 32 | 1.0 | 5.5 |
| 3 | 800 | 580 | 64 | 68 | 56 | 32 | 1.0 | 6.4 |

**Compute MR, B%, OME, and SEC for each trial. Identify the most efficient trial and justify.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Trial 1:** $M_{dal} = 552$, $M_{broken} = 72$, $E = 6.0$ kWh.
Mass balance: $552 + 72 + 64 + 80 + 32 = 800$ ✓

$$
MR_1 = \dfrac{552}{800} \times 100 = 69.00 \ \%
$$

$$
B_1 = \dfrac{72}{552 + 72} \times 100 = \dfrac{72}{624} \times 100 = 11.54 \ \%
$$

$$
OME_1 = 69 \times \dfrac{100 - 11.54}{100} = 69 \times 0.8846 = 61.04 \ \%
$$

$$
SEC_1 = \dfrac{6.0}{0.552} = 10.87 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
$$

---

**Trial 2:** $M_{dal} = 568$, $M_{broken} = 80$, $E = 5.5$ kWh.

$$
MR_2 = \dfrac{568}{800} \times 100 = 71.00 \ \%
$$

$$
B_2 = \dfrac{80}{568 + 80} \times 100 = \dfrac{80}{648} \times 100 = 12.35 \ \%
$$

$$
OME_2 = 71 \times 0.8765 = 62.23 \ \%
$$

$$
SEC_2 = \dfrac{5.5}{0.568} = 9.68 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
$$

---

**Trial 3:** $M_{dal} = 580$, $M_{broken} = 64$, $E = 6.4$ kWh.

$$
MR_3 = \dfrac{580}{800} \times 100 = 72.50 \ \%
$$

$$
B_3 = \dfrac{64}{580 + 64} \times 100 = \dfrac{64}{644} \times 100 = 9.94 \ \%
$$

$$
OME_3 = 72.5 \times 0.9006 = 65.29 \ \%
$$

$$
SEC_3 = \dfrac{6.4}{0.580} = 11.03 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
$$

---

**Comparison Table:**

| Trial | MR (%) | B (%) | OME (%) | SEC (kWh/t) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 69.00 | 11.54 | 61.04 | 10.87 |
| 2 | 71.00 | 12.35 | 62.23 | **9.68** |
| **3** | **72.50** | **9.94** | **65.29** | 11.03 |

**Conclusion:** **Trial 3 is the most efficient**, having the **highest MR (72.5 %)**, the **lowest broken % (9.94 %)**, and the **highest OME (65.29 %)**. Although Trial 2 has slightly better SEC, the OME — the combined index of yield and quality — is the primary industrial KPI, and Trial 3 dominates it.

> *(7 marks: 1.5 per trial + 1 for comparison table + 0.5 for justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgot to convert kg → tonne for SEC.** Always convert denominator to tonnes: $0.380$ tonne, not $380$ kg. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 2. **Wrong denominator for Broken %:** Some students use $M_{in}$ instead of $(M_{dal} + M_{broken})$. This inflates the broken % and gives the wrong OME. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
> 3. **Did not show mass-balance verification.** Always state: "$M_{dal} + M_{broken} + M_{husk} + M_{kuttu} + M_{loss} = M_{in}$ — closed system." Examiners reward this discipline.
> 4. **Single-index answer:** Reporting only MR or only B% without combining into OME. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 5. **Confusing w.b. and d.b. moisture:** A pulse with 8 % w.b. has 8.7 % d.b. — both are correct, but state which you are using.
> 6. **No units in the final answer:** Always suffix with **%** for ratios and **kWh/tonne** for SEC.
> 7. **No process-flow diagram for 7-markers:** KTU strictly awards 2 marks for the diagram alone.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision — 5 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] **Definition of pulse mill** → Primary processing unit for dehulling and splitting legume seeds.
- [ ] **BIS Standard recovery for tur dal** → **≥ 72 %** (IS 11476:1985).
- [ ] **3-C-D-S-P mnemonic** → Cleaning, Conditioning, Dehulling, Splitting, Polishing.
- [ ] **Milling Recovery formula** → $MR = (M_{dal}/M_{in}) \times 100$.
- [ ] **Broken % formula** → $B = M_{broken} / (M_{dal} + M_{broken}) \times 100$.
- [ ] **Overall Milling Efficiency** → $OME = MR \times (100 - B) / 100$.
- [ ] **Specific Energy Consumption** → $SEC = E / M_{dal}$ in **kWh/tonne**.
- [ ] **CFTRI mill components** → Pre-cleaner, Pitting, Dehuller, Aspirator, Polisher, Grader.
- [ ] **Conditioning protocol** → Water 0.5–2 %, Oil 0.1–0.5 %, hold 2–12 h.
- [ ] **Emery roller speed range** → 800–1200 rpm.
- [ ] **Abrasive dehulling principle** → Hull is brittle, cotyledon is elastic — selective abrasion.
- [ ] **Moisture standard (FSSAI)** → ≤ 10 % (w.b.) for finished dal.
- [ ] **Husk-to-cotyledon ratio for tur** → ~0.11 (so theoretical max MR ≈ 88.5 %).
- [ ] **Industry benchmark SEC** → 25–40 kWh/tonne.
- [ ] **Why conditioning matters** → Hardens hull + softens kernel = ↑ recovery + ↓ brokens.
- [ ] **Mass balance check** → Always verify inputs equal outputs in any numerical.
- [ ] **OME > MR alone** → OME penalises brokens, so it is the true industrial KPI.
- [ ] **Diagram discipline** → Always draw a labelled block diagram for 7-mark sub-parts.
- [ ] **Unit discipline** → Every numerical answer ends with **%** or **kWh/tonne**, never naked numbers.
- [ ] **Anti-hallucination** → Only quote IS 11476:1985, FSSAI 2022, IIPR, CFTRI, CIAE — no fabricated references.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Mills and Their Performance Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Oil Mills and Their Performance Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **oil mill** is a mechanical processing unit designed to extract vegetable oil from oil-bearing seeds and nuts (such as groundnut, soybean, mustard, sunflower, sesame, coconut, and cottonseed) by applying compressive, shearing, and sometimes thermal energy. **Performance evaluation** of an oil mill is the systematic quantification of its operational effectiveness using engineering parameters such as **oil recovery efficiency**, **throughput capacity**, **specific energy consumption**, **cake oil retention**, and **mechanical efficiency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGL607 – Module 2):**
> Students are expected to conduct an experiment on an oil expeller, record readings of feed rate, oil yield, cake output, moisture content, power consumption, and compute the standard performance indices used by industry and certification bodies.

## 1.2 Classification of Oil Mills

| Class | Sub-Type | Principle | Typical Oil Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Traditional | Ghani / Kolhu / Chekku | Mortar-pestle crushing + pressing | **60 – 70 %** |
| Mechanical Expeller | Screw / Hydraulic | Continuous high-pressure squeezing | **75 – 88 %** |
| Solvent Extraction | Batch / Continuous | Hexane leaching of cake | **95 – 99 %** |
| Combined | Expeller + Solvent | Pre-press followed by leaching | **98 – 99.5 %** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste with your fingers. The harder and more uniformly you press, the more paste comes out — but a thin residue always remains stuck to the walls. An **oil mill is essentially an industrial, continuous, super-charged version of that squeezing action**, where a rotating screw (the "finger") pushes the seed through a restricted barrel (the "tube") against enormous back-pressure. **Performance evaluation** is the lab report card: it tells the engineer how completely the mill has "emptied" the seed of its oil, how fast it did so, and how much electricity it burned in the process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Quality Indicators (must-know for KTU viva):**
> 1. **Oil Yield (% w/w)** — mass of oil obtained per mass of seed fed.
> 2. **Cake Oil Retention (% w/w)** — mass of oil left behind in the pressed cake.
> 3. **Specific Energy Consumption (kWh · t⁻¹)** — energy required per tonne of seed processed.

## 1.4 Why Performance Evaluation Matters

- Determines whether a mill is operating **below, at, or above its design rating**.
- Detects **wear of screw, cage bars, and choke plate** (signalled by rising cake oil).
- Provides the **economic breakeven** between mechanical pressing and solvent leaching.
- Mandatory for **BIS / FSSAI / ISO 22000** food-safety compliance audits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Screw Expeller (Most-Examined Mill)

A screw expeller is a **positive-displacement, continuous, single-shaft press** consisting of the following functional blocks:

1. **Hopper & Feed Conveyor** – meters conditioned seed (5 – 8 % moisture) into the cage.
2. **Rotating Worm Shaft (Screw)** – a tapered, flighted shaft whose root diameter increases towards the discharge end. As the seed advances, the **volume between consecutive flights decreases**, building pressure.
3. **Barrel / Cage** – a slotted cylindrical housing (vertical or horizontal) that allows expressed oil to drain while retaining the compressed cake.
4. **Choke / Cone Mechanism** – an adjustable restriction at the discharge that sets the **back-pressure** and therefore the residence time.
5. **Cake Discharger & Cutter** – cuts the extruded cake into pellets for downstream solvent extraction or storage.
6. **Drive Assembly** – typically a **7.5 kW to 75 kW** electric motor with reduction gearbox (15 – 60 rpm worm speed).

## 2.2 Factors Governing Extraction Performance

| # | Factor | Effect on Performance |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Moisture content of seed** | Optimal: **6 – 9 % w/w**. Too dry → dust, low yield; too wet → slippery, low pressure. |
| 2 | **Seed temperature (cooking/conditioning)** | **80 – 110 °C** denatures proteins, ruptures oil cells, lowers oil viscosity. |
| 3 | **Particle size after crushing/flaking** | Finer particles → more cell rupture, but higher frictional heating. |
| 4 | **Shaft rotational speed** | Slower rotation → higher pressure → better extraction, lower throughput. |
| 5 | **Choke setting** | Tighter choke → higher pressure → lower cake oil, lower throughput. |
| 6 | **Wear of screw flights** | Worn flights → pressure loss → rising cake oil, falling yield. |
| 7 | **Oilseed variety & oil content** | Higher inherent oil → higher absolute yield, similar % recovery. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are dimensionless percentages unless units are explicitly given. Always carry units in numerical problems to earn full marks.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Oil Yield** | $Y_{oil} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) |
| 2 | **Cake Yield** | $Y_{cake} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{cake}}{m_{seed}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) |
| 3 | **Oil Extraction Efficiency** | $\eta_{ext} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed} \cdot O_c} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | **Cake Oil Retention** | $C_{oil} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{cake} \cdot O_{cake}}{m_{seed}} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | **Mass Balance Closure** | $Y_{oil} + Y_{cake} \;=\; 100$ (ideal, dry basis) | % |
| 6 | **Throughput** | $T \;=\; \dfrac{m_{seed}}{t}$ | kg · h⁻¹ or t · h⁻¹ |
| 7 | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $SEC \;=\; \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m_{seed}}$ | kWh · t⁻¹ |
| 8 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} \;=\; \dfrac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | **Crude Protein in Cake** | $CP_{cake} \;=\; \dfrac{N \times 6.25}{m_{cake}}$ | % |
| 10 | **Free Fatty Acid (FFA)** | $FFA \;=\; \dfrac{V_{KOH} \cdot N_{KOH} \times 28.2}{m_{oil}}$ | % (as oleic) |

**Notation used in the table**

- $m_{oil}$ = mass of oil obtained (kg)
- $m_{seed}$ = mass of seed fed (kg)
- $m_{cake}$ = mass of cake obtained (kg)
- $O_c$ = oil content of seed (decimal or %)
- $O_{cake}$ = oil content of cake (decimal or %)
- $P$ = motor power draw (kW)
- $t$ = time of operation (h)
- $V_{KOH}$ = titre volume of KOH (mL)
- $N_{KOH}$ = normality of KOH
- 28.2 = equivalent weight of oleic acid (g · eq⁻¹) × 100 / 1000

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Managers** use the SEC value to compare expeller vendors and to size solar/biogas offsets.
- **Process Engineers** correlate $\eta_{ext}$ with cake oil content to schedule screw replacement (typically when $O_{cake}$ rises above **9 % w/w**).
- **Quality Auditors** verify FFA to ensure oil is fit for **refining, not direct consumption** (refining-grade: FFA < 5 %, edible cold-pressed: FFA < 2 %).
- **Design Engineers** use the throughput–SEC curve to right-size motors and gearboxes for new installations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mass Balance Closure for an Oil Expeller

Consider a single-pass expeller fed with **$m_{seed}$** kg of seed. Under steady-state, the input splits into exactly **two** output streams: oil ($m_{oil}$) and cake ($m_{cake}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input mass flow} &= \text{Output mass flow} \\[2pt]
m_{seed} &= m_{oil} + m_{cake} \qquad \text{(1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides of Equation (1) by $m_{seed}$ and multiplying by **100** converts it to percentage yield form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{m_{seed}}{m_{seed}} \times 100 &= \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed}} \times 100 \;+\; \frac{m_{cake}}{m_{seed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
100 &= Y_{oil} + Y_{cake} \qquad \text{(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **mass balance closure equation** — every performance calculation must satisfy it; any deviation indicates moisture loss, volatile carry-over, or weighing error.

## 3.2 Derivation of Oil Extraction Efficiency

By definition, **Oil Extraction Efficiency** is the *actual oil obtained* expressed as a percentage of the *theoretical maximum* (which is the oil content of the seed itself).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ext} &= \frac{\text{Actual oil obtained}}{\text{Oil originally present in seed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed} \cdot O_c} \times 100 \qquad \text{(3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $m_{oil} = m_{seed} \cdot Y_{oil}/100$ into Equation (3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ext} &= \frac{m_{seed} \cdot \frac{Y_{oil}}{100}}{m_{seed} \cdot O_c} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{Y_{oil}}{O_c} \times 100 \qquad \text{(4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $\eta_{ext} = 100\%$ means the mill extracted *all* the oil in the seed (impossible in a single mechanical press — solvent extraction is needed for $> 95\%$). A good expeller typically achieves **80 – 88 %**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Cake Oil Retention

Let $O_{cake}$ be the residual oil fraction in the cake (decimal). The mass of oil retained in the cake is $m_{cake} \cdot O_{cake}$. As a percentage of input seed mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{oil} &= \frac{m_{cake} \cdot O_{cake}}{m_{seed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= Y_{cake} \cdot O_{cake} \qquad \text{(5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining Equation (2), (3), and (5) yields the **theoretical identity**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ext} + \frac{C_{oil}}{O_c} \times 100 &= 100 \qquad \text{(6)}
\end{aligned}
$$

which is the basis for cross-verifying experimental data.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement.** In an experiment on a **15 kW screw expeller**, **20 kg of groundnut kernels** (initial moisture 7 %, oil content **45 % w/w**) are processed in **8 minutes**. The oil obtained weighs **7.6 kg** and the cake weighs **12.1 kg**. The cake, on Soxhlet analysis, contains **8.0 % w/w** oil. Compute: (i) Oil Yield, (ii) Cake Yield, (iii) Oil Extraction Efficiency, (iv) Cake Oil Retention, (v) Throughput, (vi) Specific Energy Consumption, (vii) Mass Balance Closure Check.

### Given Data (write first, compute later — KTU best practice)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{seed} &= 20 \text{ kg} \\
m_{oil} &= 7.6 \text{ kg} \\
m_{cake} &= 12.1 \text{ kg} \\
O_c &= 0.45 \quad (45\%) \\
O_{cake} &= 0.08 \quad (8\%) \\
P &= 15 \text{ kW} \\
t &= 8 \text{ min} = \dfrac{8}{60} \text{ h} = 0.1333 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (i) Oil Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{oil} &= \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.6}{20} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 38.0 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### (ii) Cake Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{cake} &= \frac{m_{cake}}{m_{seed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{12.1}{20} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 60.5 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### (iii) Oil Extraction Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ext} &= \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed} \cdot O_c} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.6}{20 \times 0.45} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.6}{9.0} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 84.44 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### (iv) Cake Oil Retention

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{oil} &= Y_{cake} \cdot O_{cake} \\[4pt]
&= 60.5 \times 0.08 \\[4pt]
&= 4.84 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### (v) Throughput

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{m_{seed}}{t} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20}{0.1333} \\[4pt]
&= 150 \text{ kg · h}^{-1} \;=\; 0.150 \text{ t · h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (vi) Specific Energy Consumption

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \frac{P \cdot t}{m_{seed}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{15 \times 0.1333}{20} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2.0}{20} \\[4pt]
&= 0.10 \text{ kWh · kg}^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= 100 \text{ kWh · t}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (vii) Mass Balance Closure Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{oil} + Y_{cake} &= 38.0 + 60.5 \\[4pt]
&= 98.5 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The shortfall of **1.5 %** is attributed to **moisture evaporation** during the hot-pressing ($20 \text{ kg} \times 0.07 = 1.4 \text{ kg}$ water evaporated ≈ 1.5 % mass loss). Excellent closure for an experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip (KTU):** Always report the closure check. If $|Y_{oil} + Y_{cake} - 100| > 3\%$, recompute — there is a weighing or sampling error. Marks are reserved for this verification step in **Part B** questions.

## 3.5 Cross-Verification Using Equation (6)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ext} + \frac{C_{oil}}{O_c} \times 100 &= 84.44 + \frac{4.84}{45} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 84.44 + 10.76 \\[4pt]
&= 95.20 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The **4.8 %** deficit from 100 % reflects (a) the closure shortfall above, and (b) the oil lost as foots/entrained fines — perfectly within experimental tolerance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Screw Expeller

The schematic below maps the **functional signal/energy/material flow** of an oil mill experiment. Node identifiers are alphanumeric per Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Seed Hopper] --> A2[Cracker and Flaker]
    A2 --> A3[Cooker and Conditioner 80 to 110 degC]
    A3 --> A4[Horizontal Screw Cage]
    A4 --> A5[Choke and Cone Assembly]
    A5 --> A6[Cake Pelletizer and Cutter]

    A4 --> A7[Oil Pan and Strainer]
    A7 --> A8[Filter Press]
    A8 --> A9[Crude Oil Storage Tank]

    B1[3 Phase AC Motor 15 kW] --> B2[Reduction Gearbox]
    B2 --> A4
    B1 -. power draw .-> C1[Energy Meter kWh]
    C1 --> D1[SEC Calculator kWh per ton]

    A1 -. feed mass .-> E1[Weighing Balance]
    A9 -. oil mass .-> E1
    A6 -. cake mass .-> E1
    E1 --> F1[Performance Index Sheet]

    F1 --> F2[Oil Yield percent]
    F1 --> F3[Extraction Efficiency percent]
    F1 --> F4[Cake Oil Retention percent]
    F1 --> F5[Throughput kg per hour]

    style A4 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#B3E5A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Process-Flow Diagram of the Experiment (Module-2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start Experiment] --> stepB[Weigh 20 kg Clean Seed m_seed]
    stepB --> stepC[Determine Moisture by Oven Method]
    stepC --> stepD[Determine Oil Content by Soxhlet O_c]
    stepD --> stepE[Set Choke, Warm Mill 5 min]
    stepE --> stepF[Feed Seed, Start Stopwatch]
    stepF --> stepG[Record Motor Power P from Meter]
    stepG --> stepH[Collect Oil m_oil and Cake m_cake]
    stepH --> stepI[Analyse Cake for Residual Oil O_cake]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute Y_oil, Y_cake, eta_ext, SEC]
    stepJ --> stepK{Closure within 3 percent?}
    stepK -- Yes --> stepL[Tabulate and Plot SEC vs Throughput]
    stepK -- No --> stepM[Recheck Weighing and Sampling]
    stepM --> stepB

    style stepJ fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
    style stepL fill:#B3E5A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
```

## 4.3 Functional Role of Each Block (Mapping for Viva)

| Node | Functional Role | Engineering Variable |
|---|---|---|
| A2 | Cell-wall rupture, surface area increase | Flake thickness **0.20 – 0.35 mm** |
| A3 | Protein coagulation, viscosity reduction | Steam injection, residence **15 – 30 min** |
| A4 | Pressure build-up (1 – 35 MPa) | Screw compression ratio **1:8 to 1:14** |
| A5 | Back-pressure set-point | Choke gap **6 – 14 mm** |
| C1 | Real-time power logging | Sampling interval **30 s** |
| F1 | Consolidated report card | Tabulated mean ± SD over 3 trials |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define oil extraction efficiency. List two factors that affect it during mechanical expelling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Oil extraction efficiency is defined as the ratio of the actual mass of oil obtained from a seed to the theoretical mass of oil present in the seed, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\eta_{ext} = \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed} \cdot O_c} \times 100
$$

**Two affecting factors:**
1. **Moisture content of the seed** — optimum is **6 – 9 % w/w**; deviation in either direction reduces yield.
2. **Conditioning temperature** — heating the seed to **80 – 110 °C** coagulates proteins and reduces oil viscosity, improving extraction.

*(1 mark definition + 2 × 1 mark for factors = 3 marks)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is specific energy consumption? State its typical range for a 15 kW screw expeller processing groundnut.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific energy consumption (SEC) is the electrical energy consumed per unit mass of seed processed. Formula:

$$
SEC = \frac{P \times t}{m_{seed}} \;\; [\text{kWh · t}^{-1}]
$$

For a **15 kW screw expeller** processing groundnut under standard operating conditions, the typical SEC lies in the range **80 – 120 kWh · t⁻¹** (i.e., **0.08 – 0.12 kWh · kg⁻¹**).

*(1 mark formula + 1 mark definition + 1 mark numerical range = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **construction and working of a screw-type oil expeller**. Explain the role of the choke and the conditioning step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment on a **10 kW screw expeller**, **15 kg of soybean seeds** (oil content **20 % w/w**, moisture **8 %**) were processed in **12 minutes**. The recovered oil weighed **2.40 kg** and the cake weighed **12.30 kg**. Soxhlet analysis showed the cake contained **2.5 % w/w** oil. Determine: **(i)** Oil yield, **(ii)** Cake yield, **(iii)** Extraction efficiency, **(iv)** Cake oil retention, **(v)** Throughput in kg · h⁻¹, **(vi)** SEC in kWh · t⁻¹, **(vii)** Verify mass balance closure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a)

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A screw expeller consists of a **hopper**, a **horizontal cylindrical cage** with vertical/horizontal slots, a **worm shaft** with increasing root diameter, a **choke/cone** at the discharge, and a **gear-driven motor (7.5 – 75 kW)**.

2. **Working (2 marks):** The conditioned seed enters the cage; the rotating screw conveys it forward while the reducing volume between flights generates pressure (**1 – 35 MPa**). Oil escapes through the cage slots; the compressed cake is extruded through the choke.

3. **Role of choke (1 mark):** The choke sets the **back-pressure**, controls **residence time**, and therefore governs the trade-off between **throughput and cake oil content**.

4. **Role of conditioning (1 mark):** Heating to **80 – 110 °C** ruptures oil cells, coagulates proteins, and lowers oil viscosity, enhancing recovery.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b)

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{seed} &= 15 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{oil} = 2.40 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{cake} = 12.30 \text{ kg}, \\
O_c &= 0.20, \quad O_{cake} = 0.025, \quad P = 10 \text{ kW}, \quad t = 12 \text{ min} = 0.20 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Oil Yield**

$$
Y_{oil} = \frac{2.40}{15} \times 100 = 16.00 \;\%
$$

**(ii) Cake Yield**

$$
Y_{cake} = \frac{12.30}{15} \times 100 = 82.00 \;\%
$$

**(iii) Extraction Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{ext} = \frac{2.40}{15 \times 0.20} \times 100 = \frac{2.40}{3.00} \times 100 = 80.00 \;\%
$$

**(iv) Cake Oil Retention**

$$
C_{oil} = Y_{cake} \times O_{cake} = 82.00 \times 0.025 = 2.05 \;\%
$$

**(v) Throughput**

$$
T = \frac{15}{0.20} = 75 \text{ kg · h}^{-1}
$$

**(vi) Specific Energy Consumption**

$$
SEC = \frac{10 \times 0.20}{15} = 0.1333 \text{ kWh · kg}^{-1} = 133.3 \text{ kWh · t}^{-1}
$$

**(vii) Mass Balance Closure**

$$
Y_{oil} + Y_{cake} = 16.00 + 82.00 = 98.00 \;\%
$$

Shortfall **2.00 %** = **moisture loss** during hot pressing (≈ 0.30 kg, which equals **2 %** of seed mass). **Closure verified.**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`**

**(a)** Compare **traditional Ghani**, **mechanical screw expeller**, and **solvent extraction** methods of oil milling in terms of principle, recovery, capital cost, and typical oil quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a 30-minute trial on a screw expeller, the following data were recorded: **Motor rating = 18.5 kW**, **Actual power draw = 14.2 kW**, **Seed fed = 85 kg**, **Oil obtained = 32 kg**, **Cake obtained = 51 kg**, **Oil content of seed = 48 %**, **Residual oil in cake = 9.5 %**. Calculate the extraction efficiency, cake oil retention, throughput (t · h⁻¹), and SEC (kWh · t⁻¹). Also determine the mechanical efficiency of the drive if the useful shaft work was 9.8 kW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Feature | **Ghani (Traditional)** | **Screw Expeller** | **Solvent Extraction** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Mortar-pestle crushing by animal/engine power | Continuous screw pressing | Hexane leaching of pre-pressed cake |
| Recovery | **60 – 70 %** | **75 – 88 %** | **95 – 99 %** |
| Capital Cost | **Very Low** (< ₹1 lakh) | **Medium** (₹5 – 25 lakh) | **High** (₹50 lakh – ₹5 cr) |
| Oil Quality | Excellent (cold, unrefined, high FFA) | Moderate (heat-affected) | Lower (traces of solvent, refined) |
| Throughput | **5 – 20 kg · h⁻¹** | **50 – 500 kg · h⁻¹** | **5 – 500 t · day⁻¹** |
| Labour | High (skilled operator) | Low | Low (automated) |
| Best suited for | Small batches, premium cold-pressed oil | Edible oil plants, rural industries | Large commercial refineries |

*(Each row carries partial credit; key entries – recovery, cost, quality – are mandatory.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — Calculations

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{seed} &= 85 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{oil} = 32 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{cake} = 51 \text{ kg}, \\
O_c &= 0.48, \quad O_{cake} = 0.095, \quad P_{draw} = 14.2 \text{ kW}, \\
t &= 0.50 \text{ h}, \quad P_{shaft} = 9.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Extraction Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{ext} = \frac{32}{85 \times 0.48} \times 100 = \frac{32}{40.8} \times 100 = 78.43 \;\%
$$

**Cake Oil Retention**

$$
C_{oil} = Y_{cake} \times O_{cake} = \frac{51}{85} \times 100 \times 0.095 = 60.00 \times 0.095 = 5.70 \;\%
$$

**Throughput**

$$
T = \frac{85}{0.50} = 170 \text{ kg · h}^{-1} = 0.170 \text{ t · h}^{-1}
$$

**Specific Energy Consumption**

$$
SEC = \frac{14.2 \times 0.50}{85} = \frac{7.10}{85} = 0.0835 \text{ kWh · kg}^{-1} = 83.53 \text{ kWh · t}^{-1}
$$

**Mechanical Efficiency of Drive**

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{draw}} \times 100 = \frac{9.8}{14.2} \times 100 = 69.01 \;\%
$$

The remaining **30.99 %** of electrical input is dissipated as gearbox friction and motor losses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake (loses 1 mark):** Reporting Oil Yield as Extraction Efficiency. They are *different* — yield uses $m_{seed}$ as denominator, efficiency uses $m_{seed} \cdot O_c$.
> 2. **Common mistake (loses 1 mark):** Forgetting to convert minutes to hours in throughput and SEC calculations.
> 3. **Common mistake (loses 1 mark):** Not verifying mass balance closure. KTU examiners **explicitly deduct** marks when the closure check is missing.
> 4. **Common mistake (loses 2 marks):** Using rated motor power (e.g., 15 kW) instead of **actual measured power draw** for SEC — the latter is what the mill *actually consumed*, which is what the question demands.
> 5. **Common mistake (loses 0.5 mark):** Writing units as just "%" without specifying "w/w" — KTU 2024 scheme insists on **mass basis clarity**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Oil Yield** = (oil / seed) × 100 — *amount obtained*.
- [x] **Cake Yield** = (cake / seed) × 100 — *amount retained*.
- [x] **Oil Yield + Cake Yield ≈ 100 %** (closure identity, valid on dry basis).
- [x] **Extraction Efficiency** uses **oil content** in denominator — *quality indicator*.
- [x] **Cake Oil Retention** = $Y_{cake} \cdot O_{cake}$ — *mill-wear indicator*.
- [x] **SEC** must use **actual measured kW**, not rated motor kW.
- [x] **Optimum seed moisture** for expelling: **6 – 9 % w/w**.
- [x] **Optimum conditioning temperature**: **80 – 110 °C**.
- [x] **Screw compression ratio** (typical): **1 : 8 to 1 : 14**.
- [x] **Choke gap** typical: **6 – 14 mm** (smaller → higher pressure → lower cake oil).
- [x] **Good expeller recovery**: **80 – 88 %**; **Ghani**: **60 – 70 %**; **Solvent**: **95 – 99 %**.
- [x] **Throughput** is reported in **kg · h⁻¹** or **t · h⁻¹** — always state.
- [x] **Mechanical efficiency** = shaft power / input electrical power.
- [x] **Mass balance verification** is **mandatory** in KTU lab reports.
- [x] **Soxhlet** method is the standard reference for $O_c$ and $O_{cake}$.
- [x] **FFA** < 2 % = cold-pressed / edible grade; > 5 % = refining-grade only.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation of Peeler and Slicer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation of Peeler and Slicer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Performance Evaluation of a Peeler

**Formal Definition:**
*Performance evaluation of a peeler* is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a mechanical peeling machine's operational effectiveness in removing the outer skin/peel of fruits, vegetables, or root crops (e.g., potato, carrot, onion, garlic, apple) by measuring its **throughput capacity, peeling efficiency, peeling loss, mechanical damage, and specific energy consumption** under standard operating conditions.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are peeling a potato with a knife for dinner. Now imagine 500 potatoes need to be peeled in 1 hour for a hotel kitchen. You obviously cannot use a hand knife. A *peeler machine* (like an abrasive drum peeler or knife-blade peeler) does the same job mechanically. But how do we know if the machine is doing a *good job*? We need to check:
- How much potato got peeled correctly (efficiency)
- How much potato flesh was wasted along with the peel (loss)
- How many potatoes were bruised or damaged (damage)
- How fast it processes (capacity)
- How much electricity it consumes (energy)

This measurement of "how good" the machine is doing its job is called **performance evaluation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is a core lab-based topic under Module Experiment 2. Students must memorize the **6 standard performance parameters** (Capacity, Efficiency, Loss, Damage, Uniformity, Energy) as they appear frequently in viva voce and 3-mark questions.

---

### 1.2 Performance Evaluation of a Slicer

**Formal Definition:**
*Performance evaluation of a slicer* is the quantitative measurement of a slicing machine's ability to cut agricultural produce into **uniform slices of defined thickness** by assessing the *slicing efficiency, slice thickness uniformity, throughput capacity, cutting force/energy, and percentage of acceptable (standard) slices versus broken/irregular slices*.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think about cutting an onion into thin, even slices for a restaurant. A chef's knife works, but a *slicer machine* (gravity-fed slicer, rotary slicer, or centrifugal slicer) cuts hundreds of onions per hour into identical rings. We evaluate the slicer by asking:
- Are all slices the same thickness? (uniformity)
- How many slices per minute? (capacity)
- How many slices broke, cracked, or were uneven? (rejection rate)
- How much motor power was used? (energy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric Reminder:** A high-quality slicer should produce **≥ 90% acceptable slices** with **± 0.5 mm thickness tolerance** for the unit to be commercially viable in a food processing plant.

### 1.3 Types of Peelers and Slicers Studied in Module Experiment 2

| Equipment | Common Type Used in Lab | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Peeler** | Abrasive Drum Peeler / Knife-blade Peeler | Friction / Cutting action removes skin |
| **Slicer** | Rotary Gravity-Fed Slicer | Rotating disc blades cut falling produce |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Moisture Content:** All performance evaluations in KTU labs are conducted on produce conditioned to **70–85% w.b. (wet basis) moisture** for fruits/vegetables like potato, carrot, or apple.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle – Peeler

A typical **abrasive drum peeler** works as follows:
1. The cleaned produce (e.g., potatoes) is fed through a hopper.
2. The produce tumbles against a **rotating abrasive drum** (coated with carborundum).
3. The **frictional shear** between the rough drum surface and the produce skin **abrades (grinds off)** the outer peel.
4. A **water spray bar** continuously washes away the removed peel.
5. The peeled produce exits through a discharge chute.

**KTU Key Point:** The peeling depth must be controlled. Too deep → high loss. Too shallow → peel remains.

### 2.2 Operating Principle – Slicer

A **rotary gravity-fed slicer** works as follows:
1. Produce is loaded into the **feed hopper** at the top.
2. Gravity pulls the produce down onto a **high-speed rotating disc fitted with sharp blades**.
3. The blades slice the produce into uniform rings/sheets.
4. Sliced material exits at the bottom.
5. Slice thickness is controlled by the **gap between the blade and the adjustable cutting gauge**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize all 8 formulas below. The 5 most-likely ones for Part A (3 marks) are starred (★).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ★1 | **Peeling Efficiency** $\eta_p$ | $\eta_p = \dfrac{M_{peeled}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | % | $M_{peeled}$ = mass of properly peeled produce; $M_{total}$ = total mass fed |
| ★2 | **Peeling Loss** $L_p$ | $L_p = \dfrac{M_{original} - M_{peeled}}{M_{original}} \times 100$ | % | $M_{original}$ = mass before peeling |
| ★3 | **Machine Throughput (Capacity)** $C$ | $C = \dfrac{M_{processed}}{t}$ | kg/h | $M_{processed}$ = mass handled; $t$ = time |
| ★4 | **Machine Efficiency** $\eta_m$ | $\eta_m = \dfrac{C_{effective}}{C_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | $C_{effective}$ = actual capacity; $C_{theoretical}$ = rated capacity |
| ★5 | **Mechanical Damage** $D_m$ | $D_m = \dfrac{N_{damaged}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | $N_{damaged}$ = number of damaged units |
| ★6 | **Slicing Efficiency** $\eta_s$ | $\eta_s = \dfrac{N_{acceptable}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | $N_{acceptable}$ = uniform & unbroken slices |
| ★7 | **Slice Thickness Uniformity** $U_t$ | $U_t = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma}{t_{mean}}\right) \times 100$ | % | $\sigma$ = standard deviation; $t_{mean}$ = mean thickness (mm) |
| ★8 | **Specific Energy Consumption** $E_s$ | $E_s = \dfrac{P \times t}{M_{processed}}$ | kWh/kg | $P$ = power (kW); $t$ = time (h) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule:** Vertical bar `$\vert$` is used inside the table to represent absolute/divider values so the markdown structure is not corrupted. Always use $\vert$ instead of `|` in cell content.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Plant Design:** Capacity ($C$) directly decides the size of receiving, washing, and storage stations.
- **Economic Justification:** Higher $\eta_p$ means less raw material wastage → lower cost per kg of finished product.
- **Quality Control (ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000):** Damage percentage $D_m$ must be $< 5\%$ for export-grade produce.
- **Energy Auditing (BEE Star Rating):** $E_s$ is a key parameter for Government of India food processing subsidies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Parameter Relationships

The **6 key parameters** are interdependent. We summarize their logical relationship below:

1. As **drum rotation speed** $\uparrow$ → Peeling rate $\uparrow$, but **damage** $D_m$ also $\uparrow$ and **loss** $L_p$ $\uparrow$.
2. As **residence time** in peeler $\uparrow$ → $\eta_p \uparrow$ and $L_p \uparrow$ (trade-off curve).
3. As **blade sharpness** $\uparrow$ (slicer) → $\eta_s \uparrow$ and $E_s \downarrow$.
4. As **feed rate** $\uparrow$ → $C \uparrow$, but $\eta_p \downarrow$ and $D_m \downarrow$ (throughput vs quality trade-off).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Peeler Performance

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU ESE Pattern):**
A laboratory abrasive drum peeler was used to process **20 kg of potatoes**. After peeling, **17.6 kg** of properly peeled potato was recovered, and the rest was rejected as peeling loss or damaged. The motor consumed **0.5 kWh** of electricity in **30 minutes** of continuous operation. Calculate:
(a) Peeling efficiency
(b) Peeling loss
(c) Throughput capacity
(d) Specific energy consumption

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- $M_{original} = 20$ kg
- $M_{peeled} = 17.6$ kg
- $t = 30$ min $= 0.5$ h
- $P \times t = 0.5$ kWh

**Step 1 — Peeling Loss $L_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= \frac{M_{original} - M_{peeled}}{M_{original}} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{20 - 17.6}{20} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{2.4}{20} \times 100 \\
    &= 12\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The difference (2.4 kg) represents the peel and flesh removed together. Dividing by original mass normalizes to a percentage.

**Step 2 — Peeling Efficiency $\eta_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_p &= \frac{M_{peeled}}{M_{original}} \times 100 \\
       &= \frac{17.6}{20} \times 100 \\
       &= 88\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Acceptable industrial benchmark for an abrasive peeler is **85–95%**. Our value 88% falls within range.

**Step 3 — Throughput Capacity $C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{M_{processed}}{t} \\
  &= \frac{20 \text{ kg}}{0.5 \text{ h}} \\
  &= 40 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Capacity = input mass divided by operating time.

**Step 4 — Specific Energy $E_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \frac{P \times t}{M_{processed}} \\
    &= \frac{0.5 \text{ kWh}}{20 \text{ kg}} \\
    &= 0.025 \text{ kWh/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Normalizing energy by output mass enables fair comparison between machines of different capacities.

**Final Answer Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| Peeling Efficiency | 88% | 85–95% ✓ |
| Peeling Loss | 12% | < 15% ✓ |
| Capacity | 40 kg/h | Spec-dependent |
| Specific Energy | 0.025 kWh/kg | 0.02–0.05 kWh/kg ✓ |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Slicer Performance

**Problem Statement:**
A rotary slicer processed **500 onion pieces** in 10 minutes. Of these, **460** produced uniform, unbroken slices of target thickness (3 mm). The mean thickness measured was **3.05 mm** with a standard deviation of **0.18 mm**. Calculate:
(a) Slicing efficiency
(b) Thickness uniformity
(c) Throughput capacity (in pieces/h)

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Slicing Efficiency $\eta_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{N_{acceptable}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
       &= \frac{460}{500} \times 100 \\
       &= 92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Thickness Uniformity $U_t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= \left(1 - \frac{\sigma}{t_{mean}}\right) \times 100 \\
    &= \left(1 - \frac{0.18}{3.05}\right) \times 100 \\
    &= (1 - 0.0590) \times 100 \\
    &= 94.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The coefficient of variation $\sigma / t_{mean} = 0.059$ is very low, indicating the slicer is highly consistent.

**Step 3 — Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{N_{total}}{t} = \frac{500 \text{ pieces}}{10/60 \text{ h}} \\
  &= \frac{500}{0.1667} \\
  &= 3000 \text{ pieces/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Slicing Efficiency | 92% |
| Thickness Uniformity | 94.1% |
| Capacity | 3000 pieces/h |

---

### 3.4 Performance Comparison Logic (Peeler vs. Slicer)

| Aspect | Peeler | Slicer |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Quality Metric** | Peeling Efficiency $\eta_p$ | Slicing Efficiency $\eta_s$ |
| **Main Loss Mode** | Peeling loss $L_p$ (flesh wasted) | Broken/irregular slices |
| **Critical Control Variable** | Drum speed, residence time | Blade sharpness, feed rate |
| **Damage Type** | Bruising, flesh gouging | Cracking, edge tearing |
| **Energy Form** | Friction-dominated | Cutting-dominated |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Performance Evaluation Methodology

This flow diagram represents the *experimental procedure* a student would follow during the lab session, mapped as a functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Produce Input: Potato or Onion 20 kg Batch"] --> B["Step 1: Cleaning and Weighing Initial Mass M0"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Feed to Peeler Machine"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Record Operating Time t and Motor Power P"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Collect Peeled Produce"]
    E --> F{"Visual Inspection and Grading"}
    F --> G["Grade A: Properly Peeled Mass Mp"]
    F --> H["Grade B: Damaged or Partial Mass Md"]
    F --> I["Grade C: Unpeeled Mass Mu"]
    G --> J["Compute Peeling Efficiency Peeling Loss Throughput"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Decision Output: Acceptable Machine Performance or Recalibrate"]

    A2["Raw Produce: Onion or Carrot 500 pieces"] --> B2["Step 1: Visual Inspection and Counting N0"]
    B2 --> C2["Step 2: Feed to Slicer Machine"]
    C2 --> D2["Step 3: Record Time and Measure Slice Thickness"]
    D2 --> E2["Step 4: Collect Slices"]
    E2 --> F2{"Grading of Slices"}
    F2 --> G2["Grade A: Acceptable Uniform Slices Nacc"]
    F2 --> H2["Grade B: Broken or Irregular Nbr"]
    G2 --> I2["Compute Slicing Efficiency Thickness Uniformity Capacity"]
    H2 --> I2
    I2 --> J2["Decision Output: Slicer Performance Report"]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Internal Mass Balance in Peeler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0["Input Mass M0 kg"] --> M1["Acceptable Peeled Mp kg"]
    M0 --> M2["Peel Removed with Flesh Ml kg"]
    M0 --> M3["Damaged and Rejected Md kg"]

    M1 --> Calc["Performance Calculator"]
    M2 --> Calc
    M3 --> Calc

    Calc --> Eff["Peeling Efficiency percent"]
    Calc --> Loss["Peeling Loss percent"]
    Calc --> Dam["Mechanical Damage percent"]
    Calc --> Cap["Throughput kg per hour"]
    Calc --> En["Specific Energy kWh per kg"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Sub-Block — Slicer Quality Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input["Total Slices N0"] --> Acc["Acceptable Nacc"]
    Input --> Brk["Broken Nbr"]
    Input --> Irr["Irregular Thickness Nir"]

    Acc --> Seff["Slicing Efficiency"]
    Brk --> Seff
    Irr --> Seff

    Seff --> Final["Slicer Performance Report"]
    Input --> Cap2["Throughput in pieces per hour"]
    Cap2 --> Final
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are *block-level functional maps* (not physical engineering drawings). For a physical schematic of an abrasive drum peeler, refer to the standard FAO Agricultural Services Bulletin on root crop processing (institutional lab manual).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define peeling efficiency and state its formula. What is the typical acceptable range for an abrasive drum peeler?*

**Model Answer:**
Peeling efficiency is the ratio of the mass of properly peeled produce to the total mass of produce fed into the peeler, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta_p = \frac{M_{peeled}}{M_{total}} \times 100
$$

The typical acceptable range for an abrasive drum peeler processing potatoes is **85% to 95%**.

**(3 marks: Definition 1 + Formula 1 + Range 1)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any four performance parameters used to evaluate a slicer.*

**Model Answer:**
The four performance parameters used to evaluate a slicer are:
1. **Slicing efficiency** (ratio of acceptable to total slices)
2. **Slice thickness uniformity** (consistency of slice thickness)
3. **Throughput capacity** (pieces processed per hour)
4. **Mechanical damage / breakage percentage** (broken or cracked slices)

**(3 marks: 4 parameters × 0.75 each ≈ 3 marks; or 0.5 mark per parameter for 4 parameters + 1 mark for elaboration of two)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions for lab-oriented modules provide **internal choice**. We provide two alternatives: **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the procedure for evaluating the performance of an **abrasive drum peeler**. List all the parameters calculated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a peeler test, **25 kg** of potatoes were processed in **45 minutes**. The properly peeled mass was **21.5 kg** and **1.0 kg** was damaged/rejected. The motor rating is **1.0 kW**. Calculate: (i) Peeling efficiency, (ii) Peeling loss, (iii) Mechanical damage percentage, (iv) Throughput capacity, (v) Specific energy consumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The performance evaluation procedure is as follows (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Sample preparation:** Clean 20–25 kg of uniform-size potatoes. Weigh and record initial mass $M_0$.
2. **Machine setup:** Start the abrasive drum peeler, adjust drum speed (typically 200–400 rpm), and set water spray flow rate.
3. **Feeding:** Feed the produce into the hopper. Record the start time $t_1$ and power meter reading.
4. **Operation:** Allow the peeler to run for a fixed residence time (typically 1–3 minutes per batch).
5. **Collection:** Collect the discharged produce. Record end time $t_2$ and final power reading.
6. **Sorting/Grading:** Manually grade the discharged produce into:
   - Properly peeled ($M_p$)
   - Damaged/over-peeled ($M_d$)
   - Unpeeled/partially peeled ($M_u$)
7. **Calculations:** Compute $\eta_p$, $L_p$, $D_m$, $C$, and $E_s$ using the formulas in Section 2.3.
8. **Reporting:** Tabulate the results and compare with standard benchmarks.

**(Marks: 1 for sample prep + 1 for setup + 1 for feeding/operation + 1 for collection + 1 for grading + 1 for calculations + 1 for reporting)**

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $M_0 = 25$ kg, $M_p = 21.5$ kg, $M_d = 1.0$ kg, $t = 45$ min $= 0.75$ h, $P = 1.0$ kW.

**(i) Peeling Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_p &= \frac{M_p}{M_0} \times 100 = \frac{21.5}{25} \times 100 = 86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Peeling Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= \frac{M_0 - M_p}{M_0} \times 100 = \frac{25 - 21.5}{25} \times 100 = 14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mechanical Damage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_m &= \frac{M_d}{M_0} \times 100 = \frac{1.0}{25} \times 100 = 4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Throughput Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{M_0}{t} = \frac{25}{0.75} = 33.33 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Specific Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \frac{P \times t}{M_0} = \frac{1.0 \times 0.75}{25} = 0.03 \text{ kWh/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Marks: 7/5 = 1.4 marks per sub-question, with 1 bonus mark for clear tabulation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **rotary gravity-fed slicer** with a block diagram. List the parameters used to evaluate its performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slicer processed **800 tomato pieces** in **20 minutes**. **720** slices were acceptable uniform slices. The mean thickness was **4.8 mm** and standard deviation was **0.30 mm**. Calculate: (i) Slicing efficiency, (ii) Thickness uniformity, (iii) Capacity in pieces per hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Principle (3 marks):** A rotary gravity-fed slicer uses the **gravitational potential energy** of produce stored in a hopper to feed the material onto a **high-speed rotating disc fitted with radial blades**. As the disc rotates at high speed (typically 500–1500 rpm), the blades shear the produce into uniform slices. The slice thickness is precisely controlled by an **adjustable cutting gauge** that sets the clearance between the blade tip and the stationary bed.

2. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Students should draw: Hopper → Feed chute → Rotating disc with blades → Sliced product collection → Thickness gauge). For textual reference, see Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1.

3. **Performance Parameters (2 marks):**
   - Slicing efficiency $\eta_s$
   - Slice thickness uniformity $U_t$
   - Throughput capacity $C$ (pieces/h or kg/h)
   - Mechanical damage / broken slice percentage
   - Specific energy consumption

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $N_{total} = 800$, $N_{acc} = 720$, $t = 20$ min $= 1/3$ h, $t_{mean} = 4.8$ mm, $\sigma = 0.30$ mm.

**(i) Slicing Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{N_{acc}}{N_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{720}{800} \times 100 = 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Thickness Uniformity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= \left(1 - \frac{\sigma}{t_{mean}}\right) \times 100 \\
    &= \left(1 - \frac{0.30}{4.8}\right) \times 100 \\
    &= (1 - 0.0625) \times 100 \\
    &= 93.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{N_{total}}{t} = \frac{800}{1/3} = 2400 \text{ pieces/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Marks: ~2.3 per sub-part)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Students often write capacity in *kg/s* instead of *kg/h*. Always convert time to **hours** before dividing.
> 2. **Confusing $L_p$ and $\eta_p$:** Some students compute peeling loss as $\frac{M_p}{M_0}$ — this is WRONG. Loss = $\frac{M_0 - M_p}{M_0}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the 100 multiplier:** Many lose 1 mark by writing $0.88$ instead of $88\%$.
> 4. **Mislabelling damage:** Mechanical damage $D_m$ is computed on **mass basis** for peelers and **number/count basis** for slicers — be consistent.
> 5. **Power vs Energy:** Specific energy = total energy (kWh) / mass (kg), NOT power alone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and Keep)**

- [ ] **Peeling Efficiency** $\eta_p = (M_{peeled} / M_{total}) \times 100$ — typical range **85–95%**.
- [ ] **Peeling Loss** $L_p = ((M_0 - M_p) / M_0) \times 100$ — typical range **5–15%**.
- [ ] **Mechanical Damage** $D_m = (M_d / M_0) \times 100$ — should be **< 5%** for export-grade.
- [ ] **Throughput Capacity** $C = M / t$ — always express in **kg/h or pieces/h**.
- [ ] **Slicing Efficiency** $\eta_s = (N_{acc} / N_{total}) \times 100$ — typical **≥ 90%**.
- [ ] **Thickness Uniformity** $U_t = (1 - \sigma / t_{mean}) \times 100$ — typical **≥ 90%**.
- [ ] **Specific Energy** $E_s = (P \times t) / M$ — typical **0.02–0.05 kWh/kg** for peelers.
- [ ] **Machine Efficiency** $\eta_m = (C_{eff} / C_{theory}) \times 100$.
- [ ] **Trade-off Principle:** Higher speed ↑ capacity but ↑ damage and ↑ loss.
- [ ] **Always express percentages** (efficiency, loss, damage) with the **%** symbol.
- [ ] **Always convert time** to hours before computing capacity or specific energy.
- [ ] **Benchmarks to memorize:** Peeler — $\eta_p \geq 85\%$, $D_m \leq 5\%$. Slicer — $\eta_s \geq 90\%$, $U_t \geq 90\%$.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Performance Evaluation of Peeler and Slicer**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation of Juicer and Pulper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation of Juicer and Pulper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Evaluation of Juicer and Pulper** is the systematic experimental procedure used to quantify the operational effectiveness of fruit-juice extraction equipment (Juicer) and fruit/vegetable pulping machinery (Pulper) in terms of **extraction yield, extraction efficiency, throughput capacity, specific energy consumption, and mechanical efficiency**.

A **Juicer** is a mechanical device that separates the liquid juice from the solid pulp/peel/seeds of fruits or vegetables by applying shear, crushing, and centrifugal force. A **Pulper** is a machine that reduces whole fruits/vegetables (after blanching/peeling) into a fine homogeneous pulp or puree by passing them through a rotating set of paddles/brushes against a perforated screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students frequently confuse "Juicer" and "Pulper." A juicer *separates* juice from solids; a pulper *homogenizes* the entire fruit into a thick pulp (e.g., tomato pulp, mango pulp) without extracting the liquid separately.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine squeezing an orange with your hand — the liquid that drips out is the **juice**, and the leftover fibrous skin and pulp are the **waste**. Now imagine instead using a grinder to crush the entire orange into a thick paste with no separation — that is **pulping**. The juicer is like a *selective sieve that keeps liquids and rejects solids*, while the pulper is like a *kitchen blender that keeps everything together but refines the texture*.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The five **primary performance metrics** evaluated in this experiment are:
- **Juice Yield (%)** — Ratio of juice mass obtained to raw material mass.
- **Extraction Efficiency (%)** — Ratio of juice obtained to the actual juice content in the feed.
- **Capacity / Throughput** — Mass of feed processed per unit time (**kg/h**).
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — Power drawn per unit mass processed (**kWh/kg** or **J/kg**).
- **Mechanical Efficiency** — Ratio of useful output energy to input electrical energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles

**Juicer (Working Principle):**
1. Feed fruits/vegetables are fed into the hopper.
2. A rotating cutter/grater (in centrifugal juicers) or auger (in cold-press/slow juicers) crushes the produce.
3. Centrifugal force or mechanical pressing drives juice through a fine mesh/strainer.
4. Pulp is ejected into a separate collection bin; juice flows out via a spout.

**Pulper (Working Principle):**
1. Pre-processed (washed, peeled, deseeded) fruits are fed into the hopper.
2. A rotor fitted with paddles or brushes rotates at high speed (typically **1440 rpm** or higher).
3. Material is forced through a perforated screen (mesh size **0.5 mm – 3 mm**) by centrifugal force.
4. The skin, seeds, and fibrous matter are retained as waste, and refined pulp exits the screen.

### 2.2 Categories of Equipment

| Equipment Type | Sub-type | Operating Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Juicer | Centrifugal | High-speed cutter + centrifugal force | Apple, pineapple |
| Juicer | Cold-press / Masticating | Slow rotating auger + pressing | Pomegranate, leafy greens |
| Juicer | Citrus | Reamer cone + manual/auto press | Orange, lemon |
| Pulper | Batch Pulper | Intermittent feeding, paddle rotation | Small-scale mango pulp |
| Pulper | Continuous Pulper | Inline feeding, brush-type rotor | Tomato puree industry |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following formulas — they are the *core numerical backbone* of this experiment.

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Juice Yield | $Y_j = \dfrac{m_j}{m_f} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | Extraction Efficiency | $\eta_e = \dfrac{m_j}{m_f \cdot J_c} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Pulper Efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{m_p}{m_f} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Capacity (Throughput) | $Q = \dfrac{m_f}{t}$ | kg/h |
| 5 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m_f}$ | kWh/kg |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Mass Balance (Juicer) | $m_f = m_j + m_p + m_l$ | kg |
| 8 | Loss Factor | $\% L = \dfrac{m_l}{m_f} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Volumetric Capacity | $Q_v = \dfrac{V_f}{t}$ | L/h |
| 10 | Power Input | $P_{in} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W |

Where:
- $m_f$ = Mass of feed (kg)
- $m_j$ = Mass of juice obtained (kg)
- $m_p$ = Mass of pulp/waste (kg)
- $m_l$ = Mass loss (kg)
- $J_c$ = Juice content fraction in the raw material (decimal)
- $t$ = Time of operation (h)
- $P$ = Power consumed (kW)
- $V$ = Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $\cos\phi$ = Power factor

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

In the Indian **food processing industry**, performance evaluation of juicers and pulpers is critical for:
- **Capacity planning** in juice concentrate plants (e.g., Parle Agro, Dabur).
- **Energy auditing** to minimize operational cost (SEC benchmarks: **0.05–0.15 kWh/kg**).
- **Quality control** of products like Aamras, tomato ketchup, and fruit nectar.
- **Equipment selection** by comparing throughput and extraction efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Juice Yield

**Given:**
- Mass of raw fruit fed into juicer: $m_f$ (kg)
- Mass of juice collected: $m_j$ (kg)
- Mass of pulp/waste ejected: $m_p$ (kg)

**Derivation:**

The juice yield is defined as the ratio of usable output (juice) to total input (raw fruit).

$$
Y_j = \frac{\text{Mass of Juice Obtained}}{\text{Mass of Raw Material Fed}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the variables:

$$
Y_j = \frac{m_j}{m_f} \times 100
$$

**Dimensional Check:**

$$
Y_j = \frac{[\text{kg}]}{[\text{kg}]} \times [\%] = [\%]
$$

The formula is **dimensionally consistent** and yields a value between **0% and 100%**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Extraction Efficiency

**Given:**
- Mass of juice obtained: $m_j$
- Mass of feed: $m_f$
- Juice content of the raw fruit (from literature/lab test): $J_c$ (decimal fraction, e.g., 0.55 for orange)

**Conceptual Logic:**
Extraction efficiency compares what we *actually extracted* vs. what *could have been extracted* if 100% recovery occurred.

$$
\eta_e = \frac{\text{Actual Juice Obtained}}{\text{Theoretical Maximum Juice in Feed}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_e = \frac{m_j}{m_f \cdot J_c} \times 100
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Specific Energy Consumption

**Given:**
- Power input to motor: $P$ (kW)
- Time of operation: $t$ (h)
- Mass processed: $m_f$ (kg)

**Step 1:** Total electrical energy consumed is:

$$
E = P \cdot t \quad [\text{kWh}]
$$

**Step 2:** Specific energy consumption per kg of feed is:

$$
SEC = \frac{E}{m_f} = \frac{P \cdot t}{m_f} \quad [\text{kWh/kg}]
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A centrifugal juicer is used to process **5 kg** of oranges in **3 minutes**. The juice collected weighs **2.8 kg**, and the wet pulp weighs **2.0 kg**. The juice content of oranges is **0.56** (fraction). Voltage measured is **220 V**, current **2.5 A**, and power factor **0.85**. Calculate:
- (a) Juice Yield
- (b) Extraction Efficiency
- (c) Capacity (kg/h)
- (d) Mass Loss (%)
- (e) Specific Energy Consumption

**Given:**
$m_f = 5$ kg, $m_j = 2.8$ kg, $m_p = 2.0$ kg, $J_c = 0.56$, $t = 3 \text{ min} = 0.05 \text{ h}$, $V = 220$ V, $I = 2.5$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$

**Solution:**

**(a) Juice Yield:**

$$
Y_j = \frac{m_j}{m_f} \times 100 = \frac{2.8}{5.0} \times 100 = 56\%
$$

**(b) Extraction Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_e = \frac{m_j}{m_f \cdot J_c} \times 100 = \frac{2.8}{5.0 \times 0.56} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_e = \frac{2.8}{2.8} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

**(c) Capacity:**

$$
Q = \frac{m_f}{t} = \frac{5}{0.05} = 100 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(d) Mass Loss:**

First, check mass balance:

$$
m_f = m_j + m_p + m_l \implies m_l = 5.0 - 2.8 - 2.0 = 0.2 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\% L = \frac{0.2}{5.0} \times 100 = 4\%
$$

**(e) Specific Energy Consumption:**

First, compute input power:

$$
P_{in} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 220 \times 2.5 \times 0.85 = 467.5 \text{ W} = 0.4675 \text{ kW}
$$

Then:

$$
SEC = \frac{P_{in} \cdot t}{m_f} = \frac{0.4675 \times 0.05}{5.0} = 4.675 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kWh/kg}
$$

$$
SEC \approx 0.00468 \text{ kWh/kg} = 16.83 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Expected Ranges

| Parameter | Centrifugal Juicer | Cold-Press Juicer | Continuous Pulper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Juice Yield (%) | 50 – 65 | 65 – 85 | N/A (pulping) |
| Extraction Efficiency (%) | 75 – 90 | 90 – 98 | 85 – 95 |
| Capacity (kg/h) | 80 – 200 | 30 – 80 | 200 – 1000 |
| SEC (kWh/kg) | 0.005 – 0.015 | 0.015 – 0.030 | 0.003 – 0.010 |
| Mesh/Screen Size (mm) | 0.5 – 1.0 | N/A | 0.5 – 3.0 |
| Typical Rotor Speed (rpm) | 3000 – 14000 | 40 – 100 | 1440 – 2880 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Centrifugal Juicer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Fruit Feed] --> B[Hopper]
    B --> C[Rotating Cutter Grater]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Chamber]
    D --> E[Fine Mesh Strainer]
    E --> F[Juice Outlet Spout]
    E --> G[Pulp Ejection Port]
    C -.power.-> H[Electric Motor]
    H -.drives.-> C
    F --> I[Juice Collection Vessel]
    G --> J[Pulp Collection Bin]
    F -.mass.-> K[Mass Measurement]
    K --> L[Yield and Efficiency Calculation]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Continuous Pulper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Pre-processed Fruit] --> A2[Feed Hopper]
    A2 --> B1[Rotating Paddle Rotor]
    B1 --> B2[Perforated Screen]
    B2 --> C1[Pulp Outlet]
    B2 --> C2[Waste Outlet Skin Seeds Fiber]
    B1 -.power.-> D1[Electric Motor 1 to 2 HP]
    D1 -.1440 rpm.-> B1
    C1 --> E1[Pulped Mass Collection]
    E1 --> F1[Pulper Efficiency Calculation]
    C2 --> E2[Waste Mass Measurement]
```

### 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Weigh Raw Material] --> S2[Step 2: Note Initial Moisture and Juice Content]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Start Machine Record Voltage Current Time]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Feed Material Through Hopper]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Collect Juice and Pulp Separately]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Weigh Juice and Pulp]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Calculate Yield Efficiency SEC Capacity]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Tabulate and Plot Graphs]
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Schematic of Performance Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Voltmeter] --> M1[Motor]
    P2[Ammeter] --> M1
    P3[Stopwatch] --> T1[Timer Block]
    M1 --> J1[Juicer or Pulper Unit]
    J1 --> B1[Balance for Mass Measurement]
    T1 --> B1
    B1 --> D1[Data Logger]
    D1 --> R1[Results Yield Efficiency SEC]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term *Extraction Efficiency* of a juicer. Write the formula and state the units.

**Model Answer:**
Extraction efficiency is the ratio of the actual mass of juice extracted to the theoretical maximum juice present in the raw feed, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta_e = \frac{m_j}{m_f \cdot J_c} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$
