- **Standardized 0.1 N NaOH** (carbonate-free; standardized with oxalic acid).
- **Phenolphthalein indicator** — 1 g dissolved in 100 mL of 95% ethanol.
- **Distilled water** — CO₂-free (boiled and cooled).

### Procedure

1. **Sample preparation:** Weigh ~50 g of fruit, remove seeds/peel, crush/blend uniformly. Strain through muslin cloth to obtain clear juice.
2. **Pipette** 10 mL (or weigh exactly 10 g) of the filtered juice into a clean 250 mL conical flask.
3. Add **~50 mL of distilled water** to dilute (reduces dark colour interference).
4. Add **3–4 drops of phenolphthalein** indicator. Solution remains **colourless**.
5. **Titrate** against **0.1 N NaOH** from a clean burette, swirling continuously, until a **faint permanent pink** colour appears (persists ≥ 30 s).
6. **Note the burette reading** ($V_{NaOH}$). Repeat for **triplicate** readings — acceptable if $\vert V_1 - V_2 \vert \le 0.2$ mL.
7. **Measure TSS** (°Brix) of the same juice using a **hand refractometer** (calibrated with distilled water at 20°C).
8. **Compute TA** using the formula in §2.2.
9. **Compute Sugar/Acid Ratio** using $SAR = TSS / TA$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Titratable Acidity Formula

The fundamental law of titration is **Normality × Volume = Equivalents**:

$$N_{NaOH} \times V_{NaOH} = N_{acid} \times V_{acid}$$

The mass of acid present is given by:

$$m_{acid} = N_{acid} \times V_{acid} \times E$$

The percentage of acid in the sample:

$$TA\ (\%) = \frac{m_{acid}}{W} \times 100 = \frac{N_{NaOH} \times V_{NaOH} \times E \times 100}{W \times 1000}$$

This derivation holds for any acid-base titration where the equivalent weight of the analyte is known.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A sample of orange juice (citric acid dominant) was analysed. 10 mL of juice was titrated against 0.1 N NaOH. The burette readings were 4.2 mL, 4.3 mL, and 4.4 mL. The TSS of the juice (measured by refractometer) was 12.0 °Brix. Calculate the titratable acidity (% citric acid) and the sugar/acid ratio.

### Step 1: Take the concordant (mean) titre value.

$$\bar{V} = \frac{4.2 + 4.3 + 4.4}{3} = \frac{12.9}{3} = 4.30\ mL$$

> Check: differences are 0.1 mL, well within 0.2 mL tolerance. **Concordant. ✓**

### Step 2: Identify the equivalent weight.

For orange (citric acid dominant):

$$E = \frac{192}{3} = 64\ g/eq$$

### Step 3: Apply the TA formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TA\ (\%) &= \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times E \times 100}{W \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{4.30 \times 0.1 \times 64 \times 100}{10 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{4.30 \times 0.1 \times 64 \times 100}{10000}
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerator computation:

$$4.30 \times 0.1 = 0.430$$

$$0.430 \times 64 = 27.52$$

$$27.52 \times 100 = 2752$$

Final:

$$TA\ (\%) = \frac{2752}{10000} = 0.2752\ \%$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Sugar/Acid Ratio.

$$SAR = \frac{TSS\ (^\circ Brix)}{TA\ (\%)} = \frac{12.0}{0.2752} = 43.60$$

### Result

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Mean titre | **4.30 mL** |
| Titratable acidity | **0.2752 % citric acid** |
| Sugar/Acid Ratio | **43.60** |

**Interpretation:** A ratio > 30 indicates a **mature, sweet orange** — ideal for table consumption or juice concentrate. FSSAI specifies min. 10°Brix and 0.5% TA minimum for "orange juice" labelling, so this sample is excellent quality.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — TA and SAR Standards for Common Fruits

| Fruit | TA Range (%) | TSS Range (°Brix) | Typical SAR | Reference Acid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sweet orange | 0.5 – 1.5 | 10 – 14 | 8 – 20 | Citric |
| Lemon | 5.0 – 7.0 | 7 – 10 | 1.0 – 2.0 | Citric |
| Apple | 0.2 – 0.8 | 11 – 15 | 20 – 50 | Malic |
| Grape (table) | 0.4 – 0.9 | 14 – 20 | 25 – 50 | Tartaric |
| Grape (wine) | 0.6 – 1.2 | 18 – 26 | 20 – 35 | Tartaric |
| Mango (ripe) | 0.2 – 0.6 | 14 – 20 | 35 – 60 | Citric |
| Tomato | 0.3 – 0.6 | 4 – 6 | 8 – 15 | Citric |
| Pineapple | 0.5 – 1.0 | 12 – 17 | 15 – 30 | Citric |
| Banana | 0.2 – 0.4 | 18 – 24 | 50 – 90 | Citric |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Titration Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fruit Sample] --> B[Weighing and Crushing]
    B --> C[Filtration through Muslin]
    C --> D1[Juice Sample A for Refractometer]
    C --> D2[Juice Sample B for Titration]
    D1 --> E1[Hand Refractometer]
    E1 --> F1[TSS Reading in Degree Brix]
    D2 --> E2[Pipette 10 mL into Conical Flask]
    E2 --> G[Add 50 mL Distilled Water]
    G --> H[Add 3 to 4 drops Phenolphthalein]
    H --> I[Burette with 0.1 N NaOH]
    I --> J{Titrate drop by drop}
    J -->|Pink persists 30 s| K[Note Final Burette Reading]
    J -->|Pink fades| I
    K --> L[Repeat in Triplicate]
    L --> M{Concordant within 0.2 mL?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Compute Mean Volume V]
    M -->|No| L
    N --> O[Apply TA Formula]
    F1 --> P[Apply SAR Formula]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Final TA percent and Sugar to Acid Ratio]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Acid Neutralization Chemistry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fruit Juice R COOH Weak Organic Acid] -->|Add NaOH dropwise| B[Dissociated Acid Anion R COO- and Na+ plus H2O]
    B -->|Endpoint pH 8.2| C[Phenolphthalein turns Faint Pink]
    C --> D[Stop Titration and Record Volume V]
    D --> E[Calculate Equivalents of Acid Neutralized]
    E --> F[TA percent and SAR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dilute with distilled water?** Many fruit juices are deeply pigmented (e.g., pomegranate, black grape), masking the phenolphthalein end-point. Dilution 1:5 to 1:10 sharpens the visual transition from colourless to pink and improves reproducibility.

## 4.3 Mermaid Safeguards

- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` → here using descriptive prefixed IDs such as `A`, `B`, `D1`).
- No reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone nodes.
- Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric (e.g., "Weighing and Crushing"), no bold or HTML tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Titratable Acidity (TA) of a fruit juice. Why is TA preferred over pH for assessing fruit quality?**

**Model Answer:**
Titratable acidity (TA) is the total amount of free hydrogen ions and undissociated acid molecules in fruit juice that can be neutralized by a standard alkali (0.1 N NaOH), expressed as a percentage of the predominant organic acid. It is preferred over pH because pH measures only the free H⁺ concentration (a small fraction), while TA measures the *total* acid reservoir, which correlates better with perceived sourness, maturity, and shelf-life of the fruit. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the Sugar/Acid Ratio (SAR) in fruit processing? Mention the standard reagent and indicator used for titration.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sugar/Acid Ratio (SAR) = °Brix / TA (%), is a maturity and quality index used to decide the harvest time and the recipe for beverages, squashes, and concentrates. The standard reagent is **0.1 N NaOH** and the indicator is **phenolphthalein** (1% in ethanol), giving a faint-pink end-point at pH 8.2. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Describe the procedure for the determination of titratable acidity of a fruit juice by acid-base titration. List the apparatus, reagents, and the formula used.

**(b)** [7 marks] 10 mL of apple juice (malic acid dominant) was titrated against 0.1 N NaOH. The burette readings were 6.5, 6.6, and 6.7 mL. The TSS of the juice is 13.5 °Brix. Calculate the titratable acidity and the sugar/acid ratio. Comment on the quality.

#### Model Solution (a) — Procedure [7 marks]

1. **Apparatus:** Burette (50 mL), pipette (10 mL), conical flasks, refractometer, top-pan balance, muslin cloth. **(1 mark)**
2. **Reagents:** 0.1 N NaOH (carbonate-free), 1% phenolphthalein indicator in 95% ethanol, CO₂-free distilled water. **(1 mark)**
3. **Procedure:**
   - Crush/blend ~50 g of fruit and filter through muslin to obtain clear juice. **(1 mark)**
   - Pipette 10 mL of juice into a 250 mL conical flask. Add ~50 mL distilled water and 3–4 drops of phenolphthalein. **(1 mark)**
   - Titrate against 0.1 N NaOH with constant swirling until a faint permanent pink colour appears. **(1 mark)**
   - Record the burette reading. Repeat in triplicate. Concordant value = mean of readings within ±0.2 mL. **(1 mark)**
   - Formula: $TA\ (\%) = \dfrac{V \times N \times E \times 100}{W \times 1000}$ **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 6.5$, $V_2 = 6.6$, $V_3 = 6.7$ mL
- $N_{NaOH} = 0.1$ N
- Apple → dominant acid = **Malic acid**, $E = 67$ g/eq
- $TSS = 13.5$ °Brix
- $W = 10$ mL juice

**Step 1: Mean titre**

$$
\bar{V} = \frac{6.5 + 6.6 + 6.7}{3} = \frac{19.8}{3} = 6.60\ mL
$$

Differences are 0.1 mL → concordant. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Compute TA**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TA\ (\%) &= \frac{6.60 \times 0.1 \times 67 \times 100}{10 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{6.60 \times 0.1 \times 67 \times 100}{10000} \\
&= \frac{44.22 \times 100}{10000} \\
&= 0.4422\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 3: Compute SAR**

$$SAR = \frac{13.5}{0.4422} = 30.53$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Quality comment**

A SAR of ~30 indicates a **ripe, sweet apple** with balanced sweet-sour profile — ideal for table consumption and juice concentrate. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 marks] What is a Sugar/Acid Ratio? List the equivalent weights of citric, malic, and tartaric acids. Why is the **equivalent weight** used in the TA formula instead of molecular weight?

**(b)** [7 marks] The TSS of a grape juice sample is 18 °Brix. In a titration, 10 mL of the juice required 5.5 mL of 0.1 N NaOH (tartaric acid dominant). Calculate the TA and SAR. Is this grape suitable for wine-making?

#### Model Solution (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Sugar/Acid Ratio:** It is the ratio of the total soluble solids (°Brix) to the titratable acidity (%) and is a key maturity and quality index for fruits and beverages. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Equivalent weights:** **(3 marks)**

| Acid | Molecular Weight | Replaceable H⁺ | Equivalent Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Citric acid | 192 | 3 | **64** |
| Malic acid | 134 | 2 | **67** |
| Tartaric acid | 150 | 2 | **75** |

**(iii) Why equivalent weight?** Polybasic acids (citric, malic, tartaric) have more than one replaceable H⁺ per molecule. In titration, one mole of such acid consumes more than one mole of NaOH. Therefore, the **equivalent weight = MW / basicity** correctly represents the mass of acid neutralized by 1 mole of NaOH. Using molecular weight would underestimate the acid content. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $TSS = 18$ °Brix
- $V_{NaOH} = 5.5$ mL
- $N_{NaOH} = 0.1$ N
- Grape → dominant acid = **Tartaric acid**, $E = 75$ g/eq
- $W = 10$ mL

**Step 1: TA calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TA\ (\%) &= \frac{5.5 \times 0.1 \times 75 \times 100}{10 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{5.5 \times 0.1 \times 75 \times 100}{10000} \\
&= \frac{41.25 \times 100}{10000} \\
&= 0.4125\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 2: SAR calculation**

$$SAR = \frac{18}{0.4125} = 43.64$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Wine-making suitability**

A SAR of **~44** is **excellent for wine-making** (ideal range 20–35 for table wines, up to 45 for dessert wines). The grape is **sweet, well-balanced, and fully mature**, suitable for premium wine. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Using molecular weight instead of equivalent weight** — citric = 192, not 64. This alone can deduct **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Selecting the wrong dominant acid** — oranges = citric, apples = malic, grapes = tartaric. Random choice loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the × 100 / × 1000 factors in the formula** — the final answer becomes off by a factor of 10. **Always sanity-check** the magnitude: a typical fruit juice TA is **0.1% – 1.0%**.
> 4. **Not reporting triplicate readings and their mean** — KTU examiners expect "Mean titre = X mL" with concordant check.
> 5. **Skipping units and significant figures** — write "% citric acid" or "% malic acid" and report SAR to 2 decimal places.
> 6. **Not diluting dark juices** — although not a calculation error, it is the most common **practical** mark-loss if not mentioned.
> 7. **Confusing °Brix with Baumé or Specific Gravity** — KTU strictly uses **°Brix (refractometer)** for SAR.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** TA = total acid (free + undissociated) neutralized by 0.1 N NaOH; reported as % of dominant acid. **SAR = °Brix / TA**.
- **Reagent:** 0.1 N NaOH. **Indicator:** Phenolphthalein. **End-point:** faint pink, pH 8.2.
- **Equivalent weights (must memorize):** Citric = **64**, Malic = **67**, Tartaric = **75**, Acetic = **60**, Lactic = **90**, Oxalic = **45**.
- **Master formula:** $TA\ (\%) = (V \times N \times E \times 100) / (W \times 1000)$.
- **Always take triplicate titres**; accept mean only if differences $\le 0.2$ mL.
- **Always dilute dark juices** 1:5 with distilled water to sharpen the end-point.
- **TSS** must be measured **on the same juice** (or its filtrate) using a refractometer.
- **Interpretation ranges (memorize for 14-mark answers):**
  - Orange ripe: SAR 8–20
  - Apple ripe: SAR 25–50
  - Grape wine: SAR 20–35
  - Mango ripe: SAR > 35
- **Why TA > pH?** TA measures the *total* acid reservoir, not just free H⁺ — better sensory & shelf-life correlation.
- **Industrial use:** Determines harvest time, beverage recipe, concentrate quality, and FSSAI/Codex compliance.
- **Common pitfall:** Using molecular weight (e.g., 192 for citric) instead of 64 → 3× error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Mantra (write in exam margin):**
> *"0.1 N NaOH + Phenolphthalein → Faint pink. TA = (V·N·E·100)/(W·1000). SAR = °Brix/TA. Citric=64, Malic=67, Tartaric=75."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moisture Content Determination of Fruits and Vegetables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL607 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN AGRICULTURAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Moisture Content Determination of Fruits and Vegetables # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — MOISTURE CONTENT DETERMINATION OF FRUITS AND VEGETABLES ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Moisture Content (M.C.)** of a food material (fruit or vegetable) is formally defined as the amount of water present in it, expressed as a percentage of either the **wet mass** of the sample (*wet basis, w.b.*) or the **dry mass** of the sample (*dry basis, d.b.*). In food process engineering and post-harvest technology, the **oven-drying method** (often called the **gravimetric method**) is the **reference (official) method** adopted by the **Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC)** and is treated as the standard against which all rapid/electronic methods are calibrated. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In Unit Operations in Agricultural Processing, moisture content is the *primary* quality attribute governing every downstream operation — sorting, grading, drying, dehydration, storage, packaging, and shelf-life prediction. Mastering its determination is a **CO1–CO2 mapped outcome** (Apply knowledge of physical properties to food processing). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge in the Sun" Imagine two identical sponges. You soak one in water, then weigh both. - The *soaked* sponge has **water trapped inside its pores**. - If you place it in bright sunlight for hours, water evaporates, and the sponge gets lighter. - The **weight lost = weight of water removed**. - The moisture content is simply: **how much of the original weight was just water?** In fruits and vegetables, this "trapped water" is held in three forms: 1. **Free water** — loosely held in cells (most of it; evaporates easily). 2. **Bound water** — chemically or physically adsorbed to proteins, starches, sugars. 3. **Water of constitution** — chemically bound in molecular structure (not removed by normal drying). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Point:** The standard oven method removes *free water* and *most adsorbed water*, but **NOT water of constitution**. This is why the result is called **"moisture"**, not "total water". ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Boiling point of water at 1 atm | $T_b$ | **100 °C (373.15 K)** | | Atmospheric pressure reference | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | | Standard drying temperature (AOAC) | $T_{oven}$ | **100 – 105 °C** | | Vacuum oven temperature (sugar-rich samples) | $T_{vac}$ | **60 – 70 °C** | | Density of liquid water (4 °C) | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** | | Specific heat of water | $C_{pw}$ | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** | | Latent heat of vaporization (100 °C) | $\lambda_v$ | **2257 kJ/kg** | > [!TIP] > For *sugar-rich fruits* (mango, banana, grape, pineapple) the high sugar content **caramelizes and decomposes** above ~70 °C, giving **falsely high moisture readings** due to mass loss from sugar decomposition, not water loss. Hence **vacuum oven at 60–70 °C** is preferred. --- # 2. THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — METHODS, PRINCIPLES & FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Classification of Moisture Determination Methods Moisture determination methods for fruits and vegetables are broadly classified into two families: ### A. Direct (Absolute) Methods These remove water physically and measure the mass lost. 1. **Oven-drying (Convection / Forced-air)** — AOAC reference method. 2. **Vacuum oven drying** — for heat-sensitive samples. 3. **Infrared (IR) drying** — rapid, surface heating. 4. **Microwave drying** — rapid, volumetric heating. 5. **Desiccation method** — drying over $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$ (used for high-precision work). ### B. Indirect Methods These measure a property *correlated* with moisture content. 1. **Distillation method (Toluene / Dean-Stark)** — for fats/oils-rich foods. 2. **Karl Fischer titration** — chemical, very precise for low-moisture samples. 3. **Electrical resistance / capacitance meters** — rapid field method. 4. **Refractometric method (Brix)** — for juices via soluble solids. 5. **Near-Infrared Reflectance (NIR) spectroscopy** — non-destructive. 6. **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** — research grade. ## 2.2 Working Principle of Each Method ### 2.2.1 Forced-Convection Oven Method (Reference) A pre-weighed wet sample ($W$ g, including container) is placed in a hot-air oven at **100–105 °C** for a stipulated time (typically **3–6 hours**, often overnight) until **constant weight** is achieved. Water evaporates and is carried away by convection currents. The dried residue is re-weighed ($W_d$). Mass difference equals water removed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Constant weight"** = two successive weighings, 1 hour apart, differ by **≤ 0.5 mg (or ≤ 0.1% of sample mass)**. This confirms equilibrium with ambient humidity and complete free-water removal. ### 2.2.2 Vacuum Oven Method Same principle, but under **reduced pressure (≤ 50 mm Hg, ~ 6.67 kPa)** and lower temperature (**60–70 °C**). Lower pressure reduces water's boiling point, so water evaporates without high thermal stress. Prevents sugar browning. Used for honey, syrups, high-sugar fruits. ### 2.2.3 Distillation Method (Toluene) An immiscible organic solvent (toluene, b.p. **110.6 °C**) is co-distilled with the sample. Water + toluene vapors rise, condense, and separate in a **Dean-Stark trap**. Water volume is read directly. ### 2.2.4 Karl Fischer Method Based on the **Bunsen reaction**: $$\text{I}_2 + \text{SO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{HI}$$ The amount of iodine consumed (measured electrometrically) is stoichiometrically equal to water present. Highly accurate (down to **ppm** level), used for spices, dehydrated powders. ### 2.2.5 Electrical Resistance / Capacitance - **Resistance type:** As moisture rises, electrical resistance falls (water conducts). Calibrated for specific product. - **Capacitance type:** Dielectric constant of water (~80) is far higher than dry matter (~2–5). Capacitance rises with moisture. ### 2.2.6 NIR Spectroscopy Water has strong absorption bands at **1940 nm and 1450 nm**. Reflectance or transmittance at these wavelengths correlates with moisture. Non-destructive, suitable for online sorting. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in every KTU exam cycle in either Part A or Part B.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mass of water | $W_w = W - W_d$ | g | Where $W$ = wet sample mass, $W_d$ = dry sample mass (both incl. container) | | 2 | Mass of dry solid | $W_s = W_d - W_c$ | g | $W_c$ = empty container mass | | 3 | Net wet mass | $W_n = W - W_c$ | g | | | 4 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W - W_c} \times 100$ | % (w.b.) | Standard food-industry reporting | | 5 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W_d - W_c} \times 100$ | % (d.b.) | Engineering/drying calculations | | 6 | Conversion w.b. → d.b. | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$ | % | | | 7 | Conversion d.b. → w.b. | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}/100} \times 100$ | % | | | 8 | Total solids | $S = 100 - M_{wb}$ | % | Mass balance closure | > [!WARNING] > **Notation pitfall:** In KTU answer scripts, students often write "$W$" and "$W_d$" without specifying whether container mass is included. Always **define your symbols** at the top of the answer and state the **basis** (wet or dry) clearly. **Marks are awarded for correct basis declaration.** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Drying Equipment Design:** Drying rate, residence time, and energy consumption are computed on **dry basis**. - **Packaging Design:** Shelf-life prediction uses water activity ($a_w$), which is itself a function of moisture content. - **Storage & Logistics:** Moisture dictates microbial spoilage risk — above **$a_w$ = 0.6**, bacterial growth dominates. - **Economic Pricing:** Fruits are traded by weight; accurate moisture determines fair price (especially for dried/dehydrated produce). - **Quality Control:** Grading regulations (FSSAI, BIS, Codex Alimentarius) specify permissible moisture limits for each commodity. --- # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS AND WORKED ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Moisture Content Formula ### 3.1.1 Starting Definitions Let: - $W_c$ = mass of empty, clean, dry container (g) - $W$ = mass of container + wet sample (g) - $W_d$ = mass of container + dried sample, after oven treatment (g) Therefore: $$W_w = \text{mass of water lost} = W - W_d \quad \text{(g)}$$ $$W_s = \text{mass of dry solid (matter)} = W_d - W_c \quad \text{(g)}$$ $$W_{\text{net,wet}} = \text{mass of wet sample only} = W - W_c \quad \text{(g)}$$ ### 3.1.2 Wet-Basis Moisture Content By definition, wet-basis moisture content is the ratio of water mass to the **total wet mass of the sample**, expressed as a percentage: $$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_{\text{net,wet}}} \times 100 = \frac{W - W_d}{W - W_c} \times 100 \;\;(\% \,\text{w.b.})$$ ### 3.1.3 Dry-Basis Moisture Content Dry-basis moisture content is the ratio of water mass to the **dry solid mass only**: $$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_s} \times 100 = \frac{W - W_d}{W_d - W_c} \times 100 \;\;(\% \,\text{d.b.})$$ ### 3.1.4 Algebraic Conversion Between the Two Bases Starting from $M_{db}$: $$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$$ **Derivation:** $$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_s}$$ We know that $W_{\text{net,wet}} = W_w + W_s$, so $W_s = W_{\text{net,wet}} - W_w$. Substitute: $$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_{\text{net,wet}} - W_w}$$ Divide numerator and denominator by $W_{\text{net,wet}}$: $$M_{db} = \frac{W_w / W_{\text{net,wet}}}{1 - W_w / W_{\text{net,wet}}} = \frac{M_{wb}/100}{1 - M_{wb}/100} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by 100 to clean up: $$\boxed{M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}}$$ The reverse conversion follows similarly: $$\boxed{M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}/100} \times 100}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Exam-Style) ### Example 1 — Potato (Basic Wet-Basis Calculation) A **5.00 g** sample of grated potato is taken in a **pre-weighed** moisture dish (mass **= 22.50 g**). After oven drying at **103 °C for 5 hours** and cooling in a desiccator, the dish + dry sample weighs **26.80 g**. Find: **(a)** Moisture content (w.b.), **(b)** Moisture content (d.b.), **(c)** Total solids. **Solution:** Given: - $W_c = 22.50$ g (empty dish) - $W = 22.50 + 5.00 = 27.50$ g (dish + wet) - $W_d = 26.80$ g (dish + dry) Compute water mass: $$W_w = W - W_d = 27.50 - 26.80 = 0.70 \;\text{g}$$ Compute dry solid mass: $$W_s = W_d - W_c = 26.80 - 22.50 = 4.30 \;\text{g}$$ Compute wet sample mass: $$W_{\text{net,wet}} = W - W_c = 27.50 - 22.50 = 5.00 \;\text{g}$$ **(a) Wet-basis moisture content:** $$M_{wb} = \frac{W - W_d}{W - W_c} \times 100 = \frac{0.70}{5.00} \times 100 = \mathbf{14.0 \,\%\, w.b.}$$ **(b) Dry-basis moisture content:** $$M_{db} = \frac{W - W_d}{W_d - W_c} \times 100 = \frac{0.70}{4.30} \times 100 = \mathbf{16.28 \,\%\, d.b.}$$ **(c) Total solids:** $$S = 100 - M_{wb} = 100 - 14.0 = \mathbf{86.0 \,\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > Potato's moisture content is normally **~78–80% w.b.**. Our sample's low value suggests the data is illustrative only; for fresh potato the experiment would yield a much higher number (see Example 3). ### Example 2 — Conversion Problem (Grape) A grape sample has a moisture content of **85.0% (w.b.)**. Convert to **dry basis**. **Solution:** $$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100} = \frac{85.0}{1 - 85.0/100} = \frac{85.0}{0.15} = \mathbf{566.67 \,\%\, d.b.}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice $M_{db} \gg 100$% for high-moisture foods. This is **not an error**. Dry-basis moisture content is a *mass ratio*, expressed in *percent* by convention, and it can exceed 100%. Always state the basis clearly. ### Example 3 — Fresh Tomato (Realistic Values) A fresh tomato slice weighing **10.00 g** (wet) is dried in an oven. After drying, the sample mass is **1.65 g**. **Solution:** Compute water mass: $$W_w = 10.00 - 1.65 = 8.35 \;\text{g}$$ Compute dry solid: $$W_s = 1.65 \;\text{g}$$ Wet-basis: $$M_{wb} = \frac{8.35}{10.00} \times 100 = \mathbf{83.5 \,\%\, w.b.}$$ Dry-basis: $$M_{db} = \frac{8.35}{1.65} \times 100 = \mathbf{506.06 \,\%\, d.b.}$$ This matches the **typical 93–95% w.b.** range for fresh tomato *if* we ignore that real tomato also has volatile acids lost during drying. Real lab value of fresh tomato is around **93–95% w.b.** ### Example 4 — Constant-Weight Confirmation A banana sample is dried and weighed every hour: | Time (h) | Mass (g) | |---|---| | 0 | 25.000 | | 1 | 22.450 | | 2 | 22.130 | | 3 | 22.005 | | 4 | 21.998 | | 5 | 21.996 | Constant weight is achieved when consecutive readings differ by **≤ 0.5 mg = 0.0005 g**. Between hour 4 and 5: $\vert 21.998 - 21.996 \vert = 0.002$ g = **2 mg**. Wait — this *exceeds* 0.5 mg. So we continue. Recompute (revised threshold using 0.1% rule): $0.1\%$ of sample mass $\approx 0.025$ g = 25 mg. The 2 mg change is well within tolerance — hence **constant weight is achieved at hour 4–5**. > [!TIP] > KTU answer: state the **criterion used** (either 0.5 mg absolute or 0.1% relative), then declare constant weight. --- # 4. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Part A** answers must be concise (50–80 words), with clearly stated formula and final numerical result if applicable. No long derivations. ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define moisture content of a food material. State two expressions for it and explain the difference between wet-basis and dry-basis representation.** **Model Answer:** > Moisture content is the amount of water present in a food material, expressed as a percentage. > > **Wet basis:** $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W - W_c} \times 100$ (% of total wet mass). > > **Dry basis:** $M_{db} = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W_d - W_c} \times 100$ (% of dry solid mass). > > Food industry typically reports **wet basis**, while engineering calculations (drying, energy balance) use **dry basis** because dry solid mass stays invariant during water removal. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **Why is vacuum oven preferred over conventional oven for moisture determination in sugar-rich fruits like mango and grape?** **Model Answer:** > Sugar-rich fruits contain **fructose, sucrose, and glucose** which **decompose/caramelize** at temperatures above ~70 °C. In a conventional oven at 103 °C, sugar decomposition causes **additional mass loss not attributable to water**, leading to **falsely high moisture readings**. > > A vacuum oven operates at **reduced pressure (≤ 50 mm Hg)** and **lower temperature (60–70 °C)**, lowering water's boiling point so evaporation occurs **without thermal decomposition of sugars**, giving accurate moisture values. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` #### **Question B-A:** **(a)** Explain the **principle, apparatus, and procedure** for the determination of moisture content of fruits and vegetables by the **oven-drying method**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a moisture determination test on fresh carrot, an empty moisture dish weighs **38.25 g**. The dish + grated carrot sample weighs **48.75 g**. After drying at **103 °C for 5 hours** and cooling in a desiccator, the dish + dried sample weighs **39.55 g**. Calculate: **(i)** Moisture content (w.b.), **(ii)** Moisture content (d.b.), **(iii)** Total solids content. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Principle (2 marks):** When a wet food sample is heated in a hot-air oven at 100–105 °C, water evaporates and is swept away by convection currents. The mass loss of the sample corresponds to the amount of water removed. Moisture content is the ratio of water mass to original sample mass, expressed as a percentage. **Apparatus (2 marks):** 1. Forced-convection hot-air oven (thermostatically controlled, 100–105 °C). 2. Aluminum or stainless-steel moisture dishes with tight-fitting lids. 3. Analytical balance (readability 0.0001 g = 0.1 mg). 4. Desiccator with silica gel / CaCl₂ desiccant. 5. Glass rod / spatula for sample handling. **Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Clean and dry the moisture dish. Place in oven for 30 min, cool in desiccator, and weigh ($W_c$). 2. Accurately weigh 5–10 g of well-mixed fresh sample into the dish ($W$). 3. Place dish (lid removed) in oven at **103 °C for 3–6 hours**. 4. Remove, cover with lid, cool in desiccator for **30 min**, weigh ($W_d$). 5. Return to oven for **1 hour**, cool, reweigh. Repeat until **constant weight** (difference ≤ 0.5 mg). 6. Compute moisture using: $$M_{wb} = \frac{W - W_d}{W - W_c} \times 100 \;\;\;\%$$ > [!TIP] > Mention **"constant weight criterion"** in procedure — KTU examiner explicitly awards marks for this phrase. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $W_c = 38.25$ g - $W = 48.75$ g - $W_d = 39.55$ g **Step 1 — Water mass:** $$W_w = W - W_d = 48.75 - 39.55 = 9.20 \;\text{g}$$ **Step 2 — Dry solid mass:** $$W_s = W_d - W_c = 39.55 - 38.25 = 1.30 \;\text{g}$$ **Step 3 — Wet sample mass:** $$W_{\text{net,wet}} = W - W_c = 48.75 - 38.25 = 10.50 \;\text{g}$$ **(i) Moisture content (w.b.):** $$M_{wb} = \frac{W - W_d}{W - W_c} \times 100 = \frac{9.20}{10.50} \times 100 = \mathbf{87.62 \,\%\, w.b.}$$ **(ii) Moisture content (d.b.):** $$M_{db} = \frac{W - W_d}{W_d - W_c} \times 100 = \frac{9.20}{1.30} \times 100 = \mathbf{707.69 \,\%\, d.b.}$$ **(iii) Total solids:** $$S = 100 - M_{wb} = 100 - 87.62 = \mathbf{12.38 \,\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > The wet-basis value **~87.6%** is consistent with published fresh-carrot moisture (~86–88%). The dry-basis value of **707.7%** is correctly greater than 100% (see Section 2.3 warning). --- ### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` #### **Question B-B:** **(a)** Discuss the **various methods** used for moisture determination in food materials, highlighting the principle and suitability of each for fruits and vegetables. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A grape pulp sample has moisture content of **82% (w.b.)**. If **100 kg** of this pulp is dried to a final moisture content of **18% (w.b.)**, calculate: **(i)** Mass of water removed, **(ii)** Mass of dry solid present, **(iii)** Dry-basis moisture of the final product. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Tabulated Answer (Examiner's preferred format):** | Method | Principle | Suitability for F&V | |---|---|---| | **Oven drying** | Gravimetric — mass loss = water | Universal; AOAC reference | | **Vacuum oven** | Same, but at reduced pressure & lower temp | High-sugar fruits (mango, grape) | | **IR drying** | Radiant heating from IR lamp | Rapid QC; surface only | | **Microwave** | Volumetric heating by dipole rotation | Rapid; risk of charring | | **Distillation (toluene)** | Co-distill water + immiscible solvent | Fats-rich foods (coconut, avocado) | | **Karl Fischer** | Iodine consumed by water (Bunsen reaction) | Spices, low-moisture powders | | **Resistance meter** | Conductivity rises with water | Field/warehouse rapid checks | | **NIR** | Absorbance at 1450 / 1940 nm | Online sorting, non-destructive | **Conclusion (1 mark):** For routine KTU lab work, **oven drying is the standard**; **vacuum oven for sugar-rich fruits**; **Karl Fischer for spices**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - Initial: $M_{wb,1} = 82\%$, mass $W_1 = 100$ kg - Final: $M_{wb,2} = 18\%$ **Step 1 — Dry solid mass (constant throughout drying):** From initial state: $$W_s = W_1 \times \frac{100 - M_{wb,1}}{100} = 100 \times \frac{100 - 82}{100} = 100 \times 0.18 = 18 \;\text{kg}$$ **Step 2 — Final total mass $W_2$:** From final state: $$W_s = W_2 \times \frac{100 - M_{wb,2}}{100}$$ $$18 = W_2 \times \frac{100 - 18}{100} = W_2 \times 0.82$$ $$W_2 = \frac{18}{0.82} = 21.95 \;\text{kg}$$ **Step 3 — Water removed:** $$\Delta W_w = W_1 - W_2 = 100 - 21.95 = \mathbf{78.05 \;\text{kg}}$$ **Step 4 — Verify using initial water:** $$W_{w,1} = 100 \times 0.82 = 82 \;\text{kg}$$ $$W_{w,2} = 21.95 \times 0.18 = 3.95 \;\text{kg}$$ $$\Delta W_w = 82 - 3.95 = 78.05 \;\text{kg} \;\;\checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Final dry-basis moisture:** $$M_{db,2} = \frac{M_{wb,2}}{1 - M_{wb,2}/100} = \frac{18}{1 - 0.18} = \frac{18}{0.82} = \mathbf{21.95 \,\%\, d.b.}$$ > [!TIP] > Note that for *low-moisture* products (≤ 25% w.b.), the wet-basis and dry-basis values are **close numerically**. For *high-moisture* products (≥ 50% w.b.), they **diverge sharply** (e.g., 82% w.b. ↔ 455.6% d.b.). --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 reasons students LOSE marks in this experiment:** > > 1. **Forgetting to declare the basis** — always write "wet basis" or "dry basis" beside every numerical answer. **−1 to −2 marks if omitted.** > 2. **Using wrong denominator** — confusing $W - W_c$ with $W_d - W_c$. Wet-basis uses wet mass in denominator; dry-basis uses dry solid mass. > 3. **Not stating constant-weight criterion** — KTU awards 1 mark for the exact phrase *"until constant weight"* in procedure. > 4. **Writing dry-basis > 100% as "error"** — it is **mathematically correct** for high-moisture foods. Do not "correct" it. > 5. **Missing the cooling-in-desiccator step** — without desiccator, hot sample re-absorbs atmospheric moisture in seconds, giving **erroneous results**. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - [x] **Definition:** Moisture content = water mass expressed as % of wet or dry sample mass. - [x] **Wet-basis formula:** $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W - W_c} \times 100$ - [x] **Dry-basis formula:** $M_{db} = \dfrac{W - W_d}{W_d - W_c} \times 100$ - [x] **Conversion:** $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$ - [x] **AOAC standard oven conditions:** 100–105 °C, 3–6 h, until constant weight. - [x] **Vacuum oven:** 60–70 °C, ≤ 50 mm Hg — for sugar-rich fruits. - [x] **Constant weight criterion:** successive weighings differ by ≤ 0.5 mg or ≤ 0.1% of sample mass. - [x] **Cooling step:** always cool in desiccator (30 min) before weighing. - [x] **Sample size:** 5–10 g typical for fresh produce; smaller for dried products. - [x] **Total solids:** $S = 100 - M_{wb}$ (mass balance closure). - [x] **Methods:** Oven, Vacuum oven, IR, Microwave, Karl Fischer, Distillation, Resistance, Capacitance, NIR. - [x] **Typical moisture ranges:** Fresh fruits/veg = **70–95% w.b.**; dehydrated products = **2–10% w.b.** - [x] **Engineering utility:** drying design, packaging, shelf-life, quality grading, pricing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a moisture problem appears in the question paper, **always (1) define all symbols, (2) state the basis (w.b. or d.b.), (3) show step-by-step substitution with units, and (4) box the final numerical answer.** This single habit can lift your score by **2–3 marks** without changing the depth of your knowledge.
